topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Strategies/ Systems/ Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Maintenance Strategy?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Maintenance Strategy** is a systematic, top-level management plan that defines *when*, *how*, *why*, and *by whom* maintenance activities will be carried out on physical assets to optimize the trade-off between equipment reliability, operational availability, and life-cycle cost (LCC).

In simpler words, it is the **"Game Plan"** that an industry adopts to decide whether to wait for a machine to fail before fixing it, or to service it before it fails.

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — Your Own Body

Think of how you take care of your health. You have three possible approaches:

| Your Health Action | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Wait until you fall sick, then visit the doctor | **Breakdown (Reactive) Maintenance** |
| Brush teeth daily, exercise regularly, eat well | **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance** |
| Annual blood test / MRI scan to detect early signs | **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance** |

Just as a person who *only* goes to the doctor when sick ends up in the ICU, an industry that relies only on breakdown maintenance ends up with catastrophic losses. The choice of strategy defines the very **culture** of the plant.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot — The Big Picture

Maintenance is broadly classified into **two macro-divisions**, which then branch into strategies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Maintenance**
> 1. **Preventive (Proactive) Maintenance** — Stop failure *before* it happens.
> 2. **Corrective (Reactive) Maintenance** — Fix failure *after* it happens.
>
> All the strategies you study (TPM, RCM, CBM, PdM, BM, etc.) are simply refined *operational models* built on these two pillars.

The major types covered in **KTU Module 2** are:

- Breakdown Maintenance (BM)
- Preventive Maintenance (PM)
- Corrective Maintenance (CM)
- Predictive Maintenance (PdM)
- Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
- Planned Maintenance
- Opportunity Maintenance
- Design-Out Maintenance
- Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
- Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)
- Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Type-by-Type Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Breakdown Maintenance (BM) — "Run-to-Failure"
Also called **Reactive** or **Corrective-in-emergency** maintenance. The equipment is allowed to operate until it fails; repair is then carried out.

- **Why used:** Zero upfront planning cost; used for non-critical, cheap, easily-replaceable items (e.g., fuses, bulbs, gaskets).
- **Drawback:** Unplanned downtime, collateral damage, safety hazards, high spare-parts inventory in the long run.
- **KTU Tag:** This is the *baseline* against which all other strategies are economically justified.

### 2.1.2 Preventive Maintenance (PM) — "Service Before Failure"
A scheduled, time-based (or usage-based) intervention performed to reduce the probability of failure. Triggered by **calendar time, operating hours, or cycles**, not by machine condition.

- **Sub-forms:**
  - *Scheduled PM* (e.g., lube oil change every 500 hours)
  - *Planned PM* (PM that has documented procedures, spares, and resources)
- **Logic:** Replace components while they are still in the *useful life region* of the bathtub curve.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bathtub Curve" Connection (Background Concept)**
> PM targets the *wear-out* region (Zone 3) of the bathtub curve and reduces the impact of *random failures* (Zone 2) by periodic restoration. Zone 1 (infant mortality) is handled by Design-Out / Burn-in.

### 2.1.3 Corrective Maintenance (CM) — "Fix What is Broken"
Any maintenance performed **after a defect has been identified**, with the objective of restoring the asset to an operable state.

- *Planned CM* — defect detected, repair scheduled at the next available window.
- *Unplanned (Emergency) CM* — breakdown; immediate action required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PM vs CM Distinction (Favourite KTU Question!)**
> - **PM** is a *pre-failure* activity scheduled by time/usage.
> - **CM** is a *post-failure* activity triggered by observation of degradation.
> Every PM action is *preventive*; every CM action is *corrective*. PM *prevents* the need for CM.

### 2.1.4 Predictive Maintenance (PdM) — "Listen to the Machine"
A condition-driven strategy using **real-time or periodic monitoring** of physical parameters (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, oil debris, ultrasonics) to predict failure *before* it occurs.

- **Tools used:** Vibration analysers, infrared thermography, oil analysis, motor current signature analysis.
- **Advantage:** Maximum component utilization, minimum unplanned downtime.

### 2.1.5 Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
Closely related to PdM. CBM uses **measured condition data** to decide whether maintenance is needed. PdM is essentially the *prediction engine* inside CBM.

### 2.1.6 Opportunity Maintenance
Maintenance performed on an item *whenever it is already down for some other reason* (e.g., servicing a pump while its parent machine is being overhauled).

### 2.1.7 Design-Out Maintenance
Eliminating the need for maintenance by **redesigning the asset** to be more reliable or easier to service (e.g., using sealed-for-life bearings, modular LRU design).

### 2.1.8 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
A **company-wide, employee-involved** maintenance philosophy originating from Japan (Nakajima, 1971). Eight pillars including autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, quality maintenance, and office TPM. Target metric: **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) ≥ 85 %**.

### 2.1.9 Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)
A systematic engineering framework (developed by Nowlan & Heap for United Airlines, 1978) to determine the *most cost-effective* maintenance requirement of a physical asset based on its operating context, failure modes, and consequences.

### 2.1.10 Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)
A software backbone for planning, scheduling, tracking, and recording all maintenance activities. It is *not a strategy*, but an *enabler* of any strategy.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}$ | Average uptime between two consecutive failures |
| 2 | Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}$ | Average time to restore after failure |
| 3 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF$ (for non-repairable) | Total life / sample size |
| 4 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Uptime capability assuming perfect support |
| 5 | Achieved Availability | $A_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MWT}$ | Includes waiting for spares, admin delays |
| 6 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | Real-world, all-inclusive |
| 7 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of surviving to time $t$ |
| 8 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Constant in useful life region |
| 9 | Hazard rate link | $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(x)\, dx}$ | Generalised non-constant $\lambda$ |
| 10 | OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality |
| 11 | Cost of Downtime | $C_D = P_l \times T_d \times C_{unit}$ | Lost production $P_l$, duration $T_d$ |
| 12 | Cost Ratio Test | $\dfrac{C_{PM}}{C_{CM}} < \dfrac{1}{P_f}$ | PM justified if cost ratio is small |

> Where:
> - $T_{up}$ = total up time, $T_{down}$ = total down time, $N_f$ = number of failures
> - $MWT$ = Mean Waiting Time (administrative/logistic delay)
> - $P_f$ = probability of failure between PM intervals
> - $\lambda$ = constant failure rate

## 2.3 Why This Matters in the Real World

- **Aerospace:** RCM is mandatory for FAA-certified fleets (MSG-3 logic).
- **Manufacturing:** TPM-driven factories (Toyota, Tata Steel) routinely exceed 90 % OEE.
- **Power Plants:** CBM on turbines has reduced forced outages by **40–60 %**.
- **Cost Logic:** *Industry data shows that every ₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹4–₹10 on breakdown.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Availability from First Principles

We define availability as the long-run fraction of time that the asset is in an operable state.

**Step 1 — Define a single renewal cycle.**
A renewal cycle begins when a repair is completed and ends at the next failure.

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{up} + T_{down} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 2 — Define availability as the uptime fraction of that cycle.**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime in cycle}}{\text{Total cycle length}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the mean values.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the support delay term $MWT$.**
When logistics, spares procurement, and waiting for technicians are included, the effective downtime becomes $MTTR + MWT$, giving:

$$
A_a = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MWT}
$$

**Step 5 — Operational form.**
If we use the actual recorded calendar window $T$ instead of means:

$$
A_o = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_f} MTBF_i}{T_{calendar}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Reliability Function $R(t)$

**Step 1 — For a non-repairable item with constant failure rate $\lambda$:**

The probability of surviving a small interval $dt$ is $(1 - \lambda\, dt)$.

**Step 2 — Divide time $t$ into $n$ small slices.** Survival through all of them is:

$$
R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \lambda \frac{t}{n}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the standard exponential limit** $\displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{x}$:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Link to MTBF.**
For an exponential distribution, the mean is the reciprocal of the rate:

$$
MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R(t) = e^{-t / MTBF}
$$

## 3.3 Cost-Optimisation Analysis for PM vs CM

Let:
- $C_{PM}$ = cost of one preventive intervention
- $C_{CM}$ = cost of one corrective (breakdown) intervention
- $P_f$ = probability of failure between two PM intervals

Total expected cost per cycle if PM is done:

$$
C_{PM}^{total} = C_{PM} + P_f \cdot C_{CM}
$$

Total expected cost if no PM (pure CM):

$$
C_{CM}^{total} = C_{CM}
$$

**PM is economically justified when:**

$$
C_{PM}^{total} < C_{CM}^{total}
$$

$$
C_{PM} + P_f \cdot C_{CM} < C_{CM}
$$

$$
C_{PM} < C_{CM} (1 - P_f)
$$

$$
\frac{C_{PM}}{C_{CM}} < 1 - P_f
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:**
> A PM strategy pays off when the *ratio* $\dfrac{C_{PM}}{C_{CM}}$ is small **AND** the *failure probability* $P_f$ is large.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Strategy

| Asset Profile / Context | Recommended Strategy | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Non-critical, low-cost item (fuse, bulb) | Breakdown Maintenance | Cheaper to replace than prevent |
| Critical safety item (aircraft brake) | RCM / Design-Out | Failure cannot be tolerated |
| High OEE-demanding line (auto plant) | TPM | Operator involvement maximises uptime |
| Rotating machinery with vibration data | CBM / PdM | Rich condition data available |
| Long lead-time spare items | Planned / Scheduled PM | Avoid production stoppage |
| Equipment already down for other work | Opportunity Maintenance | No extra shutdown cost |
| Repeated chronic failure | Design-Out Maintenance | Eliminate root cause |
| Complex multi-system asset | RCM + CMMS | Need formal logic and data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Maintenance Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maintenance Management] --> B[Preventive Approach]
    A --> C[Corrective Approach]
    A --> D[Modern Frameworks]

    B --> B1[Scheduled PM]
    B --> B2[Planned PM]
    B --> B3[Condition-Based CBM]
    B --> B4[Predictive PdM]
    B --> B5[Design-Out]

    C --> C1[Breakdown / Reactive]
    C --> C2[Corrective Maintenance]
    C --> C3[Opportunity Maintenance]

    D --> D1[TPM Total Productive Maintenance]
    D --> D2[RCM Reliability Centered Maintenance]
    D --> D3[CMMS Computerized Management]

    B1 --> E[Goal: Zero Failure]
    B3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    C1 --> F[Acceptance of Failure]
    C2 --> F
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RCM Decision Logic (simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Function of Asset] --> B1[Identify Functional Failures]
    B1 --> C1[Failure Modes FMEA]
    C1 --> D1{Effects on Operations}
    D1 --> D2[Safety / Environmental Consequence]
    D1 --> D3[Operational Consequence]
    D1 --> D4[Economic Consequence]
    D2 --> E1[Safety PM Task]
    D2 --> E2[Design Change]
    D3 --> E3[Scheduled PM Task]
    D4 --> E4[Condition Based Task]
    D4 --> E5[No Scheduled Maintenance]
```

## 4.3 Maintenance Strategy Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Identify Asset] --> S1{Failure Consequence}
    S1 --> S2[Safety Risk]
    S1 --> S3[Production Loss]
    S1 --> S4[Minor Cost]
    S2 --> S5[Apply RCM Mandatory PM or Design Out]
    S3 --> S6{Condition Data Available}
    S6 --> S7[Yes Use CBM or PdM]
    S6 --> S8[No Use Scheduled PM]
    S4 --> S9[Use Breakdown or Opportunity Maintenance]
    S5 --> S10[Document in CMMS]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between PM, CM, TPM and RCM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[Preventive Maintenance] -->|input layer| TPM
    CM[Corrective Maintenance] -->|failure data input| TPM
    RCM[RCM] -->|policy generator| TPM
    TPM[TPM] -->|execution layer| OEE[OEE Improvement]
    CMMS[CMMS] -->|data backbone| PM
    CMMS -->|data backbone| CM
    CMMS -->|data backbone| TPM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** RCM decides *what* to do, TPM decides *how to involve everyone*, CMMS records *everything*, and PM/CM are the day-to-day *actions* on the shop floor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Corrective Maintenance. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Corrective Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Time or usage-based | Actual failure or observed defect |
| Timing | **Before** failure | **After** failure |
| Objective | Reduce probability of failure | Restore function to operable state |
| Cost profile | Predictable, distributed | Unpredictable, often high |
| Examples | Lube oil change every 500 h | Bearing replacement after seizure |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table.)

### Q2. What is Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)? State its key features. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
RCM is a systematic engineering framework used to determine the *most cost-effective* maintenance requirement of a physical asset, considering its operating context, failure modes, and failure consequences.

**Key Features (1.5 marks):**
1. Preserves system function rather than just component reliability.
2. Recognises four consequence categories — *safety, environmental, operational, economic*.
3. Answers seven standard questions (the Nowlan–Heap RCM decision logic).
4. Prioritises **proactive tasks** (PM, CBM) but allows *run-to-failure* when economically justified.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of preventive maintenance strategies with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Scheduled (Time-Based) PM — 2 marks**
   - Activities triggered by calendar time or operating hours.
   - *Example:* Centrifugal pump bearing greasing every 2000 hours; transformer oil filtration every 12 months.
   - Triggered irrespective of actual condition.

2. **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM) — 2 marks**
   - Maintenance triggered by the actual measured condition.
   - *Example:* Vibration spectrum analysis on a motor; thermography on switchgear contacts.
   - Uses instrumentation (vibration analyser, IR camera, oil sampler).

3. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM) — 1.5 marks**
   - Forecasts *future* failure using trend analysis, AI/ML, or statistical models.
   - *Example:* Bearing temperature trending over weeks to predict seizure date.

4. **Design-Out Maintenance — 1.5 marks**
   - Permanently redesigns components to eliminate failure modes.
   - *Example:* Replacing greased bearings with sealed-for-life bearings; changing from chain to belt drive to remove lubrication.

**(b)** A centrifugal pump has MTBF = 800 hours and MTTR = 12 hours. The plant runs 24 hours per day. Compute **(i)** Inherent Availability, **(ii)** Expected Downtime per month (30 days), and **(iii)** Comment on whether a predictive strategy is justified. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inherent Availability — 2 marks**

$$
A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{800}{800 + 12} = \frac{800}{812} = 0.9852 \;(\text{or } 98.52\%)
$$

**(ii) Expected Downtime per month — 3 marks**

Total calendar hours in 30 days:

$$
H = 24 \times 30 = 720 \text{ h}
$$

Expected uptime:

$$
U = A_i \times H = 0.9852 \times 720 = 709.34 \text{ h}
$$

Expected downtime:

$$
D = H - U = 720 - 709.34 = 10.66 \text{ h per month}
$$

Alternatively using $D = H \times (1 - A_i) = 720 \times 0.0148 = 10.66 \text{ h}$.

**(iii) Justification for PdM — 2 marks**

If each hour of downtime costs the plant ₹50,000, the monthly exposure is:

$$
C_D = 10.66 \times 50{,}000 = ₹5{,}33{,}000
$$

A CBM/PdM system costing ₹2,00,000 per month that can halve this downtime pays for itself. **Hence predictive maintenance is justified.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the pillars, objectives, and key performance indicators of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Origin & Definition — 1.5 marks**
   - Originated at Nippondenso (Toyota Group) in 1971 under Seiichi Nakajima.
   - TPM is a company-wide, employee-involvement strategy to maximise equipment effectiveness across the entire life of the asset.

2. **Eight Pillars (Any 4 needed for 2.5 marks):**
   1. Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen) — operators own daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection.
   2. Planned Maintenance — schedule-based PM with skill upgrade.
   3. Quality Maintenance — zero-defect conditions.
   4. Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen) — small-group activities.
   5. Early Equipment Management — design for maintainability.
   6. Training & Education — multi-skill development.
   7. Safety, Health, Environment.
   8. Office TPM — admin support functions.

3. **Objectives — 1.5 marks:**
   - Zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents.
   - Achieve OEE ≥ 85 %.
   - Build a culture of ownership among operators.

4. **Key Performance Indicators — 1.5 marks:**
   - **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality.
   - MTBF, MTTR trends.
   - Number of autonomous inspections completed.
   - Mean Time Between Stoppages (MTBS).
   - Number of Kaizen suggestions per employee per month.

**(b)** List and briefly explain the **seven basic questions of RCM** as formulated by Nowlan and Heap. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (1 mark per correct question + logical explanation):**

1. What are the functions and associated performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?
2. In what ways does it fail to fulfil its functions (functional failures)?
3. What causes each functional failure (failure modes)?
4. What happens when each failure occurs (failure effects)?
5. In what way does each failure matter (failure consequences — safety, environment, operational, economic)?
6. What can be done to predict or prevent each failure (proactive tasks and task intervals)?
7. What should be done if a suitable proactive task cannot be found (default actions — design change, run-to-failure)?

(Allocate 1 mark per correct question statement and 1 mark collectively for the logic of linking them into a decision process.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often *list* the seven questions but fail to show how they form a **decision logic** (failure → consequence → task choice). Marks are lost for missing the **proactive vs default action** distinction in Q7.
> - In numerical problems, **forgetting units** (h, days, months) and **mixing MTBF/MTTR** in the wrong denominator costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Maintenance = all actions** to keep equipment in working order or restore it.
- [x] **Two pillars:** Preventive (before) and Corrective (after).
- [x] **BM** is acceptable only for *non-critical, low-cost, redundant* items.
- [x] **PM** is *time/usage-based*; **CBM** is *condition-based*; **PdM** is *trend-forecast-based*.
- [x] **Opportunity Maintenance** exploits already-down windows.
- [x] **Design-Out Maintenance** removes failure causes at source.
- [x] **TPM** is people-centric (8 pillars); key metric = **OEE**.
- [x] **RCM** is engineering-centric (7 questions); outcome = optimised maintenance plan.
- [x] **CMMS** is the IT backbone — not a strategy by itself.
- [x] **Availability formula** $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ must be memorised verbatim.
- [x] **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-t / MTBF}$ — applies in the useful-life region only.
- [x] **PM justification:** $\dfrac{C_{PM}}{C_{CM}} < 1 - P_f$ is the test.
- [x] **Industry rule of thumb:** Preventive cost is roughly **1/4 to 1/10** of breakdown cost.
- [x] Always state **units** when writing numerical answers.
- [x] Mention **safety, environment, and economic** consequences — examiners love this in RCM answers.
- [x] Use the **bathtub curve** to justify PM (targets wear-out zone).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications, Basis/Factors Considered for Selection, Breakdown Maintenance, Corrective Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classifications, Basis/Factors Considered for Selection, Breakdown Maintenance, Corrective Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance** is the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions during the life cycle of an item intended to retain it in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform the required function. (Adapted from **EN 13306:2017**, the KTU-referenced standard for terminology.)

**Maintenance Classifications (KTU 2024 Scheme Taxonomy):**
Maintenance is broadly classified into the following hierarchical categories as prescribed in Module 2 of OEIET613:

| Sl. No. | Primary Class | Secondary Class | Tertiary Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | – | Breakdown Maintenance (Run-to-Failure) |
| 2 | **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Scheduled PM | Routine, Periodic, Overhaul |
| 3 | | Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM) | Predictive, Risk-Based |
| 4 | **Improvement / Modification** | Design-out Maintenance | – |
| 5 | **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | – | – |

**Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** is a sub-type of Corrective Maintenance that is performed **only after a functional failure has occurred**, with no prior attempt to prevent the failure.

**Corrective Maintenance (CM)** is performed to **identify, isolate, and rectify a fault** so that the failed equipment, machine, or system can be restored to an acceptable operational condition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Human Healthcare vs. Machine Maintenance**

Think of a machine like a **human body**:
- **Breakdown Maintenance** → Like calling a doctor **only after a heart attack**. You do nothing until something fails catastrophically, then rush to the emergency room.
- **Corrective Maintenance (Planned)** → Like scheduling a surgery once a tumour is detected through a test. You know something is wrong, you plan the fix, and you execute it at a convenient time.
- **Preventive Maintenance** → Like a routine annual health check-up and daily brushing of teeth. You act *before* sickness occurs.

The **key distinction** between Breakdown Maintenance and Planned Corrective Maintenance is **planning horizon**:
- BDM = **unplanned, reactive, urgent**.
- CM (in its planned form) = **planned, scheduled, controlled**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

In maintenance engineering, the foundational metrics are:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — Average running time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — Average time required to restore a failed system.
- **MDT (Mean Down Time)** — Total downtime including logistics, administrative, and active repair time.
- **λ (Failure Rate)** — Number of failures per unit time.
- **Availability (A)** — The probability that a system is operational at a random point in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard boundary assumption in KTU problems is the **constant failure rate regime** of the *Bathtub Curve* during the useful life period, where the **exponential reliability model** R(t) = e^(−λt) applies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Maintenance Classifications (Hierarchical Decomposition)

Maintenance classifications are not random; they are **organized by decision logic**—essentially *what triggers the maintenance action*:

- **Trigger-Based Hierarchy:**
  - **Failure-Triggered** → Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)
  - **Fault-Triggered (Known Defect)** → Corrective Maintenance (CM)
  - **Time-Triggered** → Scheduled Preventive Maintenance
  - **Condition-Triggered** → Condition-Based / Predictive Maintenance

- **Planning-Based Hierarchy:**
  - **Unplanned Maintenance** → BDM (Emergency CM)
  - **Planned Maintenance** → Scheduled CM, PM, CBM, PdM

- **Outcome-Based Hierarchy:**
  - **Restorative** → CM, BDM (returns item to working state)
  - **Preservative** → PM (keeps item in working state)

## 2.2 Basis / Factors Considered for Selection of Maintenance Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 explicitly lists the following **decision factors** for selecting the appropriate maintenance type. Examiners expect these as standard 3-mark or 7-mark answers.

The selection of a maintenance strategy is governed by **eight core factors**:

1. **Cost of Maintenance vs. Cost of Downtime** — If downtime cost >> maintenance cost, choose preventive. If the inverse, breakdown may be optimal.
2. **Criticality of Equipment** — Critical assets (e.g., aircraft engines, power plant turbines) demand CBM/PdM; non-critical assets (e.g., office fans) can tolerate BDM.
3. **Failure Pattern / Bathhtub Curve Position** — If in wear-out phase, schedule PM. If in infant-mortality phase, use BDM until burn-in is complete.
4. **Reliability & Safety Requirements** — Higher safety demands drive predictive/condition-based monitoring.
5. **Equipment Complexity & Diagnosability** — Complex systems justify CBM; simple systems may not justify the sensor cost.
6. **Availability of Spares & Maintenance Resources** — BDM is feasible only if spares and technicians are immediately available.
7. **Production Schedule & Operational Continuity** — Continuous process plants (refineries, 24×7 manufacturing) cannot afford BDM; batch-process plants may tolerate it.
8. **Age of Equipment & Depreciation** — Old, fully-depreciated equipment with high failure rates often justifies *replacement* over BDM.

## 2.3 Theoretical Framework: Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Exact Formulation):** Breakdown Maintenance is corrective maintenance performed *after* a functional failure has been detected, *without* any prior attempt to predict or prevent that failure. It is a **run-to-failure (RTF)** strategy.

**Why BDM is chosen despite its drawbacks:**
- **Low initial investment** — No sensors, no planned downtime, no PM scheduling software.
- **Suitable for non-critical, redundant, or low-cost items** — e.g., light bulbs, non-critical pumps in standby.
- **Maximum utilization of useful life** — Equipment is used until actual failure, no premature part replacement.
- **Simple to administer** — Requires minimal planning infrastructure.

**Disadvantages (often asked in KTU exams):**
- **Unplanned downtime** — Production halts with no warning, leading to missed delivery schedules.
- **Higher secondary damage** — A failed bearing left running may damage the shaft, gearbox, and motor.
- **Higher repair cost** — Emergency repairs cost 3–5× more than planned repairs.
- **Safety hazards** — Catastrophic failures (e.g., boiler explosion, crane collapse) risk human life.
- **Higher inventory of emergency spares** — Cannot plan spares, so buffer stock must be high.

## 2.4 Theoretical Framework: Corrective Maintenance (CM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Corrective Maintenance is the set of maintenance actions performed **after a fault has been identified**, with the explicit intent of **restoring the item to a state where it can perform its required function**. It includes both **planned (deferred)** and **unplanned (immediate)** corrective actions.

**Two Sub-Categories of CM:**

- **Deferred (Planned) CM** — Fault detected, but repair scheduled for the next planned shutdown. Minimizes production loss.
- **Immediate (Unplanned) CM** — Fault is critical or safety-related; repair executed on emergency basis (effectively a synonym for BDM in some texts).

**The CM Process (5-Stage Model):**

1. **Fault Detection** — Operator observation, alarm, or condition monitoring system.
2. **Fault Diagnosis** — Root cause analysis using tools like FMEA, fault tree, vibration analysis.
3. **Decision to Repair/Replace** — Economic comparison of repair cost vs. replacement cost vs. lost production.
4. **Execution of Repair** — Replace failed component, restore alignment, recommission.
5. **Verification & Documentation** — Performance test, log failure data, update CMMS.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R(t)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (constant failure rate regime) | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **λ** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate | failures per hour |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum \text{Repair Times}}{\text{No. of Repairs}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **MDT** | $MDT = MTTR + MLDT$ | Mean Down Time (MTTR + Mean Logistic Delay) | hours |
| **MLDT** | $MLDT = \dfrac{\text{Total Logistic Delay Time}}{\text{No. of Failures}}$ | Mean Logistic Delay Time | hours |
| **A_i** | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Inherent Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **A_a** | $A_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MDT}$ | Achieved Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **A_o** | $A_o = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | Operational Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **C_BDM** | $C_{BDM} = C_{failure} \cdot n_f$ | Total BDM cost (cost per failure × number of failures) | currency |
| **C_PM** | $C_{PM} = C_{scheduled} \cdot n_p$ | Total PM cost | currency |
| **C_total** | $C_{total} = C_{PM} + C_{down} \cdot D_{PM} + C_{BDM} \cdot n_f$ | Total maintenance cost model | currency |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Industry:** Aircraft engines use **CM + PdM hybrid** — BDM is prohibited for safety-critical systems; engines are pulled out at condition-based triggers.
- **Power Generation:** Steam turbines in thermal plants use **scheduled CM during planned outages** (overhauls every 3–5 years).
- **Manufacturing Assembly Lines:** Critical stations use **CBM with vibration sensors**; non-critical conveyors use **BDM with spare inventory**.
- **IT Infrastructure:** Servers use **redundancy + auto-failover** (essentially making BDM invisible to the end-user).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inherent Availability from First Principles

**Starting Concept:**
Availability is the probability that a system is **operational at a random instant of time**. In a long observation period $T$, the system cycles between **uptime** and **downtime**.

**Step 1 — Define the observation period:**

$$
T = U_{total} + D_{total}
$$

where $U_{total}$ = total uptime and $D_{total}$ = total downtime.

**Step 2 — Define operational availability:**

$$
A_o = \frac{U_{total}}{T} = \frac{U_{total}}{U_{total} + D_{total}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of per-cycle averages (steady-state assumption):**

If $N$ failure cycles occur in time $T$, then the **average cycle length** is:

$$
\text{Cycle Length} = \frac{T}{N} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

The **average uptime per cycle** is $MTBF$ and the **average downtime per cycle** is $MTTR$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into availability expression:**

$$
A_i = \frac{MTBF \cdot N}{MTBF \cdot N + MTTR \cdot N} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5 — Express using failure rate λ:**

Since $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$:

$$
A_i = \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda}}{\frac{1}{\lambda} + MTTR} = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Higher MTTR (slower repairs) reduces availability. Higher failure rate λ also reduces availability. Both factors are penalized in BDM systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost Trade-off Between BDM and PM (Break-Even Model)

**Problem Setup:** A machine has failure rate λ, PM is performed every $T_p$ hours, and the costs are defined as:

- $C_f$ = cost of a BDM repair (emergency, typically 3–5× planned cost).
- $C_p$ = cost of a scheduled PM (planned, cheaper per event).
- $C_d$ = cost of downtime per hour (lost production).

**Step 1 — Expected number of failures per hour under BDM:**

$$
n_f = \lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Expected downtime per hour under BDM:**

$$
D_{BDM} = \lambda \cdot MTTR
$$

**Step 3 — Expected downtime per hour under PM (PM scheduled every $T_p$ hours, with $T_{PM}$ hours of scheduled downtime):**

$$
D_{PM} = \frac{T_{PM}}{T_p} + \lambda \cdot MTTR \cdot e^{-\lambda T_p}
$$

The second term captures **residual failures** (failures occurring despite PM, due to random failure mode).

**Step 4 — Total cost per hour under BDM:**

$$
C_{BDM}^{total} = \lambda \cdot C_f + \lambda \cdot MTTR \cdot C_d
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost per hour under PM:**

$$
C_{PM}^{total} = \frac{C_p}{T_p} + \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda T_p} \cdot C_f + \frac{T_{PM}}{T_p} \cdot C_d + \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda T_p} \cdot MTTR \cdot C_d
$$

**Step 6 — Optimal PM interval (set derivative to zero):**

The optimum $T_p^*$ satisfies:

$$
\frac{dC_{PM}^{total}}{dT_p} = 0
$$

For a simplified textbook KTU version where the **downtime cost is dominated by emergency failure cost**, the break-even condition becomes:

$$
C_f \cdot \lambda = \frac{C_p}{T_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_p^* = \frac{C_p}{\lambda \cdot C_f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This formula is the classic exam question. Note that $T_p^*$ is inversely proportional to λ — higher failure rates demand more frequent PM. It is also proportional to $C_p$ and inversely proportional to $C_f$ — expensive emergency repairs justify aggressive PM.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reliability Function (Exponential Model)

**Step 1 — Define reliability:**
Reliability $R(t)$ is the probability that a system survives up to time $t$ without failure.

**Step 2 — Start with the differential equation:**

For a constant failure rate λ, the probability of surviving an infinitesimal interval $dt$ is:

$$
R(t + dt) = R(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \, dt)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$
\frac{R(t+dt) - R(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot R(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $dt \to 0$:**

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot R(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with initial condition $R(0) = 1$:**

$$
\int_1^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean of exponential distribution = 1/λ = MTBF.** This is a key result — the mean of the exponential reliability model equals the MTBF.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (BDM vs. CM vs. PM vs. CBM)

| Parameter | **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** | **Planned Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | **Scheduled Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trigger** | Functional failure | Known fault (detected) | Calendar / hours / cycles | Sensor threshold breach |
| **Planning** | None (unplanned) | Planned, scheduled | Planned, fixed interval | Planned but trigger-based |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned, random | Planned, controlled | Planned, controlled | Planned, minimal |
| **Cost per event** | Highest (3–5×) | Moderate | Lowest | Moderate (sensor cost) |
| **Spare inventory** | High emergency buffer | Moderate | Planned stock | Low, on-demand |
| **Equipment life** | Often reduced | Restored to standard | Often extended | Maximized |
| **Risk of secondary damage** | High | Moderate | Low | Very low |
| **Best suited for** | Non-critical, low-cost, redundant items | Identifiable defects, scheduled shutdowns | Wear-out regime, high-criticality assets | Critical, sensor-friendly, high-value assets |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** *Distinguish between Breakdown Maintenance and Corrective Maintenance. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM):** Performed *only after* a functional failure has occurred; unplanned and reactive; no advance preparation.
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Performed *after a fault is identified* (not necessarily after full functional failure); may be **planned/deferred** to a convenient shutdown window.
- **Key Distinction:** BDM is a *subset* of CM. CM is the broader category that includes both emergency repair (BDM) and scheduled fault repair. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** *List any three factors considered while selecting a maintenance strategy. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Criticality of equipment** — Safety- and production-critical assets demand preventive/condition-based strategies; non-critical assets may use BDM.
2. **Cost trade-off between preventive cost and failure/downtime cost** — If downtime cost > maintenance cost, PM/CBM is preferred.
3. **Failure pattern / position on the bathtub curve** — Random-failure regime favors BDM; wear-out regime demands scheduled PM. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Module 2 questions demand a **classification table or hierarchy diagram** in the answer. Students who write a wall of text without structure lose 2–3 marks. Always present classifications in a **table format** with **at least 3 columns** (Type → Trigger → Application). Also, define *each* term in **one sentence** before elaborating — definitional omissions are a common 1-mark penalty.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]*

> **(a)** Classify maintenance into different categories. Explain any two categories in detail. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various factors that are considered while selecting a particular maintenance strategy for an equipment in an industry. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Classification of Maintenance:**

| Category | Sub-Category | Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reactive / Unplanned** | Breakdown Maintenance (BDM) | Functional failure |
| **Reactive / Planned** | Corrective Maintenance (CM) | Identified fault |
| **Proactive** | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Calendar / hours / cycles |
| **Proactive** | Condition-Based (CBM) / Predictive (PdM) | Sensor threshold |
| **Improvement** | Design-Out / Modifications | Reliability data trend |

**Detailed Explanation of Two Categories:**

**1. Breakdown Maintenance (BDM):** This is a *run-to-failure* strategy. The equipment is operated until it fails, and only then is repair undertaken. It requires minimum upfront investment in sensors or planning but causes **unplanned downtime, secondary damage, high emergency repair cost, and safety risks**. It is most suitable for non-critical, low-cost, or redundant equipment (e.g., standby pumps, light fixtures, office electronics). **(3.5 marks for BDM)**

**2. Preventive Maintenance (PM):** This is a *time-based* strategy where maintenance is performed at predetermined intervals (calendar, operating hours, or cycles) to reduce the probability of failure. PM includes routine servicing, lubrication, calibration, and overhaul. It extends equipment life, reduces unplanned downtime, and improves safety but introduces **planned downtime** and risk of *over-maintenance* (replacing parts that still had useful life). **(3.5 marks for PM)**

**(b) Factors Considered for Selection of Maintenance Strategy:**

1. **Cost of Maintenance vs. Cost of Downtime:** When downtime cost (lost production + penalty + reputational damage) is much higher than PM cost, PM or CBM is preferred. BDM is economical when the item is cheap to replace. **(1 mark)**

2. **Criticality of Equipment:** Mission-critical equipment (aircraft engines, main boilers) demands high-reliability strategies (CBM/PdM). Non-critical items (office equipment) tolerate BDM. **(1 mark)**

3. **Failure Pattern (Bathtub Curve Position):** In the infant-mortality phase, BDM is appropriate as failures are random. In the wear-out phase, scheduled PM is mandatory. In the useful-life phase, CBM is optimal. **(1 mark)**

4. **Safety and Environmental Requirements:** Equipment whose failure can cause injury, fatality, or environmental damage (e.g., pressure vessels, nuclear systems) must never rely on BDM. **(1 mark)**

5. **Equipment Complexity and Diagnosability:** Complex systems with built-in sensors and condition-monitoring capability (e.g., CNC machines, large turbines) justify CBM investment. Simple machines do not. **(1 mark)**

6. **Availability of Spares and Skilled Manpower:** If emergency spares and technicians are available 24×7, BDM is feasible. If not, PM is needed. **(1 mark)**

7. **Production Schedule:** Continuous-process plants (refineries, 24×7 production) cannot tolerate BDM; batch-process plants can. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q - Internal Choice]*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the concept of Breakdown Maintenance. State its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of Corrective Maintenance. Describe the five stages of the Corrective Maintenance process with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Breakdown Maintenance — Concept, Advantages, Disadvantages:**

**Concept:** Breakdown Maintenance is the corrective maintenance action performed *only after* a functional failure has occurred, with the philosophy of *"operate until it breaks, then repair."* It is purely reactive; no prior intervention is made to prevent or predict the failure. **(2 marks for concept + definition)**

**Advantages of BDM:**

1. **Minimum initial cost** — No need for sensors, PM scheduling software, or condition-monitoring infrastructure. **(1 mark)**
2. **Maximum utilization of useful life** — Parts are not replaced prematurely; full service life is extracted. **(1 mark)**
3. **Simplicity of administration** — Requires no planning, no scheduling, no documentation (except post-failure). **(1 mark)**
4. **Suitable for redundant systems** — Standby equipment can be allowed to fail because backups exist. **(1 mark)**

**Disadvantages of BDM:**

1. **Unplanned downtime** — Production halts with no warning, leading to missed orders and customer dissatisfaction. **(1 mark)**
2. **Higher secondary damage** — A bearing left running to failure can destroy the shaft, gearbox, and motor. **(1 mark)**
3. **3–5× higher emergency repair cost** — Overtime labour, expedited spares, and rush shipping inflate costs. **(1 mark)**
4. **Safety and environmental hazards** — Catastrophic failure (e.g., boiler explosion) can injure personnel and damage the environment. **(1 mark)**
5. **High emergency spare inventory** — Buffer stock must be maintained to avoid long waits. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Corrective Maintenance — Concept and Five-Stage Process:**

**Concept:** Corrective Maintenance is the set of maintenance actions performed after a fault has been identified, intended to restore the item to a state in which it can perform its required function. It includes both **planned (deferred) CM** and **unplanned (immediate) CM**, with BDM being a subset of the latter. **(1.5 marks)**

**The Five-Stage CM Process:**

1. **Stage 1 — Fault Detection:** The fault is identified by the operator (visual/auditory), an alarm system, or a condition-monitoring device. The asset is flagged and isolated from active service. **(1 mark)**

2. **Stage 2 — Fault Diagnosis:** The root cause is investigated using techniques such as FMEA, fault-tree analysis, vibration analysis, oil analysis, or thermography. The defective component is pinpointed. **(1 mark)**

3. **Stage 3 — Decision to Repair/Replace:** An economic and technical comparison is made: repair cost vs. replacement cost vs. cost of continued downtime. A formal "go/no-go" decision is documented. **(1 mark)**

4. **Stage 4 — Execution of Repair/Replacement:** The defective component is repaired or swapped, alignment is restored, and the equipment is reassembled. This stage includes quality checks during the repair. **(1 mark)**

5. **Stage 5 — Verification and Documentation:** The equipment is tested under load, performance is validated, and the failure data (time, mode, cause, parts used, man-hours) is logged in the CMMS for future root-cause analysis and reliability trending. **(1.5 marks)**

> *[Refer to Diagram 3 in Section 4.3 for the CM Process Flow Block Diagram.]* **(0 marks for diagram, but examiners reward clarity — the diagram in the answer script earns appreciation marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing BDM with CM** — Treating them as synonyms. BDM is a *sub-set* of CM, and the distinction of *planned vs. unplanned* must be stated explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting the bathtub curve reference** — When explaining the factors for selecting maintenance, students often forget to mention the *phase of the bathtub curve*. This alone is worth 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing disadvantages without writing advantages** — BDM is not "all bad"; the 3.5-mark "advantages" sub-part requires a balanced answer.
> 4. **No diagram in Part B** — KTU examiners reward diagrams. Always include at least one labelled block diagram or flowchart in a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — MTBF/MTTR/MDT must always carry units (hours, days, etc.).
> 6. **Writing 1-mark answers as 3-mark answers** — Avoid one-liners; a 3-mark answer needs at least **3 bullet points** or a **table**.

---
