- [x] **Always include a diagram or table** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — KTU examiners explicitly reward visual clarity.
- [x] **Criticality index** (e.g., 1–10 scale) is the single most-cited factor in the KTU Module 2 selection-factor questions.
- [x] **BDM is never recommended for safety-critical, environmental-critical, or production-critical systems.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept, Features, Routine Maintenance and Opportunistic Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept, Features, Routine Maintenance and Opportunistic Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Maintenance? (KTU 2024 Definition)

According to the **British Standard BS 3811**, maintenance is formally defined as:

> *"The combination of all technical and associated administrative actions intended to retain an item in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function."*

In simpler KTU board language: **Maintenance is the science and management discipline of keeping equipment, machinery, and physical assets in working condition** so that they continue to deliver their intended function without unexpected failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Maintenance is NOT just "repair after breakdown." It is a **proactive, planned, and economically optimized** activity that includes inspection, servicing, testing, reconditioning, and replacement.

---

## 1.2 Concept of Maintenance — The Bigger Picture

The concept of maintenance revolves around **three pillars**:

1. **Functional Integrity** — Ensuring the asset performs as designed.
2. **Economic Efficiency** — Minimizing the *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)*.
3. **Safety & Reliability** — Protecting human life, environment, and production continuity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Your Smartphone**
> Think of your smartphone. You update the software (preventive maintenance), clear the storage, replace the battery after 2 years (opportunistic replacement), and occasionally get it repaired when the screen breaks (breakdown maintenance). The phone is your "industrial asset." The same logic applies to a CNC machine in a factory, a turbine in a power plant, or a transformer in a substation. **Maintenance = keeping the asset alive and productive.**

---

## 1.3 Classification of Maintenance (Master Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following hierarchical classification of maintenance strategies:

| Sl. No. | Maintenance Type | Trigger | Philosophy |
|:-------:|:-----------------|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | **Breakdown (Reactive) Maintenance** | After failure | "Run till it breaks" |
| 2 | **Routine (Preventive) Maintenance** | Time/usage based | "Service before it fails" |
| 3 | **Opportunistic Maintenance** | When plant/equipment is already down | "Fix the neighbour while the door is open" |
| 4 | **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance** | Condition monitoring data | "Listen to the heartbeat" |
| 5 | **Corrective Maintenance** | Identified defect | "Fix the detected problem" |
| 6 | **Design-Out Maintenance** | During design stage | "Build reliability in" |

---

## 1.4 Defining the Two Key Strategies of This Module

### (A) Routine Maintenance

**Routine Maintenance** is a *time-based, scheduled, and repetitive* set of servicing activities performed on equipment at **predetermined intervals** — irrespective of whether the equipment shows any signs of deterioration.

It includes lubrication, cleaning, calibration, inspection, and minor adjustments as per the manufacturer's schedule.

### (B) Opportunistic Maintenance

**Opportunistic Maintenance** is a *benefit-by-association* strategy. When a machine, line, or plant section is already stopped for a planned shutdown or overhaul, *other nearby components* that are approaching wear-out or have minor defects are serviced **simultaneously** — to avoid a separate future shutdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this powerful?** Because the **shutdown cost is already paid.** Replacing a $200 bearing during a planned turbine overhaul (which already costs $50,000 in downtime) saves the company a *future* $50,000 shutdown. This is the core economic argument behind opportunistic maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Features of Maintenance (Universal Characteristics)

A well-designed maintenance system has the following **features** as per KTU Module 2 syllabus:

1. **Planned and Scheduled** — Activities are pre-decided, not random.
2. **Documented** — Every action is logged in a Maintenance Information System (MIS) or Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS).
3. **Cost-Centric** — Activities must justify their cost through reliability/availability gains.
4. **Skill-Oriented** — Requires trained manpower, special tools, and standard procedures.
5. **Multi-Disciplinary** — Involves mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, and safety engineers.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Updated based on failure data (RCA, FMEA).

---

## 2.2 Routine Maintenance — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Definition (KTU Standard)

> **Routine Maintenance** is *"the maintenance carried out at predetermined intervals or according to prescribed criteria, with the intention of reducing the probability of failure or the degradation of the functioning of an item."*

### 2.2.2 Core Logic (Why does it work?)

The scientific foundation of routine maintenance is the **Bathtub Curve** of equipment failure rate.

The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of equipment over its life follows:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 \quad \text{(for infant mortality region)}$$

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda_{ss} \quad \text{(for useful life region — constant)}$$

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 \cdot e^{\beta(t - t_w)} \quad \text{(for wear-out region — increasing)}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{ss}$ = steady-state failure rate (failures per unit time)
- $\beta$ = wear-out growth coefficient
- $t_w$ = onset of wear-out region

> **Engineering Insight:** By replacing components **before** they enter the wear-out region (i.e., at $t < t_w$), routine maintenance **prevents the exponential rise** in failure probability.

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Identify critical assets** using Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule — 20% of equipment causes 80% of failures).
2. **Establish OEM-recommended service intervals** (hours, cycles, calendar months).
3. **Create a Maintenance Master Plan** with checklists.
4. **Allocate manpower, spares, and tools** ahead of schedule.
5. **Execute the service task** within a permitted window.
6. **Record results, anomalies, and consumption** in the maintenance logbook.
7. **Update the plan** if new failure patterns emerge.

### 2.2.4 Features Specific to Routine Maintenance

| Feature | Description |
|:--------|:------------|
| **Time-Based** | Driven by clock or usage (hours/km/cycles). |
| **Repetitive** | The same tasks repeat at fixed intervals. |
| **Pre-Planned** | Resources are arranged before execution. |
| **Low Sophistication** | Does not require advanced condition-monitoring tools. |
| **Cost Predictable** | Budget can be planned annually. |
| **Reduces Downtime** | Planned service ≠ unplanned breakdown. |
| **Slight Over-Maintenance Risk** | May replace components that still had useful life. |

---

## 2.3 Opportunistic Maintenance — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Definition (KTU Standard)

> **Opportunistic Maintenance** is a strategy in which maintenance activities on a component or subsystem are *postponed and then bundled together* with another scheduled shutdown event for the host system, *taking advantage of the already-paid shutdown cost*.

### 2.3.2 Core Economic Logic

The fundamental economic principle is based on **minimizing the equivalent uniform annual cost (EUAC)** of equipment ownership.

Consider two scenarios for replacing a component:

**Scenario 1 — Independent Shutdown:**
$$EUAC_{indep} = C_{comp} \cdot (A/P, i, n) + C_{shut} \cdot (A/P, i, n) + C_{down}$$

**Scenario 2 — Bundled with Existing Shutdown:**
$$EUAC_{opp} = C_{comp} \cdot (A/P, i, n) + \mathbf{0} + C_{down} \text{ (already accounted)}$$

Where:
- $C_{comp}$ = cost of the component (₹)
- $C_{shut}$ = shutdown cost (₹) — **saved in opportunistic case**
- $C_{down}$ = production loss (₹/hr) × shutdown duration
- $(A/P, i, n)$ = capital recovery factor

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Opportunistic maintenance does **NOT eliminate** the maintenance cost of the component. It **avoids a redundant shutdown**. Hence, it is sometimes called *"piggyback maintenance."*

### 2.3.3 Decision Logic — When is Opportunistic Maintenance Justified?

Opportunistic Maintenance is justified when **ALL** of the following conditions hold:

1. The host system has a **scheduled shutdown** coming within a reasonable window.
2. The component's **remaining useful life** is greater than the time until the next shutdown.
3. The **failure risk during the deferral period** is statistically low (reliability > acceptable threshold, typically **0.95**).
4. The **cost of failure during deferral** is less than the cost of an additional dedicated shutdown.
5. The **resources (spares, manpower)** are available when the shutdown occurs.

### 2.3.4 Features Specific to Opportunistic Maintenance

| Feature | Description |
|:--------|:------------|
| **Event-Triggered** | Linked to another maintenance event, not the calendar. |
| **Cost-Optimizing** | Avoids duplicate shutdown costs. |
| **Risk-Based** | Requires reliability assessment of the deferred component. |
| **Bundling** | Multiple tasks are grouped into one window. |
| **Spares Flexibility** | Inventory can be optimized using pooled spares. |
| **Coordination Heavy** | Needs cross-functional planning between maintenance, production, and procurement. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|:--------|:-----------------|:--------|:------|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | Failures per unit time | failures/hr |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average uptime between failures | hours |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR$ | Average downtime per failure | hours |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Uptime fraction | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Capital Recovery Factor | $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converts present cost to annual | dimensionless |
| Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | $EUAC = C \cdot (A/P, i, n) + C_{down}$ | Annualized maintenance cost | ₹/year |
| Bathtub Curve Wear-out | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{\beta(t - t_w)}$ | Increasing failure rate | failures/hr |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Power Plants:** Turbine overhaul scheduled every 4 years — condenser tubes cleaned, boiler tubes inspected, and pump bearings replaced opportunistically during the same outage.
- **Aviation (MRO):** Aircraft A-check scheduled every 400 flight hours — landing gear, avionics, and auxiliary systems inspected in the same hangar window.
- **Manufacturing (Automotive):** Production line model changeover — every press, robot, and conveyor is serviced opportunistically during the planned changeover.
- **Oil & Gas:** Refinery turnaround (TAR — Turn Around) every 4–6 years — thousands of maintenance jobs bundled into one mega-shutdown.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability Under Routine Maintenance

**Problem Setup:** A pump has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour during its useful life. Routine maintenance is scheduled every $T = 500$ hours. Find the reliability of the pump just *before* the scheduled maintenance.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation

For components with constant failure rate (useful life region), reliability follows the exponential distribution:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the maintenance interval

With $t = T$:

$$R(T) = e^{-\lambda T}$$

### Step 3: Insert numerical values

$$R(500) = e^{-(0.002)(500)}$$

$$R(500) = e^{-1.0}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the exponential

$$R(500) = 0.3679$$

> **Interpretation:** At the moment of scheduled maintenance, the pump has only a **36.79%** probability of having survived without failure. This implies that routine maintenance at 500 hours is *too late* for this pump under this load condition.

### Step 5: Find the optimal interval $T^*$ such that $R(T^*) \geq 0.95$

$$e^{-\lambda T^*} \geq 0.95$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$-\lambda T^* \geq \ln(0.95)$$

$$T^* \leq \dfrac{-\ln(0.95)}{\lambda}$$

$$T^* \leq \dfrac{0.05129}{0.002}$$

$$T^* \leq 25.65 \text{ hours}$$

> **Conclusion:** To maintain 95% reliability between services, routine maintenance must occur every **~25 hours**, not 500 hours. This is a classic KTU board derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Availability Under Routine Maintenance

### Step 1: Define the steady-state availability equation

For a repairable system with exponential failure and repair distributions:

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

### Step 2: Substitute $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$

$$A = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\lambda}}{\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + MTTR}$$

### Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by $\lambda$

$$A = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Given $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hr and $MTTR = 8$ hours:

$$A = \dfrac{1}{1 + (0.001)(8)}$$

$$A = \dfrac{1}{1.008}$$

$$A = 0.9921 = 99.21\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-Benefit of Opportunistic vs. Independent Maintenance

### Setup

A motor must be replaced. Its service life is $n = 5$ years, annual interest rate $i = 10\%$, replacement cost $C_{comp} = \text{₹}50{,}000$. A *dedicated shutdown* costs $C_{shut} = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$. The host system has a scheduled overhaul in 2 years.

### Step 1: Calculate the Capital Recovery Factor

$$(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

$$(A/P, 0.10, 5) = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^5}{(1.10)^5 - 1}$$

$$(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$$

$$(A/P, 0.10, 5) = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1} = \dfrac{0.16105}{0.61051}$$

$$(A/P, 0.10, 5) = 0.26380$$

### Step 2: EUAC for Independent Shutdown

$$EUAC_{indep} = (C_{comp} + C_{shut}) \times (A/P, i, n)$$

$$EUAC_{indep} = (50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) \times 0.26380$$

$$EUAC_{indep} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.26380 = \text{₹}65{,}950 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 3: EUAC for Opportunistic Shutdown (host is already stopping)

$$EUAC_{opp} = C_{comp} \times (A/P, i, n) + 0$$

$$EUAC_{opp} = 50{,}000 \times 0.26380 = \text{₹}13{,}190 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Annual Savings

$$\Delta EUAC = 65{,}950 - 13{,}190 = \text{₹}52{,}760 \text{ per year}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The savings come *entirely* from avoiding the redundant shutdown cost. This is the *raison d'être* of opportunistic maintenance in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Routine vs. Opportunistic Maintenance

| Decision Criterion | Routine Maintenance | Opportunistic Maintenance |
|:-------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Time/usage interval | Existing shutdown event |
| **Cost Driver** | Service labour + spares | Avoided shutdown cost |
| **Failure Risk Window** | Between two consecutive services | Until next host shutdown |
| **Data Requirement** | OEM manual | Reliability + shutdown schedule |
| **Inventory Impact** | High (predictable consumption) | Low (pooled spares) |
| **Manpower Planning** | Distributed, scheduled | Concentrated, shutdown-window |
| **Best Suited For** | Standalone critical equipment | Components in large integrated plants |
| **Disadvantage** | Possible over-maintenance | Possible deferred-failure risk |
| **Cost Predictability** | Very high | Moderate |

---

## 3.5 Combined Optimization: Block Replacement with Opportunistic Add-Ons

In KTU Module 2, a common derivation is the **Block Replacement Policy with Opportunistic Extensions**.

If equipment fails at a Poisson rate $\lambda$, the expected number of failures in interval $(0, T]$ is:

$$E[N(T)] = \lambda T$$

The expected total cost per unit time under block replacement is:

$$E[CU] = \dfrac{C_p + \lambda T \cdot C_f}{T}$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = preventive (routine) replacement cost
- $C_f$ = failure (corrective) replacement cost
- $T$ = block interval

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(E[CU])}{dT} = \dfrac{T \cdot \lambda C_f - (C_p + \lambda T C_f)}{T^2} = 0$$

$$T \cdot \lambda C_f = C_p + \lambda T C_f$$

This simplifies to give the optimal block interval:

$$T^* = \dfrac{C_p}{\lambda (C_f - C_p)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimal routine interval $T^*$ grows when $C_p$ (preventive cost) is low and shrinks when failure cost $C_f$ dominates. This is a *high-yield KTU board result* — write it down for exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Maintenance Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MAINTENANCE STRATEGY] --> B[Reactive]
    A --> C[Preventive]
    A --> D[Predictive]
    A --> E[Opportunistic]
    A --> F[Design-Out]

    B --> B1[Breakdown Maintenance]
    B --> B2[Corrective Maintenance]

    C --> C1[Routine Maintenance]
    C --> C2[Scheduled Overhaul]
    C --> C3[Block Replacement]

    D --> D1[Vibration Analysis]
    D --> D2[Thermography]
    D --> D3[Oil Analysis]

    E --> E1[Piggyback on Planned Shutdown]
    E --> E2[Bundle with Model Changeover]

    F --> F1[Reliability Centred Design]
    F --> F2[Built-In Test Equipment]
```

---

## 4.2 Routine Maintenance — Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Equipment Running] --> B{Timer/Usage Reached T?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Issue Work Order]
    C --> D[Allocate Manpower, Spares, Tools]
    D --> E[Execute Routine Service Tasks]
    E --> F[Lubrication, Cleaning, Calibration, Inspection]
    F --> G[Log Results in CMMS]
    G --> H[Reset Timer/Counter]
    H --> A
```

---

## 4.3 Opportunistic Maintenance — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host System Scheduled Shutdown] --> B{Is component approaching wear-out?}
    B -- No --> C[No Action]
    B -- Yes --> D{Remaining life > deferral period?}
    D -- No --> E[Independent Replacement Now]
    D -- Yes --> F{Failure risk acceptable? Reliability > 0.95}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Bundle with Host Shutdown]
    G --> H[Combined Maintenance Window]
    H --> I[Single Shutdown Cost - SAVINGS ACHIEVED]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block Architecture — Routine vs. Opportunistic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROUTINE
        R1[Time Trigger]
        R2[OEM Schedule]
        R3[Repetitive Tasks]
        R4[Predictable Cost]
        R5[Possible Over-Maintenance]
    end

    subgraph OPPORTUNISTIC
        O1[Event Trigger]
        O2[Host Shutdown Driven]
        O3[Bundled Tasks]
        O4[Shutdown Cost Avoided]
        O5[Defers Failure Risk]
    end

    ROUTINE --> COMPARE{Cost & Risk Trade-off}
    OPPORTUNISTIC --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> DECISION[Optimal Maintenance Mix]
```

---

## 4.5 Cost-Optimization Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Plant as Production Planner
    participant Maint as Maintenance Engineer
    participant Finance as Cost Controller

    Plant->>Maint: Schedule of upcoming shutdown
    Maint->>Maint: Identify components due for service
    Maint->>Finance: Compute EUAC for opportunistic vs independent
    Finance-->>Maint: Approval if EUAC_opp < EUAC_indep
    Maint->>Plant: Bundle tasks into one window
    Plant-->>Maint: Confirm downtime allowance
    Maint->>Maint: Execute combined maintenance
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Routine Maintenance. List any four features of routine maintenance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Routine Maintenance is the maintenance carried out at *predetermined intervals* (time or usage-based) according to prescribed criteria, with the intention of *reducing the probability of failure* or the *degradation of functioning* of an item, as defined in BS 3811.

**Four Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Time-Based / Usage-Based Trigger** — Scheduled by hours, cycles, or calendar.
2. **Repetitive & Pre-Planned** — Same tasks recur at fixed intervals.
3. **Cost Predictable** — Annual budgeting is straightforward.
4. **OEM-Driven** — Based on manufacturer's service schedule.
5. *(Any other valid feature)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is opportunistic maintenance? When is it preferred over independent replacement?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Opportunistic Maintenance is a cost-optimization strategy in which maintenance activities on a component are *deliberately deferred and bundled* with another *already-planned shutdown* of the host system, taking advantage of the shutdown cost that is *already incurred*.

**Preferred When (1.5 Marks):**

1. A scheduled shutdown of the host plant/system is imminent.
2. The component's remaining useful life exceeds the deferral period.
3. The cost of an *independent shutdown* exceeds the cost of *deferred failure risk*.
4. Reliability during the deferral window is above the acceptable threshold (typically ≥ 0.95).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the *concept and features* of **Routine Maintenance** in detail. Discuss its *advantages and limitations* with respect to industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0015$ failures/hour. Determine: **(i)** the reliability at $t = 200$ hours, **(ii)** the optimal routine maintenance interval $T^*$ to maintain a reliability of **0.90**, and **(iii)** the steady-state availability if $MTTR = 10$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Routine Maintenance: Concept & Features (7 Marks)

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**
Routine Maintenance is a *time-based preventive maintenance* strategy. Activities like lubrication, cleaning, calibration, filter replacement, and minor adjustments are carried out at fixed intervals (hours, km, cycles, or calendar months) as prescribed by the OEM or internal engineering standards. The aim is to *prevent failure before it occurs* and to keep the equipment within its design performance envelope.

**2. Features (3 Marks):**
- **Scheduled** — Activities follow a fixed master plan.
- **Repetitive** — The same set of tasks repeats periodically.
- **OEM-Aligned** — Based on manufacturer's recommendations.
- **Documented** — Logged in CMMS/EAM systems.
- **Cost Predictable** — Spares and labour are budgeted annually.
- **Involves Standard Tools** — No sophisticated condition-monitoring required.

**3. Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Reduces unplanned downtime significantly.
- Extends equipment life.
- Improves safety and product quality.

**4. Limitations (1 Mark):**
- May cause *over-maintenance* (replacing parts with residual life).
- Does not adapt to actual equipment condition.
- High inventory holding cost for spares.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 0.0015$ failures/hour
- $t = 200$ hours (for part i)
- Target $R = 0.90$ (for part ii)
- $MTTR = 10$ hours (for part iii)

**Part (i) — Reliability at 200 hours (2 Marks):**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$R(200) = e^{-(0.0015)(200)} = e^{-0.30}$$

$$R(200) = 0.7408$$

**Interpretation:** The pump has a **74.08%** chance of surviving 200 hours without failure.

---

**Part (ii) — Optimal Interval $T^*$ for $R = 0.90$ (2.5 Marks):**

$$R(T^*) = e^{-\lambda T^*} = 0.90$$

$$-\lambda T^* = \ln(0.90)$$

$$T^* = \dfrac{-\ln(0.90)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.10536}{0.0015}$$

$$T^* = 70.24 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation:** Routine service must occur every **~70 hours** to maintain 90% reliability.

---

**Part (iii) — Steady-State Availability (2.5 Marks):**

$$MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0015} = 666.67 \text{ hours}$$

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{666.67}{666.67 + 10}$$

$$A = \dfrac{666.67}{676.67} = 0.9852 = \mathbf{98.52\%}$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the *concept, features, and economic rationale* of **Opportunistic Maintenance**. Under what conditions is it preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A motor has a service life of $n = 6$ years. The replacement cost is $C_{comp} = \text{₹}75{,}000$. If it is replaced *independently*, an additional shutdown cost of $C_{shut} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000$ is incurred. The interest rate is $i = 12\%$. The host system has a planned overhaul in 2 years. Compare the **EUAC** of independent vs. opportunistic replacement. What is the annual saving? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Opportunistic Maintenance (7 Marks)

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**
Opportunistic Maintenance is a *strategic deferral* of maintenance activity. When a host system has a *planned shutdown*, components that are due for maintenance but *not yet failed* are serviced simultaneously — leveraging the already-paid shutdown cost.

**2. Economic Rationale (2 Marks):**
A dedicated shutdown typically costs ₹1 lakh to ₹10 crore in lost production. By *piggybacking* tasks, the company pays the shutdown cost *once* instead of multiple times. This minimizes the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** without sacrificing reliability.

**3. Features (1.5 Marks):**
- Event-triggered, not time-triggered.
- Requires cross-functional coordination.
- Risk-based deferral decision.
- Bundled resource utilization.
- Spares pooling possible.

**4. Preferred Conditions (1.5 Marks):**
- Host shutdown is within the component's remaining useful life.
- Reliability during deferral remains above threshold.
- Independent shutdown cost is significantly higher than deferral risk cost.
- Spares, tools, and manpower are available during the bundled window.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 6$ years
- $C_{comp} = \text{₹}75{,}000$
- $C_{shut} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000$ (independent case)
- $i = 12\%$

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor (1.5 Marks):**

$$(A/P, 0.12, 6) = \dfrac{0.12 \times (1.12)^6}{(1.12)^6 - 1}$$

$$(1.12)^6 = 1.97382$$

$$(A/P, 0.12, 6) = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.97382}{1.97382 - 1} = \dfrac{0.23686}{0.97382}$$

$$(A/P, 0.12, 6) = 0.24323$$

**Step 2 — EUAC of Independent Replacement (2 Marks):**

$$EUAC_{indep} = (C_{comp} + C_{shut}) \times (A/P, i, n)$$

$$EUAC_{indep} = (75{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000) \times 0.24323$$

$$EUAC_{indep} = 3{,}75{,}000 \times 0.24323 = \text{₹}91{,}211 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — EUAC of Opportunistic Replacement (2 Marks):**

$$EUAC_{opp} = C_{comp} \times (A/P, i, n) + 0$$

$$EUAC_{opp} = 75{,}000 \times 0.24323 = \text{₹}18{,}242 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Savings (1.5 Marks):**

$$\Delta EUAC = 91{,}211 - 18{,}242 = \mathbf{\text{₹}72{,}969 \text{ per year}}$$

> **Conclusion:** By bundling the motor replacement with the host plant's planned overhaul, the company saves **₹72,969 annually** — a massive 80% reduction in annualized cost. This is the core economic argument for opportunistic maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Preventive" with "Routine":** Routine maintenance is a *subset* of preventive maintenance. Always mention that routine = *time-based* specifically.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** In availability calculations, **MTBF and MTTR must be in the same units** (hours or minutes). Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mismatch.
> 3. **Negative Sign in Logarithm:** Many students write $\ln(0.90) = +0.105$ instead of $-0.105$. The negative sign *must* be there to compute $T^*$ correctly.
> 4. **Misapplying Bathtub Curve:** Routine maintenance is *ineffective* in the infant-mortality and random-failure regions — it shines in the *wear-out* region only.
> 5. **Opportunistic ≠ Free:** Students often claim opportunistic maintenance "eliminates cost." It only *avoids a redundant shutdown*. The component replacement cost remains.
> 6. **Formula Transcription:** When writing $(A/P, i, n)$, ensure $(1+i)^n - 1$ is the denominator — a common sign-error loses 2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Maintenance** = retain/restore an item to perform its required function (BS 3811).
- ✅ **Routine Maintenance** = *Time/usage-based*, repetitive, OEM-driven, cost-predictable.
- ✅ **Opportunistic Maintenance** = *Event-triggered*, bundles tasks into another shutdown, *saves shutdown cost*.
- ✅ **Reliability** under constant failure rate: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ✅ **Optimal routine interval** for target reliability $R^*$: $T^* = \dfrac{-\ln(R^*)}{\lambda}$.
- ✅ **Availability**: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}$.
- ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor**: $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- ✅ **EUAC** of independent maintenance includes **both** component + shutdown cost.
- ✅ **EUAC** of opportunistic maintenance includes **only** component cost.
- ✅ **Opportunistic Justification Conditions:** (1) Host shutdown imminent, (2) Remaining life > deferral, (3) Reliability > 0.95, (4) Failure cost < saved shutdown cost.
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve** has 3 regions: infant mortality, useful life (constant $\lambda$), wear-out (increasing).
- ✅ Routine maintenance works best in the **wear-out region** by replacing components before exponential failure onset.
- ✅ KTU board exam loves the $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ derivation — practice it thoroughly.
- ✅ KTU board exam loves the EUAC comparison numerical — practice the $(A/P, i, n)$ formula.
- ✅ Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value notation — never use `\|` inside markdown tables.
- ✅ Always state units explicitly: hours, ₹/year, failures/hour, dimensionless ratios.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept, Features, Proactive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept, Features, Proactive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Proactive Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proactive Maintenance (PM)** is a systematic, scheduled, and condition-driven maintenance philosophy that anticipates equipment degradation *before* it culminates in functional failure. Unlike reactive (run-to-failure) approaches, it focuses on the **root causes of failure**, integrating routine inspection, scheduled servicing, condition monitoring, and predictive analytics to preserve the designed functional capability of physical assets throughout their operational life cycle.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, proactive maintenance is classified as a **preventive-strategic maintenance paradigm** that combines:
- **Time-based preventive actions** (scheduled overhauls)
- **Condition-based interventions** (vibration, thermography, oil analysis)
- **Risk-based prioritization** (criticality assessment)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Health Check-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Visiting a Doctor Before Falling Sick**
> Imagine you drive a two-wheeler daily. A **reactive approach** means you only repair the bike after the chain snaps on the highway. A **proactive approach** means you regularly lubricate the chain, check tyre pressure every fortnight, inspect brake pads every 5,000 km, and replace the engine oil as per schedule — *preventing* the breakdown from ever happening. Proactive maintenance is exactly this "wellness check-up" applied to industrial machinery.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Proactive Maintenance

The following universally accepted reliability and maintainability metrics form the backbone of proactive maintenance evaluation:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operational uptime between two consecutive breakdowns.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to restore a failed asset.
- **Availability (A):** The fraction of time the equipment is ready for productive use.
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** A composite metric measuring productivity against perfect manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are critical for evaluating the effectiveness of any proactive maintenance program and are frequently asked in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Philosophy of Proactive Maintenance

The proactive maintenance approach rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **Anticipation of Failure** — Failure is treated as a probabilistic event, not a surprise. Every component has a degradation curve (the **P-F Curve** — Potential Failure to Functional Failure interval).
2. **Root-Cause Elimination** — Beyond fixing symptoms, proactive maintenance seeks to identify and eliminate the underlying cause (e.g., misalignment causing premature bearing failure).
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Each maintenance event generates data that refines future maintenance strategies through feedback loops.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

A structured proactive maintenance program follows this sequence:

- **Step 1: Asset Inventory & Criticality Analysis**
  - List all machinery.
  - Rank them by criticality (production loss, safety risk, cost impact).
- **Step 2: Failure Mode Identification (FMEA)**
  - Identify potential failure modes for each critical asset.
- **Step 3: Task Selection & Scheduling**
  - Decide whether to use time-based, condition-based, or predictive tasks.
- **Step 4: Resource Allocation**
  - Assign manpower, spares, tools, and budget.
- **Step 5: Execution & Documentation**
  - Carry out tasks; record observations in CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System).
- **Step 6: Performance Review**
  - Calculate KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, OEE) and refine schedules.

## 2.3 Key Features of Proactive Maintenance

| **S.No.** | **Feature** | **Engineering Significance** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Scheduled Inspections** | Detects degradation at the incipient stage, preventing catastrophic failure |
| 2 | **Condition Monitoring** | Uses vibration, thermography, ultrasonic, oil analysis — non-destructive testing |
| 3 | **Predictive Analytics** | Forecasts Remaining Useful Life (RUL) using sensor data trends |
| 4 | **Failure Mode Focus** | Targets root causes rather than symptoms (e.g., unbalance, misalignment, lubrication) |
| 5 | **Planned Downtime** | Maintenance is executed during scheduled shutdowns, avoiding unplanned stops |
| 6 | **Data-Driven Decisions** | CMMS/EAM systems store historical data for trend analysis |
| 7 | **Cost Optimization** | Reduces spare inventory, emergency repair costs, and production losses |
| 8 | **Safety & Compliance** | Adheres to OSHA and statutory requirements; improves workplace safety |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four formulas constitute the **core mathematical toolkit** for any proactive maintenance problem in KTU examinations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \quad [\text{hours/failure}]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\text{Total Repair Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \quad [\text{hours/repair}]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability (A)} &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100 \quad [\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality} \quad [\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In a power plant, an availability of 95% means the turbine is operationally ready 95% of the scheduled time. In automotive assembly, an OEE of 85% (World-Class benchmark) indicates near-optimal utilization. Proactive maintenance programs target **A ≥ 95%** and **OEE ≥ 85%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Availability Equation from First Principles

**Given Data:**
A centrifugal pump operates for **8,000 hours** in one year. During this period, it failed **4 times**, and the cumulative downtime for all repairs was **40 hours**.

**Objective:** Compute the Availability of the pump.

### Step 1: Calculate Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Operating Time} &= 8000 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Number of Failures} &= 4 \\
\text{MTBF} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\
&= \frac{8000}{4} \\
\text{MTBF} &= 2000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The pump, on average, runs 2,000 hours before encountering a failure event.

### Step 2: Calculate Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Repair Downtime} &= 40 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Number of Repairs} &= 4 \\
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\text{Total Repair Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \\
&= \frac{40}{4} \\
\text{MTTR} &= 10 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** On average, the pump is offline for 10 hours per repair event — a relatively quick turnaround.

### Step 3: Calculate Availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability (A)} &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2000}{2000 + 10} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2000}{2010} \times 100 \\
&\approx 99.50 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 99.50% availability is excellent and indicates an effective proactive maintenance regime.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Maintenance Types

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield comparison** frequently tested in KTU Module-2 questions.

| **Parameter** | **Reactive (Breakdown)** | **Preventive (Scheduled)** | **Predictive (Condition)** | **Proactive (Root-Cause)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Trigger** | After failure | Calendar/usage-based | Sensor-based | Failure-cause-based |
| **Cost** | High (emergency) | Moderate | Moderate-High | Lowest long-term |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned | Planned | Planned | Planned |
| **Data Need** | None | Low | High | Very High |
| **Spares Inventory** | High (emergency) | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Equipment Life** | Reduced | Extended | Extended | Maximized |
| **Failure Prevention** | None | Partial | Strong | Strongest |
| **Examples** | Bearing collapse | Oil change every 6 months | Vibration trend triggers | Eliminate misalignment root cause |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: OEE Calculation

**Problem:** A CNC machine has the following one-month data:
- Planned Production Time = **500 hours**
- Actual Operating Time = **420 hours** (80 hours of downtime)
- Ideal Cycle Time = **2 minutes/unit**
- Total Units Produced = **11,000**
- Defective Units = **550**

**Step 1: Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability} &= \frac{\text{Actual Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{420}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 84.0 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Performance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Output} &= \frac{420 \times 60}{2} = 12{,}600 \text{ units} \\
\text{Performance} &= \frac{11{,}000}{12{,}600} \times 100 \\
&\approx 87.30 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Good Units} &= 11{,}000 - 550 = 10{,}450 \\
\text{Quality} &= \frac{10{,}450}{11{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 95.0 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: OEE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= 84.0 \ \% \times 87.30 \ \% \times 95.0 \ \% \\
&= 69.67 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** OEE of 69.67% indicates significant improvement potential through proactive maintenance interventions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Proactive Maintenance — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the functional architecture of a proactive maintenance system, illustrating how data flows from physical assets through monitoring systems into decision-making and corrective action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Asset / Equipment] --> B[In-Service Degradation Begins]
    B --> C{Failure Mode Triggered?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Potential Failure Detected]
    C -- No --> B
    D --> E[Condition Monitoring Sensors]
    E --> F[Vibration / Thermography / Oil Analysis]
    F --> G[Data Acquisition and CMMS Database]
    G --> H[Trend Analysis and RUL Prediction]
    H --> I{Threshold Exceeded?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Work Order Generated]
    I -- No --> E
    J --> K[Maintenance Planning and Scheduling]
    K --> L[Spare Parts and Resource Allocation]
    L --> M[Planned Maintenance Execution]
    M --> N[Root-Cause Analysis]
    N --> O[Design Modification or Lubrication Upgrade]
    O --> P[Feedback to CMMS for Continuous Improvement]
    P --> A
```

**Interpretation of Flow:**
- **A → B:** Every equipment begins to wear the moment it is commissioned.
- **C → D:** The P-F curve dictates a window between Potential Failure and Functional Failure.
- **E → F:** Non-destructive sensors capture degradation signatures.
- **G → H:** Data analytics translate raw signals into Remaining Useful Life forecasts.
- **I → J:** Decision logic triggers maintenance only when economically justified.
- **N → O:** Root-cause elimination distinguishes *proactive* from merely *preventive* maintenance.

---

## 4.2 Strategic Hierarchy of Maintenance Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maintenance Strategy Continuum] --> B[Reactive Maintenance]
    A --> C[Preventive Maintenance]
    A --> D[Predictive Maintenance]
    A --> E[Proactive Maintenance]
    B --> B1[Run to Failure]
    B --> B2[Emergency Repair]
    C --> C1[Time-Based Servicing]
    C --> C2[Scheduled Overhauls]
    D --> D1[Vibration Analysis]
    D --> D2[Thermography]
    D --> D3[Ultrasonic Testing]
    E --> E1[Root-Cause Elimination]
    E --> E2[Design Improvement]
    E --> E3[Operating Practice Enhancement]
```

**Key Insight:** Proactive maintenance is the **highest maturity level** in the maintenance strategy continuum. It transcends the question of *when* to maintain (predictive) and addresses *why* failures occur.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Proactive Maintenance. List any four of its key features.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Proactive maintenance is a maintenance philosophy that anticipates equipment failure by eliminating its root causes before functional breakdown occurs. It integrates scheduled inspections, condition monitoring, and root-cause analysis.

**Four Key Features:**
1. **Scheduled inspections** to detect incipient defects.
2. **Condition monitoring** using vibration, thermography, and oil analysis.
3. **Root-cause focus** — eliminates underlying causes like misalignment or contamination.
4. **Data-driven decisions** through CMMS/EAM systems for continuous improvement.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Preventive and Proactive Maintenance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Preventive Maintenance** | **Proactive Maintenance** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Basis** | Time or usage schedule | Root-cause elimination |
| **Approach** | Scheduled tasks at fixed intervals | Condition-driven + design improvements |
| **Goal** | Prevent failure occurrence | Prevent failure *recurrence* |
| **Scope** | Component-level | System-level redesign |
| **Example** | Replacing oil every 6 months | Redesigning lubrication system to extend oil life |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the concept of Proactive Maintenance. Discuss its salient features with examples from a manufacturing industry.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:**
Proactive maintenance is a forward-looking maintenance strategy that combines **predictive diagnostics**, **scheduled interventions**, and **root-cause analysis** to prevent failures before they occur. Unlike reactive maintenance (which responds after failure) or preventive maintenance (which follows fixed schedules), proactive maintenance focuses on **why** failures happen and seeks to design them out of the system.

**Salient Features with Industrial Examples:**

1. **Condition Monitoring Using Sensors**
   - *Example:* A thermal power plant uses online vibration sensors on turbine bearings. A rising RMS velocity triggers an alert, prompting alignment correction before the bearing seizes.

2. **Scheduled Servicing**
   - *Example:* An automotive assembly line schedules robot arm lubrication every 2,000 operating hours, irrespective of visible wear.

3. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
   - *Example:* A chemical plant identifies pump seal leakage as a high-RPN (Risk Priority Number) failure mode and redesigns the seal arrangement.

4. **Root-Cause Analysis (RCA)**
   - *Example:* Repeated motor failures traced to voltage imbalance — root cause addressed by installing voltage stabilizers rather than repeatedly rewinding motors.

5. **Data-Driven Decision Making**
   - *Example:* CMMS software tracks MTBF for each asset, allowing engineers to extend or reduce service intervals based on real performance.

6. **Integration with Design and Operations**
   - *Example:* Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) updated to include proper startup/shutdown sequences, reducing thermal stress failures.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A hydraulic press in a forging shop operates for 6,000 hours in a year. During this period, it experienced 5 breakdowns with a total downtime of 75 hours. Calculate (i) MTBF, (ii) MTTR, and (iii) Availability. Comment on the effectiveness of the maintenance system.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total Operating Time = 6,000 hours
- Number of Failures = 5
- Total Downtime = 75 hours

**(i) MTBF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \frac{6000}{5} \\
&= 1200 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) MTTR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{75}{5} \\
&= 15 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability} &= \frac{1200}{1200 + 15} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1200}{1215} \times 100 \\
&\approx 98.77 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Availability of 98.77% is good but not world-class (≥99.5%). MTTR of 15 hours is high; the maintenance system should be reviewed to **reduce repair time** through better spare availability, operator training, and condition monitoring. Implementing proactive maintenance could potentially raise availability to **≥99.5%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a proactive maintenance system. Highlight the role of condition monitoring in this architecture.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (Refer to SECTION 4.1 for the full Mermaid architecture).**

The functional architecture of proactive maintenance comprises the following sequential blocks:

1. **Equipment** — Physical asset under operation.
2. **Degradation Detection** — Sensors capture incipient fault signatures.
3. **Condition Monitoring** — Vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, ultrasonic testing.
4. **Data Acquisition System (CMMS/EAM)** — Centralized data repository.
5. **Trend Analysis & RUL Prediction** — Algorithms forecast remaining useful life.
6. **Decision Threshold Module** — Compares actual values against pre-set limits.
7. **Work Order Generation** — Automatic notification when thresholds are breached.
8. **Planned Execution** — Maintenance performed during scheduled downtime.
9. **Root-Cause Feedback Loop** — Lessons fed back to design and operations.

**Role of Condition Monitoring:**
Condition monitoring acts as the **sensory nervous system** of proactive maintenance. It enables:
- **Early detection** during the P-F interval (Potential Failure to Functional Failure).
- **Quantitative trending** of parameters like vibration amplitude, temperature rise, and lubricant particle counts.
- **Cost avoidance** by preventing catastrophic secondary damage.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the various features that differentiate proactive maintenance from conventional preventive maintenance. Why is proactive maintenance considered the most advanced maintenance strategy?

**Model Answer:**

**Distinguishing Features:**

| **Feature** | **Preventive Maintenance** | **Proactive Maintenance** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Maintenance Trigger | Fixed time/usage intervals | Condition trend + root cause |
| Failure Addressed | Recurrence of symptoms | Original failure cause |
| Information Source | Manufacturer manuals | Sensor data + failure history |
| Equipment Life Impact | Moderate improvement | Significant life extension |
| Spare Inventory | Moderate | Optimized (just-in-time) |
| Cost Effectiveness | Good | Excellent (life-cycle cost) |
| Continuous Improvement | Limited | Built-in feedback loop |

**Why Proactive is the Most Advanced Strategy:**

1. **Addresses Root Causes:** It goes beyond symptom treatment to eliminate the fundamental causes of failure — misalignment, contamination, improper lubrication, and overloading.

2. **Maximum Equipment Life Extension:** By preventing repeated failure modes, asset life is significantly prolonged, improving return on capital.

3. **Lowest Life-Cycle Cost:** Although initial investment in monitoring systems may be high, the long-term savings in spares, downtime, and energy exceed this cost.

4. **Highest Availability:** Predictive intervention during the P-F window ensures near-zero unplanned downtime.

5. **Integration with Industry 4.0:** Proactive maintenance aligns perfectly with IoT-enabled smart manufacturing, AI-driven analytics, and digital twins.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "Preventive" with "Proactive"** — this is the single most common mistake costing 2–3 marks per answer.
> - **Avoid omitting numerical units** in MTBF/MTTR calculations (always write `hours` or `hours/failure`).
> - **Never state Proactive = Predictive** — Predictive is a *subset* used within the broader Proactive strategy.
> - **Always mention root-cause analysis** when defining Proactive — this is the hallmark feature examiners expect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Proactive maintenance = *Anticipation + Root-Cause Elimination + Continuous Improvement.*
- **Core Philosophy:** Prevent failure *recurrence*, not just *occurrence.*
- **Key Strategies Under Proactive Umbrella:** Preventive + Predictive + Condition-Based + Risk-Based maintenance.
- **Hallmark Feature:** Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) using tools like **5-Why Analysis** and **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**.
- **Sensory Tools:** Vibration analysis, thermography, oil/ferrography, ultrasonic NDT.
- **Standard Metrics (Master These):**
  - $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$
  - $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$
  - $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
  - $\text{OEE} = A \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$
- **P-F Curve Concept:** The window between Potential Failure (P) and Functional Failure (F) is where proactive intervention must occur.
- **Maturity Hierarchy:** Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Proactive (highest maturity).
- **World-Class Benchmarks:** Availability ≥ **99.5%**, OEE ≥ **85%**.
- **Difference to Memorize:**
  - Preventive = *When* to fix (time-based).
  - Predictive = *When* failure will occur (condition-based).
  - Proactive = *Why* failure occurs (root-cause based).
- **Industrial Examples:** Power plant turbine monitoring, automotive robot arm servicing, CNC machine OEE tracking.
- **Exam Tip:** Always close any proactive maintenance answer with a **life-cycle cost** or **continuous improvement** statement to earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept, Features, Preventive Maintenance - Basics, Predictive Maintenance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept, Features, Preventive Maintenance - Basics, Predictive Maintenance

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Maintenance Engineering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance Engineering** is the discipline of engineering concerned with the planning, scheduling, execution, and control of all activities required to keep a physical asset, machine, or production system in a desired operating condition, or to restore it to that condition, in the most cost-effective manner over its service life.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, Maintenance Engineering is treated as a **strategic management function** that bridges operations management, reliability engineering, and asset life-cycle economics. It is not merely "repairing broken machines" — it is a **proactive, planned, data-driven philosophy** that aims to maximize **equipment effectiveness** while minimizing **total cost of ownership (TCO)**.

The British Standard **BS 3811 : 1984** (still widely cited in KTU references) defines maintenance as:
> *"The combination of all technical and corresponding administrative actions intended to retain an item in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body Checkup

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of maintenance like human healthcare.
>
> - **Breakdown Maintenance (Reactive)** = Wait until you have a heart attack, then rush to the ER.
> - **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** = Going for a regular health checkup every 6 months — blood tests, ECG, dental cleaning — even though you "feel fine."
> - **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** = Wearing a smartwatch (Apple Watch, Fitbit) that continuously monitors your heart rate, blood oxygen, ECG — and **alerts you the moment something looks abnormal**, *before* any disease actually develops.
>
> PdM is to PM what modern wearable health tech is to the old annual physical. PM is *calendar-based*; PdM is *condition-based*.

This analogy perfectly captures the philosophical shift in modern maintenance thinking — from **"fix when broken"** → **"service on schedule"** → **"service only when symptoms appear"**.

### 1.3 Concept of Maintenance — Key Terminology

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition | Standard Metric |
|------|-------------------------|-----------------|
| **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | Average time a system performs its intended function between two consecutive failures | **hours** |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | Average time required to restore a failed system to working condition | **hours** |
| **Availability (A)** | Fraction of time that a system is in a functioning state | **dimensionless (%)** |
| **Reliability R(t)** | Probability that a system performs its function under stated conditions for a stated period | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |
| **Maintainability** | Probability that a failed system will be restored to operational state within a given time | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |

### 1.4 Features of an Effective Maintenance System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Hallmark Features of Modern Maintenance Engineering (KTU-Standard Framework):**

1. **Proactive Orientation** — Driven by planning and forecasting, *not* by crisis.
2. **Systematic Documentation** — Every action is logged (CMMS — Computerized Maintenance Management System).
3. **Reliability-Centered** — Decisions are based on failure data, not intuition.
4. **Cost-Optimization Mindset** — Balances maintenance cost vs. downtime cost.
5. **Safety & Environmental Compliance** — Aligned with OSHA / Factory Act / ISO 14001.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Uses root cause analysis (RCA) and feedback loops.
7. **Cross-functional Integration** — Involves Operations, Quality, Design, and Finance teams.

### 1.5 Preventive Maintenance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is a scheduled, time- or usage-based maintenance strategy wherein servicing, inspection, lubrication, calibration, or part replacement is carried out **at predetermined intervals** — regardless of whether the equipment is currently showing any signs of degradation.

The goal of PM is to **reduce the probability of failure** or **delay degradation** of components — analogous to changing your car's engine oil every 5,000 km *even if the oil looks fine*.

### 1.6 Predictive Maintenance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** — also called **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** — is a strategy that uses real-time or near-real-time **non-invasive monitoring** of equipment health parameters (vibration, temperature, acoustics, lubrication chemistry) to **detect incipient faults**, diagnose their root cause, and **predict the Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** so that maintenance action is taken *only when actually warranted*.

The ISO 13373 / ISO 17359 standards govern vibration-based and condition-monitoring-based PdM respectively.

### 1.7 The Preventive ↔ Predictive Spectrum

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Ladder (from least to most intelligent):**
>
> **Reactive (BM)** → **Preventive (PM, calendar-based)** → **Predictive (PdM, condition-based)** → **Prescriptive (AI-driven, prescriptive analytics)**
>
> PM *assumes* failure modes will develop on a known schedule.
> PdM *measures* whether failure modes are actually developing, and intervenes only when evidence demands it.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Maintenance Strategy — Hierarchical Logic

Modern maintenance strategies are arranged in a **maturity hierarchy** that reflects increasing levels of information technology, data availability, and engineering sophistication:

#### Step 1 — Reactive (Breakdown) Maintenance
- **Trigger:** Equipment has already failed.
- **Logic:** Wait → Fail → Repair → Resume.
- **Advantage:** Zero upfront maintenance cost.
- **Disadvantage:** Catastrophic downtime cost, secondary damage, safety hazards.

#### Step 2 — Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance
- **Trigger:** Calendar time *or* usage hours.
- **Logic:** Service at $T_{PM}$ regardless of condition.
- **Advantage:** Reduces unplanned downtime.
- **Disadvantage:** May service equipment that is still healthy (over-maintenance cost) OR miss incipient faults that develop faster than the schedule (under-maintenance risk).

#### Step 3 — Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance
- **Trigger:** Measured degradation parameter crosses a preset alarm threshold.
- **Logic:** Continuously monitor → Alarm → Diagnose → Schedule intervention before failure.
- **Advantage:** Maintenance performed *exactly when needed* — minimizing both downtime and unnecessary servicing.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher initial sensor + software investment; requires trained analysts.

### 2.2 The Preventive Maintenance Framework — Deep Dive

PM consists of **two fundamental sub-activities**:

**(a) Planned (Scheduled) Servicing**
- Lubrication, cleaning, calibration, filter replacement, alignment checks.
- Performed at fixed intervals (daily, weekly, monthly, annually) or after fixed usage units (e.g., every 500 machine-hours).

**(b) Condition-Directed Servicing**
- Inspection rounds (visual, auditory, tactile).
- Non-destructive testing (NDT) such as dye-penetrant, ultrasonic thickness gauging.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> Note carefully that PM is *broader* than PdM. PM **includes** condition-directed tasks that may rely on simple inspections (which are themselves a primitive form of condition monitoring). PdM is the *technologically advanced* descendant of this condition-directed branch.

#### 2.2.1 The PM Decision Logic — Six Operational Steps

1. **Identify** critical equipment using Pareto analysis (80/20 rule).
2. **Classify** equipment by criticality (A, B, C ranking).
3. **List** all relevant preventive tasks for each asset.
4. **Determine** the frequency of each task (manufacturer recommendation + historical failure data).
5. **Schedule** the tasks using CMMS / Gantt chart tools.
6. **Review & Update** the PM plan every 6–12 months based on failure data feedback.

### 2.3 Predictive Maintenance Framework — Deep Dive

PdM is built on three scientific pillars:

| Pillar | What is Measured | Sensor / Instrument |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Vibration Analysis** | Acceleration, velocity, displacement | Accelerometer, FFT analyzer |
| **Thermography (Infrared)** | Surface temperature distribution | Infrared thermal camera |
| **Oil / Lubricant Analysis** | Particle count, viscosity, wear debris | Spectrometer, ferrography |
| **Ultrasonic Testing** | High-frequency acoustic emissions | Ultrasound probe |
| **Motor Current Signature** | Current harmonics | Current transformer + analyzer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PdM Works — The Physical Principle:**
> Every rotating or reciprocating machine emits characteristic **vibration signatures** under healthy conditions. When a fault develops (imbalance, misalignment, bearing defect, gear wear), these signatures change in **predictable ways** — specific frequencies, harmonics, and sidebands emerge *long before* the fault becomes catastrophic.
> PdM exploits this **early-warning window** to intervene proactively.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Maintenance Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are universally tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize with units.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$ | Steady-state Availability (fraction of time the system works) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $$MTBF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 3 | $$MTTR = \dfrac{\sum \text{Repair (Down) Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| 4 | $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ | Reliability (exponential distribution) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$$ | Failure rate (constant-hazard region of Bathtub Curve) | failures/hour |
| 6 | $$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$$ | Maintainability function | dimensionless |
| 7 | $$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 8 | $$\text{MDT} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$$ | Mean Downtime | hours |
| 9 | $$\text{Cost}_{\text{optimal}} = \dfrac{d(C_{PM} + C_{CM})}{dT_{PM}} = 0$$ | Optimal PM interval (derivative condition) | currency |
| 10 | $$\text{PM Benefit} = \dfrac{C_{\text{breakdown}} - C_{PM}}{C_{PM}}$$ | Cost-benefit ratio for PM justification | dimensionless |

Where:
- $A$ = Availability
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $R(t)$ = Reliability at time $t$
- $\lambda$ = Failure rate
- $\mu$ = Repair rate
- $OEE$ = Overall Equipment Effectiveness
- $A, P, Q$ = Availability, Performance, Quality factors
- $C_{PM}$ = Cost of Preventive Maintenance per cycle
- $C_{CM}$ = Cost of Corrective (Breakdown) Maintenance
- $T_{PM}$ = PM interval (cycle time)

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Rule:** The vertical bar symbol `\vert` (NOT the markdown pipe `\vert`-breaker) is used in the formulas above. In your own notes, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values — never the raw `|` inside a markdown table cell, as it will break rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are the **universal vocabulary** of maintenance engineers worldwide. Specifically:

- **Formula 1 (Availability)** is the **single most important metric** tracked in any plant. Automobile manufacturing lines typically target $A \geq 0.95$ (95%).
- **Formula 4 (Exponential Reliability)** applies during the **useful life** region of the Bathtub Curve (constant hazard).
- **Formula 9 (Optimal PM Interval)** is the foundation of **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** scheduling — it tells you *exactly how often* to service to minimize total lifetime cost.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Availability Formula

> **Goal:** Prove the famous relation
> $$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

#### Step 1 — Define the observation window

Consider a machine observed over a long time horizon $T_{total}$. During this horizon, the machine alternates between **Operating** and **Down (Repair)** states.

Let:
- Total operating time = $T_{op}$
- Total down time = $T_{down}$
- Number of failures = $N$

#### Step 2 — Express MTBF and MTTR using $N$

By definition:
$$MTBF = \dfrac{T_{op}}{N} \quad \text{and} \quad MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N}$$

#### Step 3 — Express availability as time fraction

$$A = \dfrac{\text{Time system is UP}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{T_{op}}{T_{op} + T_{down}}$$

#### Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{1}{N}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{T_{op}/N}{T_{op}/N + T_{down}/N} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Interpretation

The numerator represents the "good" time, the denominator represents the "good + bad" time. This ratio tells the plant manager the **fraction of calendar time** that the equipment is actually productive.

> **Engineering Insight:** If a CNC machine in an aerospace plant has $MTBF = 900$ hours and $MTTR = 9$ hours, then $A = 909/900 \text{ wait, recalculated} = 900/(900+9) = 0.990 = 99.0\%$ — excellent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Preventive Maintenance Interval

> **Goal:** Find the PM cycle $T_{PM}^*$ that minimizes **total expected maintenance cost per unit time**.

#### Step 1 — Define the two competing cost components

Let:
- $C_{PM}$ = cost of one scheduled PM activity (labor + spares + lost production during PM)
- $C_{CM}$ = cost of one corrective (breakdown) maintenance event (always $C_{CM} \gg C_{PM}$)
- $T_{PM}$ = PM interval (years, months, hours)
- $\lambda$ = failure rate of the equipment (failures per unit time)

#### Step 2 — Expected number of failures per PM cycle

We assume that the PM activity **resets the equipment to "as-good-as-new"** condition (the standard "**renewal-theory assumption**" used in reliability engineering).

The expected number of failures during one PM cycle of length $T_{PM}$ is:
$$N_f = \lambda \cdot T_{PM}$$

(Valid when $\lambda T_{PM} \ll 1$, i.e., the cycle is short relative to MTBF.)

#### Step 3 — Total expected cost per PM cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{cycle}} &= \text{PM cost} + \text{Expected breakdown cost} \\[4pt]
                 &= C_{PM} + N_f \cdot C_{CM} \\[4pt]
                 &= C_{PM} + \lambda \, T_{PM} \, C_{CM}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Cost per unit time (annualized)

The cycle length is $T_{PM}$, so the **cost per unit time** is:
$$
C_{\text{rate}}(T_{PM}) = \dfrac{C_{PM}}{T_{PM}} + \lambda \, C_{CM}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The second term $\lambda C_{CM}$ is **independent** of $T_{PM}$. Only the first term — the *PM scheduling cost rate* — decreases as $T_{PM}$ grows.

#### Step 5 — Minimize by setting derivative to zero

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC_{\text{rate}}}{dT_{PM}} &= -\dfrac{C_{PM}}{T_{PM}^{\,2}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This first-order condition has **no finite solution** — meaning the model predicts infinite $T_{PM}$. This is the classic limitation of the simple model. We introduce a **breakdown cost that grows with $T_{PM}$** (machines that run longer between PMs suffer more catastrophic failures):

$$
C_{\text{rate}}(T_{PM}) = \dfrac{C_{PM}}{T_{PM}} + \lambda \, C_{CM} + k \cdot T_{PM}
$$

where $k$ is a penalty factor representing degradation accumulation. Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC_{\text{rate}}}{dT_{PM}} &= -\dfrac{C_{PM}}{T_{PM}^{\,2}} + k = 0 \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad T_{PM}^{\,*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{PM}}{k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

$$
T_{PM}^{\,*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{PM}}{k}}
$$

> The optimal PM interval is the **geometric mean** of PM cost and the degradation penalty rate. Doubling PM cost increases $T_{PM}^*$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bathtub Curve & Reliability $R(t)$

> **Goal:** Show why the simple exponential reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies **only** in the middle (useful life) region.

#### Step 1 — General reliability definition

$R(t)$ = probability that an item survives from time 0 to time $t$ without failure.

#### Step 2 — For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (memoryless process)

In a small interval $dt$, the probability of failure is $\lambda \, dt$. The probability of survival is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t + dt) &= R(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \, dt) \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dR}{dt} &= -\lambda \, R(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Solve the first-order ODE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{R(t)} \dfrac{dR}{R} &= -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt \\[4pt]
\ln R(t) &= -\lambda t \\[4pt]
R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **exponential reliability function**, valid only when $\lambda$ is constant — which holds in the **useful life region** of the Bathtub Curve, between the infant-mortality burn-in phase and the wear-out phase.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PM vs. PdM

| Dimension | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Calendar / usage hours | Measured condition threshold |
| **Failure model assumed** | Known, periodic wear-out | Random / progressive degradation |
| **Cost per intervention** | Low to moderate | Moderate (but fewer interventions) |
| **Downtime frequency** | Higher (scheduled stops) | Lower (only when needed) |
| **Initial setup cost** | Low | High (sensors + software + training) |
| **Spare-part inventory** | High (everything stocked in advance) | Optimized (just-in-time) |
| **Skill requirement** | Routine technician | Data analyst / vibration specialist |
| **Best suited for** | Non-critical assets, simple machines | Critical, expensive, high-speed rotating equipment |
| **Failure detection lead time** | None (failure already scheduled) | Weeks to months in advance |
| **KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) alignment** | CO 2 — Apply PM planning | CO 3 — Apply condition-monitoring techniques |
