```mermaid
    f5["Multiple End-State Visualization"]
    f6["Initiating Event Driven"]
    
    feature --> f1
    feature --> f2
    feature --> f3
    feature --> f4
    feature --> f5
    feature --> f6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Event Tree Analysis (ETA). List any four salient features of ETA.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a forward-looking, inductive safety analysis technique that begins with an **initiating event** and systematically traces the success or failure of subsequent safety functions to determine all possible end-state outcomes and their probabilities.
>
> **Four Salient Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Forward/Inductive Logic:** Analysis proceeds from cause (initiating event) to consequence (end-state).
> 2. **Binary Branching:** Each safety function is evaluated as either successful (S) or failed (F).
> 3. **Time-Sequenced:** Safety functions are arranged in the order they are expected to respond.
> 4. **Quantitative Output:** Provides numerical probability and frequency values for each end-state, enabling risk-based decisions.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the merits of Event Tree Analysis in maintenance engineering decision-making.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
> 1. **Visual Clarity:** Easy-to-understand graphical representation accessible to engineers and management. **(1 Mark)**
> 2. **Identifies Critical Safety Functions:** Highlights which safety barriers demand prioritized maintenance based on failure probability. **(1 Mark)**
> 3. **Quantifies Risk:** Provides frequencies of accident sequences, supporting risk-informed maintenance strategies such as RCM and condition-based monitoring. **(1 Mark)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **features and merits** of Event Tree Analysis (ETA) in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reactor cooling system has an initiating event (loss of coolant) with frequency $1.2 \times 10^{-2}$/yr. Two independent safety functions respond in sequence:
- Safety Function 1 (Emergency Core Cooling): $P_1 = 0.98$
- Safety Function 2 (Containment Spray): $P_2 = 0.995$

Construct the event tree and compute the probability and frequency of the catastrophic end-state (both safety functions fail). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

> **Features of ETA (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Inductive/Forward Analysis:** Begins with an initiating event and progresses forward through the accident sequence. *(1 Mark)*
> 2. **Branching Logic:** Each safety function introduces binary (or multi-state) branches representing success/failure. *(1 Mark)*
> 3. **Sequential Representation:** Safety functions are placed in the order they are expected to operate, reflecting real-time accident progression. *(1 Mark)*
> 4. **Accident Sequence Identification:** Each unique path through the tree represents a distinct accident sequence, leading to defined end-states. *(1 Mark)*
>
> **Merits of ETA (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Simplicity:** Graphical and intuitive; easy to communicate to non-specialists. *(1 Mark)*
> 2. **Quantitative Power:** Provides both qualitative (sequence) and quantitative (probability/frequency) outputs. *(1 Mark)*
> 3. **Maintenance Decision Support:** Identifies high-failure-probability safety functions for prioritized maintenance, inspection, and testing. *(1 Mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Identify branch failure probabilities:** *(1 Mark)*

$$Q_1 = 1 - P_1 = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02$$

$$Q_2 = 1 - P_2 = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Event Tree (conceptual description):** *(2 Marks)*

The tree has 4 end-states:
- S-S: Safe Shutdown
- S-F: Minor Incident
- F-S: Controlled Failure
- F-F: Catastrophe

**Step 3 — Compute the probability of the catastrophic path (F-F):** *(2 Marks)*

$$P_{\text{F-F}} = Q_1 \times Q_2 = 0.02 \times 0.005$$

$$P_{\text{F-F}} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the frequency of the catastrophic end-state:** *(2 Marks)*

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{F-F}}$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = (1.2 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ events/year}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **methodology of constructing an Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** for a chemical reactor overpressure scenario involving three safety functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the system described in part (a), the following data is given:

| Safety Function | Failure Rate $\lambda$ (per year) | Mission Time $t$ (yr) |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| SF1: Pressure Relief Valve | 0.005 | 1 |
| SF2: Emergency Cooling | 0.02 | 1 |
| SF3: Containment | 0.002 | 1 |

If the initiating event frequency is $5 \times 10^{-3}$/yr, compute the **frequency of all 8 end-states**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Identify the initiating event:** *(1 Mark)*
Overpressure in chemical reactor (due to blocked outlet, runaway reaction, etc.).

**Step 2 — Identify three safety functions in sequence:** *(1 Mark)*
1. Pressure Relief Valve
2. Emergency Cooling System
3. Containment Structure

**Step 3 — Construct event tree:** *(3 Marks)*

> Initiating Event → SF1 (S/F) → SF2 (S/F) → SF3 (S/F) → 8 End-States

Each safety function branches into Success (S, upward) or Failure (F, downward), producing $2^3 = 8$ accident sequences:
1. S-S-S → Safe Shutdown
2. S-S-F → Minor Release
3. S-F-S → Controlled Incident
4. S-F-F → Local Damage
5. F-S-S → Late Recovery
6. F-S-F → Major Release
7. F-F-S → Significant Incident
8. F-F-F → **Catastrophic Failure**

**Step 4 — Assign probabilities and compute results:** *(2 Marks)*
Each branch probability is determined from the component failure rate data.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Compute reliabilities (success probabilities):** *(2 Marks)*

$$P_1 = e^{-\lambda_1 t} = e^{-0.005} = 0.99501$$

$$Q_1 = 1 - P_1 = 0.00499$$

$$P_2 = e^{-\lambda_2 t} = e^{-0.02} = 0.98020$$

$$Q_2 = 1 - P_2 = 0.01980$$

$$P_3 = e^{-\lambda_3 t} = e^{-0.002} = 0.99800$$

$$Q_3 = 1 - P_3 = 0.00200$$

**Step 2 — Compute all 8 path probabilities:** *(2 Marks)*

| Path | Probability Calculation | Value |
|------|------------------------|-------|
| S-S-S | $0.99501 \times 0.98020 \times 0.99800$ | $0.97332$ |
| S-S-F | $0.99501 \times 0.98020 \times 0.00200$ | $1.951 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S-F-S | $0.99501 \times 0.01980 \times 0.99800$ | $1.966 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| S-F-F | $0.99501 \times 0.01980 \times 0.00200$ | $3.940 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-S-S | $0.00499 \times 0.98020 \times 0.99800$ | $4.881 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| F-S-F | $0.00499 \times 0.98020 \times 0.00200$ | $9.782 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| F-F-S | $0.00499 \times 0.01980 \times 0.99800$ | $9.860 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-F-F | $0.00499 \times 0.01980 \times 0.00200$ | $1.976 \times 10^{-7}$ |

**Step 3 — Multiply each path by $f_{\text{IE}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ to get frequencies:** *(3 Marks)*

| Path | Frequency (events/year) |
|------|------------------------|
| S-S-S | $4.8666 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S-S-F | $9.755 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S-F-S | $9.830 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| S-F-F | $1.970 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| F-S-S | $2.441 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-S-F | $4.891 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| F-F-S | $4.930 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| F-F-F | $9.880 \times 10^{-10}$ |

> **Conclusion:** The "Catastrophic Failure" end-state (F-F-F) occurs at $9.88 \times 10^{-10}$ events/year — an exceptionally rare outcome, validating the safety system design.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ETA with FTA:** Students often mix up Event Tree Analysis (forward) with Fault Tree Analysis (backward, AND/OR gates). State explicitly: ETA is **forward + inductive + binary branches**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Conservation Rule:** $\sum P_{\text{path}} = 1$. If your 8 paths don't sum to 1, you have an arithmetic error.
>
> 3. **Using Failure Rate as Probability:** $\lambda$ (per year) is **not** a probability. You must convert via $P_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ or $P_i = 1 - \lambda_i t$ for small $\lambda t$.
>
> 4. **Not Labelling Branches:** Each branch must clearly show **Success (S)** or **Failure (F)** with the corresponding probability. Examiners deduct marks for unmarked branches.
>
> 5. **Ignoring End-State Frequencies:** Computing path probabilities alone is incomplete. Always multiply by $f_{\text{IE}}$ to get the **frequency** — KTU examiners specifically ask for this.
>
> 6. **Mixing Units:** $\lambda$ is per year, $t$ is in years, $f_{\text{IE}}$ is events/year, $F$ is events/year. Keep units consistent or lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ETA Features & Merits**
>
> ✅ ETA = **Forward + Inductive + Binary Branching + Quantitative**
>
> ✅ **Initiating Event** is the starting point of every ETA
>
> ✅ **Safety Functions** are arranged in **chronological/temporal sequence**
>
> ✅ Each branch is **Success (S)** with probability $P_i$ or **Failure (F)** with probability $Q_i = 1 - P_i$
>
> ✅ Number of end-states = $2^n$ where $n$ = number of safety functions
>
> ✅ Path Probability: $P_{\text{path}} = \prod P_i$ (or $Q_i$ as appropriate)
>
> ✅ End-State Frequency: $F_{\text{end-state}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{path}}$
>
> ✅ Conservation Rule: $\sum P_{\text{path}} = 1$
>
> ✅ **Merits:** Visual, intuitive, quantitative, identifies critical safety functions, supports RCM and risk-based maintenance
>
> ✅ **Contrast with FTA:** ETA = forward from cause; FTA = backward from top event using logic gates
>
> ✅ **Reliability Function:** $P_i = R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ (constant failure rate assumption)
>
> ✅ **Total Risk:** $\text{Risk} = \sum F_{\text{end-state},k} \times C_k$ (frequency × consequence)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Procedure, Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Merits and Procedure, Root Cause Analysis (RCA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Root Cause Analysis (RCA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** is defined as a **structured, systematic, and evidence-based investigative methodology** used in maintenance engineering to identify the *fundamental underlying cause(s)* of a failure, defect, or non-conformance event, by going beyond the visible symptoms until the deepest controllable causal factor is uncovered.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEIET613 syllabus**, RCA is positioned as the *cornerstone analytical tool* of modern proactive maintenance strategy (RCM, TPM, and Reliability-Centered Maintenance). It shifts the engineer's mindset from **reactive fire-fighting ("why did this break?")** to **proactive prevention ("how do we stop it from ever breaking again?")**.

The most cited industry definition is by **Norm Ohmae** and **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota), which states:
> *"A root cause is a fundamental causal factor that, if removed, would prevent the recurrence of an undesirable event."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective and the Dominoes"

Imagine you walk into your kitchen and find water all over the floor. You mop it up and walk away. Next day, water again. You mop again. **This is NOT Root Cause Analysis — this is symptomatic repair.**

Now imagine you play *detective*:

1. You notice the floor is wet.
2. You look up — the ceiling has a stain.
3. You go to the floor above — a pipe joint is dripping.
4. You investigate why the joint leaks — the rubber washer is 8 years old and brittle.
5. **ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED:** Aged washer + no preventive replacement schedule.

This is **RCA**. In a manufacturing plant:

- **Symptom:** A pump bearing seizes.
- **Apparent cause:** Bearing failed (anyone can see this).
- **Root cause:** Lubrication schedule was skipped for 11 weeks because the auto-greaser was misaligned during a 2022 overhaul.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Highly important for KTU exams):**
> - **Symptom / Apparent Cause:** What you *see* at the failure site.
> - **Root Cause:** The *deepest controllable factor* in the cause-and-effect chain whose elimination prevents recurrence.

### 1.3 Why RCA Matters in Maintenance Engineering (Industry Context)

In a typical process plant, the **5-25 rule** (often cited in reliability literature) states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For every **1 dollar** spent on **preventive action (RCA-driven fix)**, **5 dollars** are saved on **direct repair**, and **25 dollars** are saved on **consequential losses** (production downtime, secondary damage, safety incidents, warranty claims).

### 1.4 Merits of RCA — The Strategic Payoff Matrix

| Strategic Domain | Specific Merit |
|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Reduces Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) for recurring faults |
| **Availability** | Increases Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) |
| **Safety** | Prevents catastrophic accidents (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City) |
| **Cost** | Lowers Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of assets |
| **Knowledge** | Builds organizational learning and corporate memory |
| **Compliance** | Satisfies ISO 9001, ISO 14001, OSHA PSM, and NABL audit requirements |
| **Culture** | Drives a *no-blame*, learning-oriented maintenance culture |

### 1.5 Core Terminology Block (Exam Favorites)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Event:** The undesirable incident itself (e.g., motor trip on 14-Mar-2025).
> - **Causal Factor:** Any contributor that influenced the event.
> - **Root Cause:** The deepest factor whose removal prevents recurrence.
> - **Contributing Cause:** A factor that worsened severity but did not initiate.
> - **Corrective Action:** Immediate action to restore function.
> - **Preventive Action:** Long-term action to eliminate the root cause.
> - **5-Whys:** A linear questioning technique (Sakichi Toyoda, Toyota).
> - **Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram:** A cause-category mapping tool (Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy of RCA

RCA rests on three philosophical pillars which must be understood before applying any technique:

1. **Causal Chain Hypothesis:** Failures are not random; they are the *final link* in a chain of contributing causes. Find the weakest link and reinforce it.
2. **Systemic Causation (NOT Human Error):** Heinrich's Domino Theory and Reason's Swiss Cheese Model both teach us that operators are usually the *last* domino to fall, not the first. Modern RCA explicitly avoids "human error" as a root cause.
3. **Evidence over Opinion:** Every causal link in RCA must be supported by physical evidence, data logs, or corroborated witness statements.

### 2.2 The Standard KTU RCA Procedure — Stepwise Logical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus expects students to memorize the **6-step RCA procedure**. This is a recurring 7 to 14 mark question in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

**Step 1 — Problem Definition & Boundary Setting**
- Define the problem statement precisely: *What happened? When? Where? To what extent?*
- Use the **SMART** format: Specific, Measurable, Actionable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **Example:** *"On 14-Mar-2025 at 02:18 hrs, Boiler Feed Pump P-101B tripped on bearing high-temperature, resulting in 4.2 hours of lost production."*

**Step 2 — Data Collection & Evidence Preservation**
- Collect SCADA / DCS historian logs, vibration spectra, oil analysis reports, operator logs, maintenance work orders, photographs, video, and operator interviews.
- The **golden rule**: *Preserve the scene before cleaning it up.*

**Step 3 — Causal Factor Identification (Mapping Phase)**
- Brainstorm all possible causes without judgment.
- Group them using either the **6M Fishbone** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature) or the **4M** framework (Man, Machine, Material, Method).

**Step 4 — Root Cause Identification (Drilling Phase)**
- Apply linear questioning (**5 Whys**) for simple single-fault events.
- Apply **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** for complex multi-path failures.
- Apply **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** for design-level issues.

**Step 5 — Recommendation of Corrective & Preventive Actions (CAPA)**
- Each root cause must have **at least one preventive action**.
- Actions must be *SMART* and assigned to a named owner with a target date.

**Step 6 — Verification, Documentation & Closure**
- Monitor post-implementation KPIs (MTBF, failure count, vibration levels).
- Close the RCA only when data confirms the root cause has been eliminated.
- File the report in the **Maintenance Knowledge Base** for future reference.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — RCA Quantitative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Although RCA is primarily qualitative, several quantitative metrics support it. **All units must be remembered** for the KTU exam.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent / Intrinsic Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Pareto 80/20** | Cumulative \% = $\dfrac{\sum \text{Frequency}_\text{class}}{\text{Total Frequency}} \times 100$ | Helps prioritize top RCA targets | % |
| **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Used in reliability block diagrams | failures / hour |
| **RPN (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | score (1 to 1000) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Table Rule Applied:** All absolute values (e.g., $|x|$, $|S - O|$) inside any table row are typeset using `\vert` to avoid breaking the KTU markdown table syntax. Example: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\vert \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} \vert}$

### 2.4 The Major RCA Techniques — Comparative Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.4.1 The **5 Whys Analysis** (Linear / Single-Fault Technique)

- **Origin:** Sakichi Toyoda, Toyota Production System, 1930s.
- **Mechanism:** Ask "Why?" iteratively, typically 5 times, until the chain reaches a *controllable* root cause.
- **Best for:** Simple, single-chain, human-procedure-related failures.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle multi-path failures; risk of confirmation bias.

#### 2.4.2 The **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** (Categorical Technique)

- **Origin:** Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968, University of Tokyo.
- **Mechanism:** Map potential causes under 6 (or 4) categorical "bones."
- **Standard 6M Categories:** Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Best for:** Group brainstorming; complex events with multiple contributory categories.

#### 2.4.3 **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** (Top-Down Deductive Technique)

- **Origin:** Bell Telephone Labs, 1961 (Minuteman Missile Program); formalized by NASA.
- **Mechanism:** Starts from the undesired TOP EVENT and uses Boolean **AND / OR gates** to decompose into basic events.
- **Best for:** Complex safety-critical systems (nuclear, aerospace, chemical).
- **Symbolic Logic:** Use of standard Boolean algebra.

#### 2.4.4 **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** (Bottom-Up Inductive Technique)

- **Origin:** U.S. Military Procedure MIL-P-1629, 1949; later expanded into automotive (AIAG-VDA, 2019).
- **Mechanism:** Lists every component, its failure modes, effects, and **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

#### 2.4.5 **Pareto Analysis** (Prioritization Technique)

- **Origin:** Vilfredo Pareto, Italian economist (1896); popularized by Joseph Juran (1950s).
- **Mechanism:** 80/20 rule — *80% of failures come from 20% of causes.*
- **Usage:** Decides *which* failure to RCA first.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of RCA

- **Power Plants:** Identifies root causes of boiler tube leaks, turbine blade cracks.
- **Aerospace:** Mandatory under FAA / EASA after any Service Difficulty Report.
- **Automotive:** Every recall begins with an RCA of field failure data.
- **Pharmaceutical & Medical Devices:** Mandated by **FDA 21 CFR 820.100** and **EU MDR**.
- **Process Industries:** Required under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 — Process Safety Management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reality Check:** In KTU ESE papers, the 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the merits and procedure of RCA with suitable maintenance examples."* Students must write **at least 4 merits** + **all 6 procedural steps** + **at least 2 techniques** to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — 5 Whys Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Industrial Example:** *On 18-Apr-2025, Hydraulic Press HP-204 in the forging shop suffered a sudden pressure drop and the operator had to emergency-stop the line.*

**Why 1:** *Why did the press lose pressure?*
> **A:** Because the hydraulic fluid leaked from the main ram cylinder.

**Why 2:** *Why did the cylinder leak?*
> **A:** Because the main piston seal (part no. SE-4408) was ruptured.

**Why 3:** *Why was the seal ruptured?*
> **A:** Because hydraulic oil temperature had been exceeding 95 °C for over 3 weeks, exceeding the seal's rated 80 °C limit.

**Why 4:** *Why was the oil temperature so high?*
> **A:** Because the oil cooler fan was not operating — its V-belt had snapped.

**Why 5:** *Why had the V-belt snapped?*
> **A:** Because the belt had reached end-of-life (rated 6,000 hrs) but was not replaced — there was no condition-based replacement schedule for it.

**ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED:**
> The absence of a **condition-based V-belt replacement schedule** in the preventive maintenance plan.

**CORRECTIVE ACTION:** Replace belt, repair seal, refill oil.
**PREVENTIVE ACTION:** Add V-belt to the SAP PM plan with a 6,000-hour trigger; install oil temperature alarm at 85 °C.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Fishbone (Ishikawa) Construction

For the *same HP-204 press failure*, we construct a Fishbone Diagram with the **6M branches**:

| Branch (6M) | Probable Causal Statements |
|---|---|
| **MAN (People)** | Operator not trained on temperature alarm response; shift handover log missing. |
| **MACHINE (Equipment)** | V-belt aging; cooler fan motor vibration high; thermistor sensor drifted out of calibration. |
| **MATERIAL** | Hydraulic oil viscosity out of spec (ISO VG 46, but found VG 68); counterfeit seal batch. |
| **METHOD (Process)** | PM schedule does not include V-belt; SOP missing for oil temp excursion. |
| **MEASUREMENT** | Local gauge faulty; no DCS trending on cooler fan amperage. |
| **MOTHER NATURE (Environment)** | Ambient shop temperature 38 °C in summer; dusty environment clogging cooler fins. |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Boolean Logic of Fault Tree Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** Students are often asked to construct a small Fault Tree and compute its top-event probability.

Let the top event be **T = Pump P-101 fails to deliver flow**. The failure logic is:

- **Event A:** Motor fails to start.
- **Event B:** Coupling shears.
- **Event C:** Pump impeller blocks.
- **Event D:** Suction line chokes.

Assume the **TOP event T** occurs if **any one** of A, B, C, D occurs (i.e., **OR gate** logic). The Boolean expression is:

$$
T = A \cup B \cup C \cup D
$$

For independent basic events, the top-event probability is derived as:

$$
P(T) = 1 - P(\bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{B}) \cdot P(\bar{C}) \cdot P(\bar{D})
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(A \cup B \cup C \cup D) \\
&= 1 - P(\overline{A \cup B \cup C \cup D}) \quad \text{(Complement rule)} \\
&= 1 - P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B} \cap \bar{C} \cap \bar{D}) \quad \text{(De Morgan's Law)} \\
&= 1 - P(\bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{B}) \cdot P(\bar{C}) \cdot P(\bar{D}) \quad \text{(Independence assumption)} \\
&= 1 - (1 - p_A)(1 - p_B)(1 - p_C)(1 - p_D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**
Suppose $p_A = 0.01$, $p_B = 0.02$, $p_C = 0.015$, $p_D = 0.025$ (one-year failure probabilities).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.015)(1 - 0.025) \\
&= 1 - (0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.985 \times 0.975) \\
&= 1 - (0.9323) \\
&\approx 0.0677 \text{ or } 6.77\% \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Maintenance Manager:** Approximately 7 times in 100 years this pump system will fail. If unacceptably high, the manager must RANK-order the basic events by **Birnbaum Importance** and target the highest contributor for an RCA-driven redesign.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN)

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where each factor is rated on a 1–10 scale:

| Factor | Meaning | 1 (Best) | 10 (Worst) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** (Severity) | Impact of failure on customer/safety | No effect | Life-threatening |
| **O** (Occurrence) | Likelihood of failure occurring | Very rare | Very frequent |
| **D** (Detection) | Probability problem escapes detection | Easily detected | Cannot be detected |

**Worked Example — Motor Bearing Failure Mode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 8 \quad \text{(Bearing seizure causes line stop)} \\
O &= 5 \quad \text{(Happens ~ once per 8 months)} \\
D &= 4 \quad \text{(Detected by vibration sensor)} \\
\text{RPN} &= 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Convention (Industry Standard):** Any RPN greater than **150** is flagged for mandatory RCA and corrective action planning. RPN 160 → triggers an RCA.

### 3.5 Pareto Analysis — Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation

Suppose a maintenance logbook records **120 failures** in the last quarter, classified as follows:

| Failure Type | Count | % of Total | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bearing failures | 48 | 40.0% | 40.0% |
| Seal leaks | 30 | 25.0% | 65.0% |
| Sensor drift | 18 | 15.0% | 80.0% |
| Coupling wear | 12 | 10.0% | 90.0% |
| Electrical trip | 8 | 6.7% | 96.7% |
| Misc. | 4 | 3.3% | 100.0% |

**Interpretation:**
- Bearings + Seals = **65%** of all failures → target these first via RCA.
- Applying the **80/20 rule**, the first three categories (**80%**) are the strategic RCA targets.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which RCA Technique

| Scenario / Trigger | Recommended Primary Tool | Supporting Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Single human-procedure slip | 5 Whys | Brainstorming |
| Multi-category workshop failure | Fishbone (Ishikawa) | 5 Whys |
| Safety / regulatory investigation | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | FMEA |
| Design review of new equipment | FMEA | FTA |
| Portfolio prioritization | Pareto Analysis | FMEA |
| Repetitive recurring failure | 5 Whys + Fishbone | Pareto |
| Catastrophic accident | Swiss Cheese Model + FTA | Bow-Tie Analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RCA — Master Procedure Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Event: Equipment Failure or Incident] --> B[Step 1: Define the Problem SMART Statement]
    B --> C[Step 2: Collect Data and Preserve Evidence]
    C --> D[Step 3: Identify Causal Factors via Brainstorming]
    D --> E[Step 4: Select RCA Technique]
    E --> F{Technique Selection}
    F -->|Single chain| G[Apply 5 Whys Analysis]
    F -->|Multi-category| H[Apply Fishbone Ishikawa 6M]
    F -->|Safety critical| I[Apply Fault Tree Analysis]
    F -->|Design review| J[Apply FMEA RPN]
    G --> K[Step 5: Identify Root Cause]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Step 6: Recommend CAPA Actions]
    L --> M[Step 7: Implement and Verify Effectiveness]
    M --> N{Verification KPI Improved?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Close RCA and File in Knowledge Base]
    N -->|No| P[Reopen and Drill Deeper]
    P --> E
    O --> Q[Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

### 4.2 5 Whys — Linear Chain Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Why 1: Apparent Symptom] --> W2[Why 2: Immediate Cause]
    W2 --> W3[Why 3: Underlying Cause]
    W3 --> W4[Why 4: Systemic Cause]
    W4 --> W5[Why 5: Root Cause Found]
    W5 --> W6{Controllable Factor?}
    W6 -->|Yes| A1[Define Preventive Action]
    W6 -->|No| W5
    A1 --> A2[Monitor KPI and Close]
```

### 4.3 Fishbone (Ishikawa) — 6M Category Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPINE[Problem Statement: Equipment Failure] --> M1
    SPINE --> M2
    SPINE --> M3
    SPINE --> M4
    SPINE --> M5
    SPINE --> M6
    M1[MAN: People Skills Training Fatigue]
    M2[MACHINE: Equipment Wear Aging Calibration]
    M3[MATERIAL: Quality Specification Storage]
    M4[METHOD: Procedure SOP PM Plan]
    M5[MEASUREMENT: Sensor Gauge Audit]
    M6[MOTHER NATURE: Temperature Humidity Dust]
```

### 4.4 Fault Tree — AND OR Gate Decomposition Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[TOP EVENT T: Pump P101 Fails]
    TOP --> OR1
    OR1 --> A[Event A: Motor Fails]
    OR1 --> B[Event B: Coupling Shears]
    OR1 --> C[Event C: Impeller Blocks]
    OR1 --> D[Event D: Suction Line Chokes]
    A --> A1[Basic: Winding short]
    A --> A2[Basic: Contactor worn]
    A --> A3[Basic: Overload trips]
    B --> B1[Basic: Misalignment]
    B --> B2[Basic: Material fatigue]
    C --> C1[Basic: Foreign object]
    C --> C2[Basic: Cavitation damage]
    D --> D1[Basic: Strainer clogged]
    D --> D2[Basic: Valve closed]
```

### 4.5 Pareto 80 by 20 Prioritization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Maintenance Failure Log: 120 Events] --> SORT[Sort Failures by Frequency Descending]
    SORT --> CALC[Calculate Cumulative Percentage]
    CALC --> PLOT[Plot Pareto Chart]
    PLOT --> DECIDE{Cumulative crosses 80 percent line}
    DECIDE -->|Yes| PRIORITY[RCA Target Selected: Vital Few]
    DECIDE -->|No| RECHECK[Continue Plotting Categories]
    PRIORITY --> ASSIGN[Assign RCA Team and Timeline]
```

### 4.6 Swiss Cheese Model — Defence-in-Depth Architecture (Reason Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HAZ[Hazards and Latent Failures] --> L1
    L1[Slice 1: Organisational Processes] --> GAP1
    GAP1 --> L2[Slice 2: Supervision]
    L2 --> GAP2
    GAP2 --> L3[Slice 3: Preconditions Unsafe Acts]
    L3 --> GAP3
    GAP3 --> L4[Slice 4: Outcome Incident]
    GAP1 -.-> FAIL[When holes align: Incident Occurs]
    GAP2 -.-> FAIL
    GAP3 -.-> FAIL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define Root Cause Analysis (RCA). List any four merits of RCA in maintenance engineering.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
> Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is a structured, systematic investigative methodology used to identify the fundamental underlying cause(s) of an equipment failure or process deviation, such that their elimination prevents recurrence.

**Merits (2 marks — any four of the following, ½ mark each):**
1. Prevents recurrence of the same failure → improves **MTBF**.
2. Reduces unplanned downtime → improves **Availability** and **OEE**.
3. Enhances safety by uncovering latent hazards.
4. Lowers **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** of assets.
5. Captures organizational knowledge and supports compliance with **ISO 9001 / OSHA PSM**.
6. Drives a proactive, **no-blame maintenance culture**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Differentiate between **Corrective Action** and **Preventive Action** with respect to RCA.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Corrective Action | Preventive Action |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Restores the failed equipment to working condition | Eliminates the root cause to prevent recurrence |
| Time horizon | Immediate / short-term | Long-term |
| Triggered by | The failure event itself | The RCA findings |
| Example | Replacing a ruptured seal | Adding the seal to a condition-based PM plan |
| Marks (KTU distribution) | 1.5 | 1.5 |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **merits of Root Cause Analysis** in the context of a modern maintenance management system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete step-by-step procedure of RCA** with a suitable industrial example of a centrifugal pump failure. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **merits of RCA** in modern maintenance management can be classified under five strategic pillars:

**1. Improved Equipment Reliability and Availability (2 marks)**
RCA systematically eliminates the deepest cause of failure. Once eliminated, the same fault cannot reappear, which directly increases **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**. Higher MTBF combined with maintained **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** raises the inherent availability $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.

**2. Reduction in Unplanned Downtime and Production Loss (1 mark)**
Each unplanned stoppage typically costs 5 to 50 times the direct repair cost. RCA-driven preventive actions reduce the *frequency* of stops, raising **OEE**.

**3. Enhanced Safety and Regulatory Compliance (1 mark)**
Many catastrophic accidents (Texas City 2005, BP Deepwater Horizon 2010) had root causes that earlier RCA investigations could have surfaced. Standards like **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** and **ISO 45001** mandate RCA after incidents.

**4. Lower Life Cycle Cost (LCC) (1 mark)**
Although RCA consumes 8–16 hours of cross-functional effort per event, the *5-25 rule* shows it saves many multiples in consequential losses.

**5. Organizational Learning and Knowledge Retention (1 mark)**
Each closed RCA becomes a documented lesson in the **Maintenance Knowledge Base / CMMS**. New engineers learn from past failures rather than repeating them.

**6. Strengthens Continuous Improvement Culture (1 mark)**
RCA institutionalizes a **PDCA cycle**: Plan (investigate), Do (implement), Check (verify KPIs), Act (standardize).

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Industrial Example:** *On 22-Jun-2025, Centrifugal Pump P-204 in a cooling water circuit suffered a catastrophic mechanical seal failure, causing 6 hours of lost production and a chemical spill of 800 L of glycol.*

**Step 1 — Problem Definition (1 mark):**
SMART statement: *"On 22-Jun-2025 at 11:42 hrs, Pump P-204 (30 kW, 1450 rpm) suffered mechanical seal failure, leading to 6 hours downtime and 800 L glycol spill. Estimated loss ₹ 4.2 lakhs."*

**Step 2 — Data Collection (1 mark):**
- DCS historian showed seal chamber temperature rising from 55 °C to 92 °C over 21 days.
- Last PM was on 12-Dec-2024 (6 months ago).
- Operator log showed dry-running alarm on 19-Jun-2025 but no action taken.

**Step 3 — Causal Factor Identification using Fishbone (2 marks):**
- **Man:** Operator unaware of dry-running alarm response SOP.
- **Machine:** Mechanical seal end-of-life; cooler fan V-belt loose.
- **Material:** Glycol contained trace water → vaporized at seal face.
- **Method:** No vibration monitoring in PM plan.
- **Measurement:** Temperature gauge calibration overdue by 14 months.
- **Mother Nature:** Summer ambient 41 °C.

**Step 4 — Root Cause Identification using 5 Whys (1 mark):**
> Dry running → seal face overheating → no flow switch interlock → seal flush plan not installed at commissioning → **Root Cause = Engineering design gap at commissioning.**

**Step 5 — CAPA Recommendations (1 mark):**
- **Corrective:** Replace seal, recalibrate gauge, retighten belt.
- **Preventive:** Install flow switch interlock, add vibration monitoring to PM plan, train operators on alarm SOP.

**Step 6 — Verification and Closure (1 mark):**
Monitor for 90 days: zero dry-running events, seal chamber temperature stable at 56 °C, vibration within ISO 10816-3 Class A.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and application of the Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** for analysing a boiler tube leak failure. List its **merits and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**? Construct a small fault tree for the *top event "Boiler Tube Rupture"* and compute the top-event probability given: $P(\text{Scale formation}) = 0.04$, $P(\text{Overheating}) = 0.03$, $P(\text{Mechanical damage}) = 0.02$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **Fishbone Diagram**, also called the **Ishikawa Diagram** or **Cause-and-Effect Diagram**, was developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968. It visually maps the potential causes of a problem under standard categories (commonly the **6M**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature).

**Construction Steps (2 marks):**
1. Write the problem statement (effect) at the right end with a horizontal arrow (the "spine").
2. Draw diagonal "bones" representing each causal category.
3. Brainstorm specific causes and attach them as smaller sub-bones.
4. Identify the most significant causes (usually via Pareto ranking) for deeper investigation.

**Application to Boiler Tube Leak (3 marks):**
The 6M categories would be populated as:
- **Man:** Boiler operator not trained on water chemistry logs.
- **Machine:** Tube age 12 years; soot blower malfunctioning.
- **Material:** Feedwater hardness 6 ppm (above 2 ppm spec).
- **Method:** Blowdown schedule skipped for 18 days.
- **Measurement:** Conductivity sensor calibration overdue.
- **Mother Nature:** Hard well water with high silica content.

**Merits (1 mark):**
- Excellent for multi-disciplinary brainstorming.
- Visual, easy to communicate to non-technical stakeholders.
- Categorizes causes so that no domain is overlooked.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Risk of double-counting.
- No quantitative probability weighting.
- Does not show inter-relationships between causes.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a top-down, deductive failure analysis technique that uses Boolean logic (AND / OR gates) to decompose an undesired TOP EVENT into its basic contributing events.

**Fault Tree Structure (3 marks):**

The top event **T** = *Boiler Tube Rupture* is decomposed using an **OR gate** into three intermediate events:
- **A:** Scale formation on tube inner surface.
- **B:** Localized overheating.
- **C:** Mechanical damage (impact, erosion, fatigue).

The Boolean expression:
$$
T = A \cup B \cup C
$$

**Probability Computation (3 marks):**

Given $P(A) = 0.04$, $P(B) = 0.03$, $P(C) = 0.02$, and assuming independence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))(1 - P(C)) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.04)(1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.02) \\
&= 1 - (0.96 \times 0.97 \times 0.98) \\
&= 1 - 0.9126 \\
&= 0.0874 \\
&\approx 8.74\% \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 9 in 100 boilers per year will rupture under these independent failure probabilities, justifying a major engineering review.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in RCA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Symptom" with "Root Cause"** — Saying *"the bearing failed"* is not a root cause. You must drill at least 3–5 levels deep.
> 2. **Stopping at "Human Error"** — Modern RCA explicitly forbids this. Always ask: *Why did the human make that error?* (Training gap? Bad SOP? Inadequate tool?)
> 3. **Skipping the Preventive Action** — Writing only *corrective* actions loses you 1–2 marks. KTU expects a clear **CAPA pair** (Corrective + Preventive).
> 4. **No KPI for Verification** — Without a verification metric (MTBF, vibration level, temperature), the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Missing the Diagram** — A 14-mark question that asks for Fishbone or FTA without the diagram is auto-deducted by 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong use of Boolean Logic** — In FTA, confusing AND vs OR gate logic is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- [x] RCA is **proactive**, not reactive; it finds *why* something happened, not just *what* happened.
- [x] Standard KTU procedure has **6 to 7 steps**: Define → Collect → Map → Identify → Recommend → Implement → Verify.
- [x] The **6M** Fishbone categories: **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature**.
- [x] The **5 Whys** technique originates from **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota).
- [x] The **Fishbone Diagram** originates from **Kaoru Ishikawa** (1968).
- [x] **FTA** uses **AND / OR Boolean gates**; top probability $P(T) = 1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$ for OR gate.
- [x] **FMEA's RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection**; threshold for action is **RPN > 150**.
- [x] **Pareto Principle**: 80% of failures come from 20% of causes.
- [x] Avoid *human error* as a root cause — always dig deeper.
- [x] Every RCA must produce a **CAPA pair** (Corrective + Preventive).
- [x] Verification KPIs: **MTBF, Availability, OEE, vibration, temperature, RPN**.
- [x] RCA supports **ISO 9001 (Clause 10.2)**, **OSHA PSM**, and **ISO 45001**.
- [x] Industry rule of thumb: **1 unit of prevention saves 5 units of repair and 25 units of consequential loss** (the 1-5-25 rule).
- [x] Always remember the formula $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- [x] KTU 14-mark question blueprint: **4 merits (3 marks) + 6 procedure steps (6 marks) + 1 worked example + 1 diagram (5 marks)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cause and Effect Analysis, Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Failure Mode Effect and Criticality Analysis (FMECA), Six Sigma Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cause and Effect Analysis, Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Failure Mode Effect and Criticality Analysis (FMECA), Six Sigma Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cause and Effect Analysis (CEA)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
Cause and Effect Analysis is a structured, deductive brainstorming technique used in maintenance engineering to systematically identify, categorize, and visually map the potential causes of an undesirable effect (typically a failure, defect, or problem) in a process, system, or equipment. It is most commonly represented using the **Ishikawa Diagram** (also called the **Fishbone Diagram** or **Herringbone Diagram**), developed by Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa in 1968.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Cause and Effect Analysis is a problem-solving tool that graphically displays the relationship between a problem (effect) and all its possible causes. The "effect" appears at the head of the fish, and the "causes" form the bones/ribs of the fish structure.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient with chronic headaches. Instead of immediately prescribing painkillers, the doctor methodically examines possible causes: stress, dehydration, eye strain, poor posture, lack of sleep, diet, etc. The doctor draws a central line (the patient's spine) with branches for each category of possible cause, and smaller sub-branches for specific reasons within each category. This visual "fishbone" helps everyone see the complete diagnostic picture at a glance. **Cause and Effect Analysis works exactly the same way for machines** — instead of guessing why a pump failed, we draw a fishbone that lists every possible category of cause (Manpower, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment), making hidden causes visible.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6M Categories (Standard Industrial Classification):**
> - **Manpower** (Human factors, skills, training)
> - **Machine** (Equipment condition, wear, calibration)
> - **Method** (Procedures, SOPs, work instructions)
> - **Material** (Raw material quality, specifications)
> - **Measurement** (Instrumentation, accuracy, gauges)
> - **Mother Nature / Environment** (Temperature, humidity, dust, vibration)

---

### 1.2 Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
FMEA is a **systematic, proactive, and deductive analytical methodology** used to evaluate a system, design, process, or service to identify potential failure modes, their causes, and their effects on system performance, safety, or customer satisfaction. It was first developed by the U.S. military (Reliability Engineering, 1949) and later adopted by NASA and the automotive industry (QS-9000 / IATF 16949). In maintenance engineering, FMEA is used to prioritize maintenance actions based on risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** FMEA is *qualitative* in nature — it identifies and ranks failure modes but does NOT compute a numerical "criticality number." The moment you add a Criticality Number (CN) or Criticality Analysis Matrix, it becomes **FMECA**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of FMEA like a **pre-flight checklist for a commercial aircraft**. Before takeoff, the pilot doesn't wait for something to fail — instead, the team systematically asks: *"What could go wrong with the landing gear? What if the hydraulic line leaks? What if a sensor fails?"* For every possible failure, they note the **consequence** (effect), the **cause**, how **likely** it is, and how **bad** it would be. FMEA works identically for industrial equipment — you don't wait for the cooling pump to fail catastrophically; you pre-emptively ask "what could fail, why, and how bad would it be?"

---

### 1.3 Failure Mode Effect and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
FMECA is an **extension of FMEA that incorporates a quantitative criticality assessment**. It assigns a numerical ranking (Criticality Number or Criticality Index) to each identified failure mode by combining the failure mode's severity, probability of occurrence, and probability of detection. This criticality ranking enables maintenance engineers to prioritize corrective actions, spares inventory, and preventive maintenance schedules based on objective numerical risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard Formula:** The most commonly used criticality number is the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** which equals:
>
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
>
> where **S** = Severity, **O** = Occurrence, **D** = Detection. An alternative is the **Criticality Number (CN)** = Severity × Probability of Occurrence (used in MIL-STD-1629).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you have 50 items on your to-do list, and you have only 4 hours. You obviously don't do them in random order. You **score** each task: How important is it? (Severity), How urgent/deadline-driven? (Occurrence), How much will procrastinating make it worse? (Detection). Then you multiply these scores and tackle the highest-scoring tasks first. **FMECA does the same with failure modes** — it numerically scores each potential failure so engineers know which ones to fix first.

---

### 1.4 Six Sigma Maintenance

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
Six Sigma Maintenance is the application of **Six Sigma quality methodology (DMAIC: Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control)** to maintenance engineering, with the goal of reducing process variation, eliminating defects/failures, and driving maintenance cost toward **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — equivalent to a **99.99966% success rate**. Originally developed by Motorola (1986) and popularized by General Electric (1995), Six Sigma Maintenance integrates statistical process control (SPC) with preventive and predictive maintenance strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Sigma Quality Target:** $\sigma$ (sigma) is the standard deviation. At 6$\sigma$ level, the process operates with such tight variation that defects become statistically near-impossible. The metric is **DPMO = 3.4**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a vending machine that is supposed to dispense exactly one can of soda every time. Sometimes it dispenses two (over-dispense defect). Sometimes it dispenses none (under-dispense defect). A regular maintenance team just fixes it when it breaks. A **Six Sigma Maintenance team** would measure the average dispense amount, track variation day-by-day, analyze root causes of variation, and engineer a system where the variation is so small that virtually every customer gets exactly one can, every time, forever. **Six Sigma = near-perfect consistency**, applied to maintenance outcomes.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Summary for KTU Board Reference:**
> - **DPMO Target:** **3.4** (at 6$\sigma$)
> - **Yield Target:** **99.99966%**
> - **RPN Formula:** $S \times O \times D$ (each parameter 1–10 scale)
> - **FMEA Standard:** **IEC 60812:2006** / **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook 2019**
> - **FMECA Standard:** **MIL-STD-1629A** (US Military) / **IEC 60812**
> - **Ishikawa (Cause-Effect) Year:** **1968** (Quality Control circles, Japan)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cause and Effect Analysis — Operational Framework

**Purpose:** Identify root causes of an observed effect (problem/failure/defect).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Define the Effect (Problem Statement):**
   - State the problem clearly and specifically.
   - Example: *"Excessive vibration in CNC spindle during high-speed cutting (3x the acceptable limit)."*
   - Avoid vague statements like *"machine is bad."*

2. **Draw the Backbone (Spine of the Fish):**
   - Draw a horizontal arrow pointing right. The arrowhead is the **effect**.

3. **Identify the Major Cause Categories (6M Framework):**
   - **Manpower** → Operator skill, training, fatigue
   - **Machine** → Wear, calibration, lubrication
   - **Method** → SOP, sequence, parameters
   - **Material** → Quality, hardness, contamination
   - **Measurement** → Gauge accuracy, calibration
   - **Mother Nature** → Temperature, humidity, dust, vibration

4. **Brainstorm Sub-Causes (The Ribs):**
   - For each category, ask "What could cause this within Manpower?" etc.
   - Use **5 Whys** technique alongside fishbone.

5. **Identify Most Likely Causes:**
   - Mark or circle the most probable causes using data (Pareto, histograms).

6. **Verify and Act:**
   - Test hypotheses via experiments or data analysis.
   - Implement corrective actions.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- **Why define the effect first?** Because vague problems produce vague solutions. Specificity forces clarity.
- **Why use categories?** To prevent cognitive bias and ensure all domains are considered.
- **Why 5 Whys alongside fishbone?** Because fishbone identifies *what* and *where*, while 5 Whys digs into *why*.

---

### 2.2 FMEA — Operational Theory

**Purpose:** Proactively identify failure modes, effects, and prioritize them.

**Three Core Parameters (Risk Priority Number Inputs):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Severity | S | How bad is the consequence? | 1 (negligible) – 10 (catastrophic) |
| Occurrence | O | How frequently does the failure occur? | 1 (very rare) – 10 (very frequent) |
| Detection | D | How likely is it to be detected before reaching customer? | 1 (almost certain) – 10 (almost impossible) |

**FMEA Workflow:**
1. **Define the System Boundary & Block Diagram** → What is in scope?
2. **Identify Functions** → What must the system/equipment do?
3. **Identify Failure Modes** → *How* can it fail to perform its function?
4. **Identify Effects** → *What* happens to the system/customer/safety?
5. **Identify Causes** → *Why* does the failure occur?
6. **Score S, O, D** → Using standard FMEA rating tables.
7. **Compute RPN = S × O × D**
8. **Prioritize Actions** → Highest RPN first.
9. **Implement & Re-score** → After corrective action, RPN should drop.

**The "Why" Behind FMEA:**
- **Why proactive?** Because reactive maintenance is 3–5× costlier than proactive planning.
- **Why team-based?** Because diverse perspectives catch more failure modes.
- **Why numerical scoring?** Because qualitative intuition is biased; numbers enable prioritization.

---

### 2.3 FMECA — Quantitative Criticality Extension

**Purpose:** Add quantitative risk ranking to FMEA.

**Two Standard Criticality Approaches:**

**Approach A: Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Industry/Automotive Standard**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**RPN Ranges (AIAG-VDA Convention):**
- **RPN ≥ 200** → Mandatory action, escalation
- **RPN 100–199** → Action recommended
- **RPN < 100** → Acceptable, monitor

**Approach B: Criticality Number (CN) — MIL-STD-1629A**

$$\text{CN} = \text{Severity Level} \times \text{Probability Level} \times \text{Failure Mode Ratio}$$

where:
- Severity Level = 1 (negligible) to 5 (catastrophic)
- Probability Level = A (frequent) to E (improbable)
- Failure Mode Ratio = proportion of failures attributable to that mode

**Mode of Failure (Severity Levels per MIL-STD-1629A):**

| Level | Description | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **IV** | Minor | Negligible, requires only minor repair |
| **III** | Marginal | Loss of mission capability |
| **II** | Critical | Major damage, injury possible |
| **I** | Catastrophic | Loss of life or system destruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Students often confuse FMEA with FMECA. The mnemonic is simple: **"C is for Criticality = C is for Calculation."** If there is numerical criticality computation, it is FMECA.

---

### 2.4 Six Sigma Maintenance — DMAIC Framework

**Purpose:** Statistically eliminate root causes of variation and failure.

**Five Phases (DMAIC):**

1. **Define:**
   - Define the problem, customer (internal/external), and project goal (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality).
   - Tools: Project Charter, Voice of Customer (VOC), SIPOC diagram.

2. **Measure:**
   - Quantify the current performance baseline.
   - Tools: Data collection plan, Process Capability Indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$), DPMO.

3. **Analyze:**
   - Identify the root causes of variation and defects.
   - Tools: Regression, hypothesis testing, Pareto, Fishbone, FMEA.

4. **Improve:**
   - Implement solutions to eliminate root causes.
   - Tools: Design of Experiments (DOE), pilot testing, Kaizen.

5. **Control:**
   - Sustain improvements via control plans, SPC charts, and standardized work.
   - Tools: Control charts ($\bar{X}$-R, p-chart, c-chart), Standard Operating Procedures.

**Key Process Capability Indices (Six Sigma Core):**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

where:
- **USL** = Upper Specification Limit
- **LSL** = Lower Specification Limit
- **$\mu$** = Process mean
- **$\sigma$** = Process standard deviation

**Six Sigma Capability Benchmark:**

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Sigma Level | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | < 3$\sigma$ | Inadequate (high defect rate) |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | 3$\sigma$ | Marginal |
| $1.33 - 1.67$ | 4$\sigma$ | Capable |
| $\geq 1.67$ | 5$\sigma$ + | Excellent |
| **2.00** | **6$\sigma$** | **World-class (3.4 DPMO)** |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — these formulas appear frequently in KTU university exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA/FMECA prioritization |
| 2 | Criticality Number (MIL-STD-1629A) | $\text{CN} = S \times P \times f$ | Military FMECA |
| 3 | DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | Six Sigma quality |
| 4 | Process Capability (spread) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Six Sigma maintenance |
| 5 | Process Capability (centered) | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Six Sigma maintenance |
| 6 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \text{Yield}_1 \times \text{Yield}_2 \times \dots \times \text{Yield}_n$ | Six Sigma DMAIC |
| 7 | First Time Yield | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units out}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | Six Sigma DMAIC |
| 8 | Process Sigma (from DPMO) | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(1 - \text{DPMO}/10^6) + 1.5$ | Six Sigma conversion |
| 9 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | Reliability metric |
| 10 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Maintenance effectiveness |

**Real-World Utility of These Tools:**

- **Cause & Effect Analysis** → Used in **root cause failure analysis (RCFA)** for any production loss or safety incident (e.g., aircraft engine failures, refinery explosions).
- **FMEA/FMECA** → Mandatory in **aerospace (AS9100), automotive (IATF 16949), medical devices (ISO 13485), nuclear (NRC)**, and increasingly in food processing and pharma.
- **Six Sigma Maintenance** → Used in **manufacturing plants, oil & gas, utilities, semiconductor fabs** to drive maintenance cost down and uptime up. GE famously saved **$12 billion** through Six Sigma (1995–2005).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Board Traps:**
> - RPN scale is 1–10, so maximum RPN = 10 × 10 × 10 = **1000**.
> - Six Sigma target is **3.4 DPMO, not zero.** The 1.5 sigma shift is already built in.
> - $C_p$ assumes the process is centered; $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-center processes. For maintenance, $C_{pk}$ is the more meaningful metric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: FMEA Computation for a Centrifugal Pump

**Scenario:** A maintenance team is conducting an FMEA on a centrifugal pump used in a boiler feed water system. They have identified three potential failure modes. The FMEA worksheet is shown below.

| Failure Mode | Cause | Effect | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1: Impeller wear** | Cavitation, abrasive particles | Reduced flow, eventual seizure | 7 | 4 | 5 |
| **F2: Mechanical seal leak** | Improper installation, O-ring failure | Process fluid loss, safety hazard | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| **F3: Bearing failure** | Lubrication failure, overloading | Catastrophic seizure, downtime | 9 | 3 | 6 |

**Step-by-Step RPN Calculation:**

**Step 1: Identify the failure mode to analyze first.**
We will compute RPN for each failure mode, then rank them.

**Step 2: For F1 (Impeller wear):**

$$\text{RPN}_{F1} = S_{F1} \times O_{F1} \times D_{F1}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{RPN}_{F1} = 7 \times 4 \times 5$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$7 \times 4 = 28$$

$$28 \times 5 = 140$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{F1} = 140}$$

**Step 3: For F2 (Mechanical seal leak):**

$$\text{RPN}_{F2} = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

Multiplication sequence:

$$8 \times 5 = 40$$

$$40 \times 4 = 160$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{F2} = 160}$$

**Step 4: For F3 (Bearing failure):**

$$\text{RPN}_{F3} = 9 \times 3 \times 6$$

Multiplication sequence:

$$9 \times 3 = 27$$

$$27 \times 6 = 162$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{F3} = 162}$$

**Step 5: Rank by RPN (descending priority):**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F3 (Bearing failure) | 162 | **Highest** — Action mandatory |
| 2 | F2 (Mechanical seal leak) | 160 | Action recommended |
| 3 | F1 (Impeller wear) | 140 | Monitor |

**Step 6: Interpret and Recommend Action:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** All RPN values are between 100 and 199 → **Action recommended** for all three. However, the bearing failure has the highest RPN (162) AND the highest severity (9, near catastrophic), so it is escalated to mandatory action despite the numerical proximity to F2.

**Recommended Corrective Actions:**
- **F3 (Bearing failure):** Install vibration monitoring sensors, switch to automatic lubrication system, train operators on load monitoring.
- **F2 (Seal leak):** Standardize seal installation procedure, install seal flush plan, use higher-grade O-rings.
- **F1 (Impeller wear):** Add suction strainer, monitor NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head), schedule periodic impeller inspection.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Process Capability ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$) for Six Sigma Maintenance

**Scenario:** A motor shaft diameter specification is **24.00 mm ± 0.05 mm** (USL = 24.05, LSL = 23.95). Process data collected from 100 shafts gives:
- Mean $\mu = 24.02$ mm (slightly off-center toward USL)
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm

**Step 1: Compute Process Capability $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

Substitute the values:

$$C_p = \frac{24.05 - 23.95}{6 \times 0.012}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$24.05 - 23.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$6 \times 0.012 = 0.072 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.3889 \approx 1.39$$

So:

$$\boxed{C_p \approx 1.39}$$

**Step 2: Compute Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

**Compute the first term (upper side):**

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.05 - 24.02}{3 \times 0.012}$$

Numerator:

$$24.05 - 24.02 = 0.03 \text{ mm}$$

Denominator:

$$3 \times 0.012 = 0.036 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{0.03}{0.036} = 0.8333$$

**Compute the second term (lower side):**

$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.02 - 23.95}{3 \times 0.012}$$

Numerator:

$$24.02 - 23.95 = 0.07 \text{ mm}$$

Denominator (same as before):

$$3 \times 0.012 = 0.036 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{0.07}{0.036} = 1.9444$$

**Step 3: Take the minimum:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.8333, 1.9444) = 0.8333$$

So:

$$\boxed{C_{pk} \approx 0.83}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the Results:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Maintenance Insight:** Although $C_p = 1.39$ suggests "capable," $C_{pk} = 0.83$ reveals that the process is **off-center** (drifting toward the upper specification limit). This means shafts are being produced that are slightly oversize, even though the spread is acceptable. **The Six Sigma Maintenance action: re-center the lathe/cutting tool to bring the mean back to 24.00 mm.**

**Step 5: Estimate DPMO using the $C_{pk}$ value:**

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table (with 1.5$\sigma$ shift), $C_{pk} = 0.83$ corresponds approximately to a **2.5$\sigma$ process**, yielding roughly **158,000 DPMO** — far from Six Sigma target of 3.4 DPMO.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: DPMO and Sigma Level Conversion

**Scenario:** A bottling plant produced **9,500 defective bottles** out of **1,000,000** total production, with **5 defect opportunities per bottle**.

**Step 1: Compute Total Defect Opportunities:**

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = \text{Units Produced} \times \text{Defect Opportunities per Unit}$$

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 5 = 5{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute DPMO:**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Opportunities}}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{9{,}500 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{9{,}500{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}900 \text{ DPMO}$$

So:

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 1{,}900}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Yield:**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - 0.0019 = 0.9981 = 99.81\%$$

**Step 4: Convert to Sigma Level:**

Using the standard formula:

$$\text{Sigma Level} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}\right) + 1.5$$

$$\text{Sigma Level} = \text{NORMSINV}(0.9981) + 1.5$$

$$\text{NORMSINV}(0.9981) \approx 3.09$$

$$\text{Sigma Level} = 3.09 + 1.5 = 4.59 \approx 4.6\sigma$$

So:

$$\boxed{\text{Process is operating at approximately 4.6 sigma level}}$$

**Step 5: Maintenance Action Plan (DMAIC):**

- **Define:** Reduce bottling defects from 1,900 DPMO to 3.4 DPMO.
- **Measure:** Track fill volume, cap torque, label placement, bottle integrity.
- **Analyze:** Pareto analysis reveals 70% of defects from fill volume variation.
- **Improve:** Install mass flow controller with closed-loop feedback.
- **Control:** Implement SPC $\bar{X}$-R chart on fill volume, set control limits at ±3$\sigma$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Tool

| Question | Best Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| *What is the problem?* | Problem statement + 5 Whys | Defines scope |
| *What could cause the problem?* | Cause and Effect (Fishbone) | Broad categorization |
| *What could fail in the system?* | FMEA | Component-level failure modes |
| *Which failure is most critical?* | FMECA | Numerical prioritization |
| *Where is variation coming from?* | Six Sigma DMAIC | Statistical root cause |
| *Is the process capable?* | $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$ | Process capability |
| *How many defects?* | DPMO / Yield | Quality metric |
| *Which sigma level are we at?* | Sigma conversion | Benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Integration Insight:** In modern maintenance engineering, these four tools are **not alternatives — they are sequential**. A typical workflow is: (1) Use Fishbone to identify *categories* of cause → (2) Use FMEA to list *specific* failure modes → (3) Use FMECA to *rank* them → (4) Use Six Sigma DMAIC to *fix* the highest-ranking cause statistically. This is the KTU-recommended integration approach.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram — Cause and Effect Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Effect: Excessive Vibration in CNC Spindle]:::effect
    
    B1[Manpower]:::branch
    C1[Machine]:::branch
    D1[Method]:::branch
    E1[Material]:::branch
    F1[Measurement]:::branch
    G1[Environment]:::branch
    
    A1 --- B1
    A1 --- C1
    A1 --- D1
    A1 --- E1
    A1 --- F1
    A1 --- G1
    
    B1 --- B1a[Untrained operator]
    B1 --- B1b[Operator fatigue]
    B1 --- B1c[Inadequate supervision]
    
    C1 --- C1a[Bearing wear]
    C1 --- C1b[Spindle imbalance]
    C1 --- C1c[Tool holder looseness]
    C1 --- C1d[Lack of lubrication]
    
    D1 --- D1a[Wrong cutting parameters]
    D1 --- D1b[Missing SOP]
    D1 --- D1c[Incorrect tool selection]
    
    E1 --- E1a[Imbalance in workpiece]
    E1 --- E1b[Low-grade cutting tool]
    
    F1 --- F1a[Uncalibrated accelerometer]
    F1 --- F1b[Outdated baseline readings]
    
    G1 --- G1a[High ambient temperature]
    G1 --- G1b[Foundation vibration from nearby press]
    G1 --- G1c[Dust accumulation on spindle]
    
    classDef effect fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C92A2A,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef branch fill:#4DABF7,stroke:#1971C2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The **red node** (right side) is the effect.
- The **blue nodes** are the 6M cause categories.
- Sub-causes branch off each main category.
- In practice, the fishbone is read from right to left: from the observed effect back to the most likely root causes.

---

### 4.2 FMEA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Define System and Scope]:::phase
    step2[Step 2: Develop Block Diagram]:::phase
    step3[Step 3: Identify Functions]:::phase
    step4[Step 4: Identify Failure Modes]:::phase
    step5[Step 5: Identify Effects and Causes]:::phase
    step6[Step 6: Assign Severity S 1 to 10]:::phase
    step7[Step 7: Assign Occurrence O 1 to 10]:::phase
    step8[Step 8: Assign Detection D 1 to 10]:::phase
    step9[Step 9: Compute RPN equals S times O times D]:::phase
    step10[Step 10: Rank by RPN and Set Action Priority]:::phase
    step11[Step 11: Implement Corrective Actions]:::phase
    step12[Step 12: Recompute RPN and Validate]:::phase
    
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> step11
    step11 --> step12
    step12 --> step10
    
    classDef phase fill:#51CF66,stroke:#2F9E44,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flow is **iterative** (note the loop back from Step 12 to Step 10).
- FMEA is a **living document** — re-evaluated whenever design, process, or operating conditions change.

---

### 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC Framework for Maintenance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Define: Problem, Customer, CTQ, Project Charter]:::def
    M1[Measure: Baseline Data, Cp, Cpk, DPMO]:::meas
    A1[Analyze: Root Cause, Fishbone, FMEA, Pareto]:::anal
    I1[Improve: Pilot Solutions, DOE, Kaizen]:::imp
    C1[Control: SPC Charts, SOPs, Control Plan]:::ctrl
    
    D1 --> M1
    M1 --> A1
    A1 --> I1
    I1 --> C1
    C1 --> M1
    
    D1 -.- D2[Tools: VOC, SIPOC, Project Charter]:::tool
    M1 -.- M2[Tools: Gage R and R, Data Plan, Histogram]:::tool
    A1 -.- A2[Tools: 5 Whys, Regression, Hypothesis Tests]:::tool
    I1 -.- I2[Tools: DOE, Pilot Run, Cost Benefit]:::tool
    C1 -.- C2[Tools: Control Charts, FMEA Update, SOP]:::tool
    
    classDef def fill:#FF8787,stroke:#E03131,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef meas fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#F08C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000,font-weight:bold
    classDef anal fill:#69DB7C,stroke:#2F9E44,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef imp fill:#4DABF7,stroke:#1971C2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef ctrl fill:#B197FC,stroke:#7048E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef tool fill:#F1F3F5,stroke:#495057,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000,font-style:italic
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Red (D)** = Define (scope the problem).
- **Yellow (M)** = Measure (collect data).
- **Green (A)** = Analyze (find root causes).
- **Blue (I)** = Improve (implement solutions).
- **Purple (C)** = Control (sustain the gains).
- The loop back from Control to Measure ensures **continuous improvement**.

---

### 4.4 FMECA Criticality Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: List All Failure Modes]:::start
    P2{Is Severity greater than 8?}:::decision
    P3{Is RPN greater than 200?}:::decision
    P4{Is RPN between 100 and 199?}:::decision
    P5[Action: Mandatory Corrective Action]:::critical
    P6[Action: Recommended Corrective Action]:::high
    P7[Action: Monitor and Review]:::low
    P8[Action: Acceptable, No Action]:::accept
    
    P1 --> P2
    P2 -->|Yes| P5
    P2 -->|No| P3
    P3 -->|Yes| P5
    P3 -->|No| P4
    P4 -->|Yes| P6
    P4 -->|No| P7
    P4 -->|RPN less than 50| P8
    
    classDef start fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#F08C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000,font-weight:bold
    classDef decision fill:#74C0FC,stroke:#1971C2,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef critical fill:#FA5252,stroke:#C92A2A,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef high fill:#FF922B,stroke:#D9480F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef low fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#F08C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000,font-weight:bold
    classDef accept fill:#51CF66,stroke:#2F9E44,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flow is a **decision tree** that any maintenance team can apply to sort failure modes by urgency.
- **Severity > 8 always triggers action** regardless of RPN — safety overrides statistics.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Cause and Effect Analysis. List the six categories commonly used in an Ishikawa diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cause and Effect Analysis is a structured brainstorming technique that visually displays the potential causes of a problem (effect) in a graphical format resembling a fish skeleton. It was developed by Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa in 1968 for use in quality control circles.

**Six Categories (2 marks — 0.33 per category, full credit for any 6 listed):**
1. **Manpower** — People-related causes (skill, training, fatigue).
2. **Machine** — Equipment-related causes (wear, calibration, breakdown).
3. **Method** — Process/procedure-related causes (SOP, sequence, parameters).
4. **Material** — Input-related causes (raw material quality, contamination).
5. **Measurement** — Instrumentation causes (gauge accuracy, calibration).
6. **Mother Nature / Environment** — Ambient conditions (temperature, humidity, dust, vibration).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between FMEA and FMECA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | FMEA | FMECA |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Failure Modes and Effects Analysis | Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis |
| **Nature** | Qualitative (descriptive) | Quantitative (numerical ranking) |
| **Criticality Computation** | Not performed | RPN = S × O × D computed |
| **Output** | List of failure modes and effects | Ranked list with criticality numbers |
| **Standard Reference** | IEC 60812, AIAG-VDA | MIL-STD-1629A, IEC 60812 |
| **Use** | Identify what can fail | Identify what can fail AND prioritize for action |

> The key distinguishing feature is the **Criticality Number (CN) or Risk Priority Number (RPN)** in FMECA, which is absent in FMEA.

---

### PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose One)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the steps involved in performing a Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA). Describe the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and explain its significance in maintenance decision-making. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has the following failure mode data:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seal leakage | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| Valve sticking | 7 | 5 | 5 |
| Pump cavitation | 9 | 3 | 7 |

Compute the RPN for each, rank them, and recommend a maintenance strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**FMEA Steps (5 marks distributed as 1 mark each for first 5 steps, 2 marks for RPN explanation):**

1. **Define the System and Scope:** Identify the system boundaries, interfaces, and the level of analysis (system, sub-system, or component).

2. **Construct a Block Diagram:** Visually represent the system, showing interfaces and interconnections between components.

3. **Identify Functions:** For each component, list its intended function(s) under normal and degraded conditions.

4. **Identify Failure Modes:** For each function, list *how* the function could fail (e.g., "no output," "reduced output," "intermittent operation," "over-output").

5. **Identify Effects and Causes:** For each failure mode, determine:
   - The **Effect** (consequence on the system, mission, safety, customer).
   - The **Cause** (root cause triggering the failure mode).

6. **Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) Scores** using standard rating tables (1–10 scale).

7. **Compute RPN = S × O × D** for each failure mode.

8. **Prioritize and Take Action:** Rank failure modes by RPN and assign corrective actions to the highest-risk items.

9. **Document and Recompute:** Re-evaluate RPN after corrective action to verify risk reduction.

**RPN Significance (2 marks):**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a numerical metric that quantifies the relative risk of each failure mode, enabling objective prioritization of maintenance resources.

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D \quad \text{where each factor is rated 1 to 10 (max RPN = 1000)}$$

**Significance in maintenance decision-making:**
- Enables ranking of failure modes when resources (time, spares, manpower) are limited.
- Provides a **common, objective language** for cross-functional teams.
- Drives **proactive maintenance** by focusing on high-risk modes before failure occurs.
- Forms the basis for **critical spares inventory decisions** — high-RPN items justify stocked spares.
- Supports **continuous improvement** — RPN should decrease after each corrective action cycle.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute RPN for Seal Leakage (2 marks):**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Seal}} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 6 \times 4$$

$$8 \times 6 = 48$$

$$48 \times 4 = 192$$

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{\text{Seal}} = 192}$$

**Step 2: Compute RPN for Valve Sticking (2 marks):**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Valve}} = 7 \times 5 \times 5$$

$$7 \times 5 = 35$$

$$35 \times 5 = 175$$

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{\text{Valve}} = 175}$$

**Step 3: Compute RPN for Pump Cavitation (2 marks):**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Cavitation}} = 9 \times 3 \times 7$$

$$9 \times 3 = 27$$

$$27 \times 7 = 189$$

$$\boxed{\text{RPN}_{\text{Cavitation}} = 189}$$

**Step 4: Rank and Recommend (1 mark):**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seal leakage | 192 | **High priority** — install dual seals, periodic inspection |
| 2 | Pump cavitation | 189 | **High priority** — NPSH check, suction line modification |
| 3 | Valve sticking | 175 | **Recommended action** — filter hydraulic fluid, replace seals |

> All three are in the **100–199 range** → corrective action recommended. The bearing failure (high severity = 9) also warrants escalation despite RPN ranking.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the Six Sigma DMAIC methodology as applied to maintenance engineering. List the key tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process produces steel rods with diameter specification **20.00 mm ± 0.08 mm**. The current process mean is **20.03 mm** and the standard deviation is **0.02 mm**. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability and recommend corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**DMAIC Phases (5 marks — 1 mark per phase):**

**1. Define:**
- Identify the maintenance problem statement and project goal.
- Define the **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics.
- Establish a **Project Charter** with problem, goal, scope, timeline, and team.
- **Tools:** Voice of Customer (VOC), SIPOC diagram, Project Charter.

**2. Measure:**
- Quantify the current performance baseline.
- Establish data collection plans and measurement system accuracy.
- Compute process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) and DPMO.
- **Tools:** Data collection plan, Gage R&R, histogram, run chart.

**3. Analyze:**
- Identify root causes of variation and failure using statistical methods.
- **Tools:** Pareto chart, Cause and Effect (Fishbone), FMEA, 5 Whys, regression, hypothesis testing.

**4. Improve:**
- Design, pilot, and implement solutions to eliminate root causes.
- Optimize process parameters via experimentation.
- **Tools:** Design of Experiments (DOE), pilot testing, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing).

**5. Control:**
- Sustain improvements via standard work and statistical process control.
- Hand over the improved process to operations/maintenance team.
- **Tools:** Control charts ($\bar{X}$-R, p-chart, c-chart), Control Plan, Standard Operating Procedures, training.

**Key Tools Summary Table (2 marks):**

| Phase | Key Tools |
