```mermaid
    node8 --> node14["Counter Strategy: Root Cause Analysis"]
    node9 --> node15["Counter Strategy: Poka Yoke and SPC"]
    node10 --> node16["Counter Strategy: First Time Right Programs"]

    node11 --> node17["Maintenance Effectiveness Output"]
    node12 --> node17
    node13 --> node17
    node14 --> node17
    node15 --> node17
    node16 --> node17

    node17 --> node18["Higher OEE greater than 85 percent"]
    node17 --> node19["Lower Lifecycle Cost per Unit"]
    node17 --> node20["Improved Safety and Compliance"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The RAM (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability) Triangle

This diagram illustrates how MTBF and MTTR are the engineering levers that determine Inherent Availability, which in turn drives Maintenance Effectiveness.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Reliability Domain"] --> nodeB["MTBF Mean Time Between Failures"]
    nodeC["Maintainability Domain"] --> nodeD["MTTR Mean Time To Repair"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["Inherent Availability Ai"]
    nodeD --> nodeE

    nodeE --> nodeF["Maintenance Effectiveness KPI"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Decisions on PM Intervals"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Spare Parts Stocking Policy"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Outsourcing vs In house Repair"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Design for Maintainability DfM"]

    nodeB --> nodeK["Improved by RCM and PdM"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["Improved by Training and Modularity"]
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeL --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The Maintenance Effectiveness Hierarchy

This shows how strategic maintenance objectives cascade down to operational metrics.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Strategic Goal: World Class Manufacturing"] --> node2["Tactical Objective: Zero Breakdowns"]
    node1 --> node3["Tactical Objective: Zero Defects"]
    node1 --> node4["Tactical Objective: Zero Accidents"]

    node2 --> node5["Operational KPI: OEE greater than 85 percent"]
    node3 --> node6["Operational KPI: First Pass Yield greater than 99 percent"]
    node4 --> node7["Operational KPI: TRIR less than 0.5"]

    node5 --> node8["Leading Indicator: MTBF trending up"]
    node5 --> node9["Leading Indicator: MTTR trending down"]
    node5 --> node10["Leading Indicator: PM Compliance greater than 95 percent"]
    node5 --> node11["Leading Indicator: Maintenance Cost less than 5 percent of RAV"]

    node8 --> node12["Maintenance Effectiveness Score"]
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
```

## 4.4 Interpretation of the Diagrams

- **Diagram 1 (OEE Tree):** Demonstrates that the three OEE factors each have two specific loss categories, and each loss has a targeted counter-strategy. This is the *diagnostic* side of Maintenance Effectiveness.
- **Diagram 2 (RAM Triangle):** Shows that Maintenance Effectiveness is *engineered* through deliberate manipulation of MTBF (reliability engineering) and MTTR (maintainability engineering). It links engineering decisions to operational outcomes.
- **Diagram 3 (Hierarchy):** Provides the *governance* view — strategic goals must be translated into tactical objectives, which must be measured by operational KPIs, which are driven by leading indicators. Maintenance Effectiveness is a **leading indicator**, not a lagging one.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Maintenance Effectiveness and distinguish it from Maintenance Efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Maintenance Effectiveness** is the degree to which a maintenance program achieves its intended objectives of preserving asset function, availability, and reliability at optimal lifecycle cost. It is a *composite* metric encompassing availability, reliability, maintainability, and quality.

**Maintenance Efficiency**, in contrast, is a *resource-utilization* metric that measures how well maintenance resources (labour, time, spare parts) are consumed — e.g., jobs completed per man-hour, or cost per work order.

**Key Distinction:** Efficiency asks *"Are we doing the maintenance right?"* (minimum waste of resources). Effectiveness asks *"Are we doing the right maintenance?"* (maximum value preservation). A team can be highly efficient (low cost) but ineffective (e.g., doing PM on a non-critical asset while ignoring a critical one). The two must be balanced — and the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **Effectiveness is the higher-order goal**; efficiency must serve effectiveness.

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each; 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is OEE? List the three factors and write one formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a TPM metric that quantifies the fraction of scheduled production time that is *truly productive* — i.e., the time spent making good pieces at ideal speed.

The three factors are:

1. **Availability ($A$)** — The fraction of scheduled time the equipment is actually running.

$$A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100$$

2. **Performance ($P$)** — The ratio of actual production rate to the design rate.

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100$$

3. **Quality ($Q$)** — The fraction of conforming units.

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100$$

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q / 10000$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing factors, 1 mark for formulas.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Six Big Losses** model in TPM. How are these losses related to the three factors of OEE? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A packaging line operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each** (16 hours total). Planned breaks total **1 hour per shift**. Unplanned downtime (breakdowns) is **2 hours per shift**. The line produced **6000 units per shift** at an ideal cycle time of **4 seconds per unit**. Defective units were **150 per shift**.

Compute: (i) Availability, (ii) Performance, (iii) Quality, (iv) OEE. Identify the dominant loss category and recommend one maintenance strategy to improve it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

The Six Big Losses is a diagnostic taxonomy developed by Seiichi Nakajima (1988) within the TPM framework. It groups all equipment-related losses into six categories, each linked to a specific OEE factor:

| **Loss** | **Category** | **OEE Factor Affected** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Equipment Failure (Unplanned Breakdowns) | Downtime | Availability |
| 2. Setup and Adjustment Losses | Downtime | Availability |
| 3. Idling and Minor Stoppages | Speed | Performance |
| 4. Reduced Speed Losses | Speed | Performance |
| 5. Defect Losses (During Production) | Quality | Quality |
| 6. Startup / Reduced Yield Losses | Quality | Quality |

*(3 marks for the table/diagram.)*

The relationship is direct: **Losses 1 and 2 reduce Availability**, **Losses 3 and 4 reduce Performance**, and **Losses 5 and 6 reduce Quality**. OEE drops by the cumulative impact of all six losses, making this taxonomy a structured framework for root-cause investigation.

A neat block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) maps the OEE tree to the six losses. The maintenance strategy is selected based on which loss is dominant — for example, PdM addresses Loss 1; SMED addresses Loss 2; operator skill development addresses Losses 3 and 4; Poka-Yoke addresses Losses 5 and 6.

*(2 marks for the linkage explanation; 2 marks for the diagnostic-to-strategy mapping.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1 — Per-Shift Calculations:**

$$T_{\text{PPT per shift}} = 8 \times 60 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{DT per shift}} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{OT per shift}} = 420 - 120 = 300 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Availability:**

$$A = \frac{300}{420} \times 100 = 71.43\%$$

**Step 3 — Performance:**

$$T_{\text{theoretical}} = 6000 \times 4 \text{ s} = 24000 \text{ s} = 400 \text{ minutes}$$

$$P = \frac{400}{300} \times 100 = 133.33\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Performance > 100% Note:** Performance exceeds 100% in this case because the actual rate (6000 pieces / 300 min = 20 pieces/min) is faster than the ideal rate (1 piece / 4 s = 15 pieces/min). For KTU exam purposes, **report the calculated value (133.33%)** and add a remark that this is often capped at 100% in industry software. This shows the examiner that you understand the convention. *(1 mark penalty risk if the student simply writes "100%" without explanation.)*

**Step 4 — Quality:**

$$Q = \frac{6000 - 150}{6000} \times 100 = 97.50\%$$

**Step 5 — OEE:**

$$\text{OEE} = \frac{71.43 \times 133.33 \times 97.50}{10000} = 92.85\%$$

*(1 mark each for A, P, Q, and OEE — total 4 marks.)*

**Step 6 — Identify Dominant Loss:**

The **low Availability (71.43%)** is the bottleneck. The dominant loss is **Loss 1: Equipment Failure / Unplanned Breakdowns** (2 hours of unplanned downtime per shift is excessive).

**Step 7 — Recommended Maintenance Strategy:**

**Predictive Maintenance (Condition Monitoring)** — Install vibration sensors, thermography, and oil analysis on critical bearings and drives of the packaging line. This detects incipient failure before it becomes a breakdown, reducing unplanned downtime and improving Availability. Alternatively, **RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance)** can be deployed to optimize PM intervals based on actual failure modes.

*(3 marks for identifying dominant loss and recommending justified strategy.)*

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice): [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Define and derive the **Inherent Availability** of a repairable system from first principles. How is it related to MTBF and MTTR? What are its limitations? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A critical compressor has been in service for **18 months (540 days, 24-hour operation)**. During this period, it failed **6 times** with the following downtime durations (in hours) per failure: **4, 6, 8, 3, 5, 10**. Compute the MTBF, MTTR, and Inherent Availability. If the target availability is **99.5%**, determine whether the current maintenance regime is effective, and recommend a corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** Inherent Availability ($A_i$) of a repairable system is the fraction of *total time* (operating + corrective repair) during which the system is *expected* to be operational. It considers only hardware uptime and corrective maintenance downtime — *excluding* preventive maintenance, supply delays, and administrative/logistics time.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a single repairable asset alternating between UP and DOWN states in renewal cycles. Let:

- $\text{MTBF}$ = mean duration of the UP state
- $\text{MTTR}$ = mean duration of the DOWN state

A complete renewal cycle has duration:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

By definition, availability is the ratio of UP time to total cycle time:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{UP time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

In terms of failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$ (where $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$):

$$A_i = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Assumes constant failure rate (memoryless process), valid only in the useful life of the "bathtub curve."
- Excludes PM time, logistics delays, and administrative downtime — so it gives a *best-case* estimate.
- Not applicable to non-repairable items (use MTTF instead of MTBF).
- Ignores the stochastic nature of failures; actual availability has a distribution.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Total Operating Time and Downtime:**

Total operating time = 540 days × 24 hours = **12960 hours**

Total downtime = $4 + 6 + 8 + 3 + 5 + 10 = \mathbf{36 \text{ hours}}$

Number of failures = **6**

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{12960}{6} = \mathbf{2160 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MTTR:**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{36}{6} = \mathbf{6 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{2160}{2160 + 6} = \frac{2160}{2166} = \mathbf{0.99723 = 99.72\%}$$

*(1 mark each for MTBF, MTTR, and $A_i$ — total 3 marks.)*

**Step 5 — Compare with Target (1 mark):**

Target $A_i \geq 99.5\%$. Achieved $A_i = 99.72\%$. **The current maintenance regime is technically MEETING the target**, but only marginally (margin of 0.22 percentage points). The trend is fragile.

**Step 6 — Corrective Action (3 marks):**

The **MTTR of 6 hours** is the lever to improve. Note that one failure took **10 hours** to repair (an outlier dragging the average up). Recommended actions:

1. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** on the 10-hour failure — investigate why repair time was so long. Was it a spare parts shortage? Skill gap? Severe failure mode?
2. **Condition Monitoring (Vibration + Thermography)** on the compressor bearings and seals to detect the failure mode earlier, allowing planned repairs during scheduled downtime.
3. **Critical Spare Parts Stocking** for high-MTTR items (impellers, seals, bearings) to eliminate logistics-induced repair delays.
4. **Cross-Skilling of Maintenance Crew** to reduce MTTR.

These actions would reduce MTTR toward 2–3 hours, pushing $A_i$ above 99.9% — a robustly effective maintenance regime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OEE with "Efficiency":** OEE is *productivity of good output vs. scheduled time*. Do NOT use OEE as a synonym for general efficiency. Many students write "OEE = good output / total output" — this is **Quality**, not OEE. Full marks lost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the /10000 in OEE:** When $A$, $P$, $Q$ are all in percent (e.g., 90, 95, 99), the product is $90 \times 95 \times 99 = 846450$, which represents *percent-squared-cubed*. Divide by $10000$ to get percent. Students who forget this get an OEE of "846450%" — automatic zero.
>
> 3. **Reporting Performance > 100% as 100% silently:** In KTU problems, always *show the calculation* and add a sentence: "Note: Performance > 100% in practice is often capped at 100% per SEMI E10 convention." This earns full credit and demonstrates depth.
>
> 4. **Using MTBF for non-repairable items:** The correct term for non-repairable items is **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**, not MTBF. Examiners check this distinction.
>
> 5. **Mixing Inherent, Achieved, and Operational Availability:** Students who write "Availability = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)" and call it "operational availability" lose 1 mark. **Inherent** Availability uses only corrective maintenance time; **Achieved** includes PM; **Operational** includes supply/admin delays.
>
> 6. **Skipping Units in the Final Answer:** Every numerical answer must have units (hours, %, etc.). "MTBF = 2160" is incomplete; "MTBF = 2160 hours" is correct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [Maintenance Effectiveness is a **composite metric** — never a single number. It blends availability, performance, quality, reliability, and cost.]
- [The most widely used operational index is **OEE = A × P × Q**, with world-class benchmark ≥ 85%.]
- [**Inherent Availability** is given by $A_i = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — this is the most-tested formula in KTU.]
- [MTBF measures **reliability**; MTTR measures **maintainability**; both are levers of effectiveness.]
- [The **Six Big Losses** of TPM provide a diagnostic taxonomy mapping to A, P, Q — favourite 14-mark question structure.]
- [Maintenance Cost Intensity should be **3%–5% of RAV** for best-in-class plants.]
- [Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER) = Benefit ÷ Cost should be **> 1** for effective maintenance.]
- [Distinguish **Effectiveness** (doing the right maintenance) from **Efficiency** (doing maintenance right).]
- [Performance factor in OEE is **capped at 100%** in industry per SEMI E10; in KTU calculations, show the value and note the cap.]
- [Reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ assumes constant failure rate — valid in the useful-life region of the bathtub curve.]
- [For non-repairable items, use **MTTF** in place of MTBF.]
- [Predictive Maintenance improves **Availability** and **Performance**; TPM and SMED target the Six Big Losses directly.]
- [RCA (Root Cause Analysis) and RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance) are the *highest-leverage* interventions for improving Maintenance Effectiveness in the long term.]
- [Always state **units** in numerical answers and **cite standards** (SEMI E10, ISO 14224) where applicable to earn full marks in KTU 2024 ESE.]
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** is the gold-standard TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) metric developed by Seiichi Nakajima (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance, 1988) that quantifies the *true productive effectiveness* of a manufacturing equipment by measuring its performance against its **designed capability**. Mathematically, OEE is the multiplicative product of three independent sub-efficiencies:

$$
\text{OEE} \;(\%) = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

Each factor is expressed as a **decimal fraction** (or percentage) bounded between **0% and 100%**. The metric isolates how much of the **Planned Production Time** is actually converted into **salable, defect-free output** at the **designed cycle speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** OEE is a core TPM measurement tool. KTU examiners frequently test the **multiplicative structure** (NOT additive) of OEE and the **identification of the Six Big Losses**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fuel Efficiency of a Machine"

Imagine you own a delivery truck rated to carry **1000 kg** at a top speed of **80 km/h** with a full tank of **50 litres** of diesel. Three things reduce its true usefulness:

1. **It stands idle in the garage half the day** (Availability Loss — *breakdowns*).
2. **It crawls at 40 km/h instead of 80 km/h** (Performance Loss — *reduced speed*).
3. **Half the boxes arrive damaged** (Quality Loss — *defects*).

The truck's *effective productivity* is a combined function of all three — **not their sum**. OEE works exactly the same way: it tells the maintenance manager what percentage of the *theoretical best* the equipment is actually delivering. A machine that is "running all the time" but slow and producing scrap is just as wasteful as one that breaks down frequently.

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class Benchmark (Nakajima):** A "world-class" OEE score is **≥ 85%** (which corresponds to only **15% total losses**). The typical global manufacturing average hovers around **60%**, meaning nearly **40% of productive capacity is wasted** — the exact scope TPM aims to recover.

### 1.3 Physical/Standard Constants

- **World-Class OEE Threshold:** **85%** (sometimes quoted as 85.6%)
- **Typical Industry Average:** **60%**
- **Loss-Free Operation (Theoretical):** **100%**
- **OEE Calculation Window:** Usually a single **shift** or **24-hour day**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of OEE — Conceptual Breakdown

OEE is built on a layered hierarchy. Each layer captures a specific category of **equipment losses**:

#### Pillar 1 — Availability
Measures the **percentage of Planned Production Time** that the equipment is *actually available* to run. Losses here are **downtime events**:
- Equipment Failures / Breakdowns
- Setup and Changeover Time (in some OEE implementations, setup is excluded from Planned Production Time)
- Planned Stops (in some implementations these are excluded)

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

#### Pillar 2 — Performance
Measures how *fast* the equipment is running compared to its **Ideal Cycle Time** (the theoretical minimum time to produce one part, as designed). Losses here are **speed losses**:
- Minor Stoppages (idling, sensor trips, blockages)
- Reduced Speed (running below design speed)

$$
\text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

#### Pillar 3 — Quality
Measures the percentage of produced pieces that meet **quality specifications** (i.e., are *good pieces*, not reworked or scrap). Losses here are **quality losses**:
- Startup Rejects (defects during warm-up)
- Production Rejects (defects during steady-state production)

$$
\text{Quality} = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
$$

### 2.2 The Six Big Losses (Nakajima's Loss Tree)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** The Six Big Losses map directly to the three OEE factors. Expect at least one **3-mark question** asking to list and classify them.

| # | Loss Category | Specific Loss | Affects OEE Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability Losses** | Equipment Failure (Breakdowns) | Availability ↓ |
| 2 | **Availability Losses** | Setup & Adjustment Losses | Availability ↓ |
| 3 | **Performance Losses** | Minor Stoppages & Idling | Performance ↓ |
| 4 | **Performance Losses** | Reduced Speed | Performance ↓ |
| 5 | **Quality Losses** | Startup Rejects (Defects) | Quality ↓ |
| 6 | **Quality Losses** | Production Rejects (Defects) | Quality ↓ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **OEE** | $\text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ | **%** (0 to 100) |
| 2 | **Availability** | $\frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 3 | **Performance** | $\frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 4 | **Quality** | $\frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 5 | **Planned Production Time** | $\text{Shift Time} - \text{Planned Stops}$ | **minutes** |
| 6 | **Operating Time** | $\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}$ | **minutes** |
| 7 | **Good Pieces** | $\text{Total Pieces} - \text{Defective Pieces}$ | **pieces** |
| 8 | **TEEP** *(Total Effective Equipment Performance)* | $\text{OEE} \times \text{Loading Factor}$ | **%** |
| 9 | **Loading Factor** | $\frac{\text{Planned Production Time}}{24 \text{ hours}}$ | **%** |
| 10 | **Ideal Cycle Time** | $\frac{1}{\text{Designed Output Rate}}$ | **seconds/piece** |
| 11 | **MTBF** (Bonus) | $\frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | **hours** |
| 12 | **MTTR** (Bonus) | $\frac{\text{Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | **hours** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** OEE is the single number plant managers monitor on dashboards. It is used in **lean manufacturing**, **Industry 4.0 smart factories**, **automotive assembly lines**, **pharmaceutical batch production**, and **semiconductor fabs** as a KPI to trigger maintenance interventions, kaizen events, and capital investment decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
> A CNC machine operates in a single 8-hour shift. Planned breaks consume 30 minutes. During the shift, the machine experiences two breakdowns totalling 45 minutes. The machine's **design cycle time is 30 seconds per piece**. During the shift, **540 pieces** were produced, out of which **30 were rejected** for quality defects. Calculate the OEE.

#### Step 1 — Determine Planned Production Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Production Time} &= \text{Shift Time} - \text{Planned Stops} \\
&= 8 \text{ hours} - 30 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 480 \text{ minutes} - 30 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 450 \text{ minutes} \quad (= 27{,}000 \text{ seconds})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We remove *planned* non-productive time (breaks, meetings) because OEE measures efficiency *relative to time we intended to produce*.

#### Step 2 — Determine Operating Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Time} &= \text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Unplanned Downtime} \\
&= 450 \text{ minutes} - 45 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 405 \text{ minutes} \quad (= 24{,}300 \text{ seconds})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Unplanned downtime (the two breakdowns) is an **Availability Loss** — it eats into the time the machine was *supposed* to be running.

#### Step 3 — Calculate Availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability} &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{405}{450} \times 100 \\
&= 90.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 10% of the planned production time was lost to breakdowns.

#### Step 4 — Calculate Performance

The total pieces produced = **540 pieces**.
The ideal cycle time = **30 seconds/piece**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Production Time} &= \text{Total Pieces} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time} \\
&= 540 \times 30 \text{ seconds} \\
&= 16{,}200 \text{ seconds} \quad (= 270 \text{ minutes})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Performance} &= \frac{\text{Theoretical Production Time}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{16{,}200}{24{,}300} \times 100 \\
&= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The machine was only producing at **two-thirds** of its design speed (due to minor stoppages, slow running, etc.) — a **massive Performance Loss**.

#### Step 5 — Calculate Quality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Good Pieces} &= \text{Total Pieces} - \text{Defective Pieces} \\
&= 540 - 30 = 510 \text{ pieces}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality} &= \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{510}{540} \times 100 \\
&= 94.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** About 5.56% of the output was scrap/rejects.

#### Step 6 — Final OEE Calculation (Multiplicative!)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality} \\
&= 0.9000 \times 0.6667 \times 0.9444 \\
&= 0.5667 \\
&= \mathbf{56.67\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly **add** the three factors (e.g., 90 + 66.67 + 94.44 = 251.11%). **This is wrong.** OEE is strictly **multiplicative** because the three factors measure *independent* loss categories. Mark deduction: **3 to 4 marks** if additive is used.

#### Step 7 — Analytical Loss Breakdown (Bonus — Often Asked)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Loss Percentage} &= 100\% - 56.67\% = 43.33\% \\
\text{Availability Loss Component} &= 100\% - 90.00\% = 10.00\% \\
\text{Performance Loss Component} &= 100\% - 66.67\% = 33.33\% \\
\text{Quality Loss Component} &= 100\% - 94.44\% = 5.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Performance Loss (33.33%)** dominates in this scenario. The maintenance team should prioritize investigating **minor stoppages** and **reduced-speed operation** rather than just breakdown repair. This is a classic **Pareto-based decision** TPM promotes.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Interpreting OEE Scores

| OEE Score | Interpretation | Recommended TPM Action |
|---|---|---|
| **< 40%** | **Critical** — equipment is in crisis state | Emergency autonomous maintenance training; basic 5S implementation |
| **40 – 60%** | **Typical / Average** — significant room for improvement | Focus on the *largest loss pillar*; quick-win kaizen |
| **60 – 75%** | **Acceptable** — common in many factories | Move towards planned maintenance; CBM introduction |
| **75 – 85%** | **Good** — competitive performance | Continuous improvement; MTBF maximization |
| **≥ 85%** | **World-Class** (Nakajima benchmark) | Maintain discipline; loss-tree reduction to zero |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — OEE Architecture & Loss Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Planned Production Time] --> B[Operating Time]
    A --> C[Downtime]
    B --> D[Actual Production Period]
    C --> C1[Equipment Failure]
    C --> C2[Setup and Adjustment]
    D --> D1[Minor Stoppages]
    D --> D2[Reduced Speed]
    D --> E[Total Pieces Produced]
    E --> E1[Good Pieces]
    E --> E2[Defective Pieces]

    A1[Availability = Operating Time / Planned Production Time] -.-> A
    B1[Performance = Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces / Operating Time] -.-> E
    C1[Quality = Good Pieces / Total Pieces] -.-> E1

    A --> F[OEE percent]
    A1 --> F
    B1 --> F
    C1 --> F
    F --> G{Availability x Performance x Quality}
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Six Big Losses Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Six Big Losses] --> P1[Availability Losses]
    L --> P2[Performance Losses]
    L --> P3[Quality Losses]

    P1 --> P1a[1. Equipment Failure]
    P1 --> P1b[2. Setup and Adjustment]

    P2 --> P2a[3. Minor Stoppages]
    P2 --> P2b[4. Reduced Speed]

    P3 --> P3a[5. Startup Rejects]
    P3 --> P3b[6. Production Rejects]

    P1a --> OEE1[Lowers OEE]
    P1b --> OEE1
    P2a --> OEE1
    P2b --> OEE1
    P3a --> OEE1
    P3b --> OEE1

    OEE1[OEE = Availability x Performance x Quality]
    OEE1 --> WC[World Class Target = 85 percent]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — OEE-Driven Maintenance Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Measure OEE Baseline] --> S2[Identify the Largest Loss Pillar]
    S2 --> S3{Which Loss Dominates?}
    S3 -->|Availability Loss| A1[Implement PM and Breakdown Analysis]
    S3 -->|Performance Loss| A2[Reduce Minor Stoppages via SMED and OJT]
    S3 -->|Quality Loss| A3[Root Cause Analysis on Defects and Poka Yoke]
    A1 --> S4[Re-measure OEE Monthly]
    A2 --> S4
    A3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5{OEE above 85 percent?}
    S5 -->|No| S2
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Maintain World Class via Autonomous Maintenance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). List the three factors on which OEE depends.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** OEE is a TPM metric that measures the percentage of planned production time that is *truly productive* — i.e., the time during which the equipment produces good pieces at its designed cycle speed. It is expressed as a percentage.
- **Three Factors (1.5 Marks):**
  1. **Availability** — actual running time vs planned time
  2. **Performance** — actual speed vs design speed
  3. **Quality** — good pieces vs total pieces produced

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the Six Big Losses of OEE as proposed by Nakajima.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Six Big Losses, classified by their parent OEE factor, are:

1. **Equipment Failure** *(Availability Loss)*
2. **Setup and Adjustment Losses** *(Availability Loss)*
3. **Minor Stoppages / Idling** *(Performance Loss)*
4. **Reduced Speed Losses** *(Performance Loss)*
5. **Startup Rejects** *(Quality Loss)*
6. **Production Rejects** *(Quality Loss)*

*(0.5 Marks per correct loss; 3 Marks for fully correct listing.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-3 question carries 14 marks. Either **Question A** *or* **Question B** must be answered (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OEE Calculation & Loss Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

A bottling plant operates two shifts of **8 hours each** (total 960 minutes). A scheduled lunch break of **40 minutes** per shift (total 80 minutes) is observed. The plant experiences the following in one day:

- **Total Unplanned Downtime** = **120 minutes** (machine jams and breakdowns)
- **Design Speed** = **120 bottles/minute**
- **Total Bottles Produced** = **60,000 bottles**
- **Defective Bottles** = **3,000 bottles**

**(a)** Calculate the **OEE** of the plant for the day. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Identify the **dominant loss category** and suggest **two TPM-based corrective actions** to reduce it. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Planned Production Time (1 Mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Production Time} &= 960 - 80 = 880 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Operating Time (1 Mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Time} &= 880 - 120 = 760 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Availability (1 Mark)**
$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{760}{880} \times 100 = \mathbf{86.36\%}
$$

**Step 4: Performance (2 Marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Output in 760 min} &= 120 \times 760 = 91{,}200 \text{ bottles} \\
\text{Performance} &= \frac{91{,}200}{60{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{151.82\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** When calculated naively, Performance may exceed 100% (151.82% here) because the actual production (60,000) is *less* than the ideal (91,200) — meaning the equipment is running **slower** than design. The correct interpretation: **Performance = 60,000 / 91,200 = 65.79%** (if the formula is inverted, since 60,000 pieces actually took 760 min, but *should* have taken 500 min at design speed). Use the **canonical formula**:
>
> $$
> \text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ sec} \times 60{,}000}{760 \times 60 \text{ sec}} = \frac{30{,}000}{45{,}600} = 65.79\%
> $$

**Corrected Performance = 65.79% (1 Mark — if marked wrong here, student loses the full 2 marks for Performance)**

**Step 5: Quality (1 Mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quality} &= \frac{60{,}000 - 3{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{57{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{95.00\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final OEE (1 Mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= 0.8636 \times 0.6579 \times 0.9500 \\
&= 0.5397 \\
&= \mathbf{53.97\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Dominant Loss Identification (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability Loss} &= 100 - 86.36 = 13.64\% \\
\text{Performance Loss} &= 100 - 65.79 = 34.21\% \\
\text{Quality Loss} &= 100 - 95.00 = 5.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**→ The DOMINANT loss is PERFORMANCE LOSS (34.21%)** — the plant is running at only ~66% of its design speed.

**Two TPM-Based Corrective Actions (2 + 2 = 4 Marks):**

1. **Implement SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die):** Reduces setup-related micro-stoppages and quick-changeover delays that bleed into performance time.
2. **Autonomous Maintenance by Operators (Jishu Hozen):** Train machine operators to perform routine cleaning, lubrication, and tightening — eliminates minor stoppages caused by jams and sensor trips.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* Deploy **OEE real-time dashboards (andon boards)** to immediately flag speed deviations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual & Analytical**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of OEE** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why OEE is considered a **multiplicative** (not additive) metric. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Differentiate between **OEE and TEEP (Total Effective Equipment Performance)**. With a suitable example, show how loading factor affects TEEP. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Concept of OEE (3 Marks):**
OEE is a composite TPM metric that measures how effectively a manufacturing equipment utilizes its planned production time by integrating three independent dimensions of equipment performance — *Availability* (running when needed), *Performance* (running at design speed), and *Quality* (producing conforming output). The metric, introduced by Seiichi Nakajima in 1988 under TPM, provides a single percentage figure that indicates the gap between *current performance* and *perfect performance* (100%).

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — Planned Production Time splits into Downtime and Operating Time → Total Pieces → split into Good/Defective — with the three ratios feeding into OEE.)*

**Why Multiplicative? (2 Marks):**
- The three OEE factors measure **independent, non-overlapping loss categories** (downtime, speed loss, defect loss).
- An additive formula (e.g., A + P + Q) would incorrectly allow a factor >100% to *compensate* for another factor <100% — which is physically impossible.
- Multiplicative structure ensures that **a single zero in any factor collapses the entire OEE to zero** — reflecting the real-world truth that a machine producing nothing (A=0) cannot contribute to performance or quality.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**OEE vs TEEP — Conceptual Difference (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | OEE | TEEP |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Base** | Planned Production Time | **24 hours / Total Calendar Time** (i.e., 24 × 60 = 1440 min) |
| **What it measures** | Efficiency *when scheduled to run* | Efficiency *across the entire day* (including non-scheduled hours) |
| **Includes Loading?** | No (assumes 100% loading) | **Yes** — incorporates the loading factor |
| **Typical Value** | 60 – 85% | 30 – 50% (always ≤ OEE) |
| **Use Case** | Operational excellence KPI | Strategic capacity-utilization KPI |

**Numerical Example (4 Marks):**
Given: $\text{OEE} = 56.67\%$ (from Section 3 example).
The plant runs only **one 8-hour shift per day**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loading Factor} &= \frac{\text{Planned Production Time}}{\text{Total Calendar Time}} \\
&= \frac{8 \text{ hours}}{24 \text{ hours}} = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TEEP} &= \text{OEE} \times \text{Loading Factor} \\
&= 0.5667 \times 0.3333 \\
&= \mathbf{18.89\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the machine operates at 56.67% efficiency *when scheduled*, the plant only uses 33.33% of the day — so the true 24-hour effectiveness is just 18.89%. Increasing TEEP requires running additional shifts (raising the loading factor), not just improving OEE.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Additive vs Multiplicative OEE (4-mark loss):** Adding A + P + Q is the **#1 mistake**. Always multiply.
> 2. **Performance Formula Inversion (2-mark loss):** Performance is *Actual Output / Theoretical Output* (or equivalent Ideal-Cycle-Time formulation). Confusing numerator/denominator gives >100% — examiner will penalize.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds (1-mark loss):** Cycle time and operating time must be in **matching units**.
> 4. **Confusing OEE and TEEP (2-mark loss):** TEEP includes loading; OEE does not. Don't use them interchangeably.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** *(multiplicative, never additive)*
- ✅ **World-Class Benchmark = 85%** *(Nakajima standard)*
- ✅ **Six Big Losses = 2 Availability + 2 Performance + 2 Quality losses**
- ✅ **Availability Loss = Downtime** (breakdowns + setup)
- ✅ **Performance Loss = Minor stoppages + Reduced speed**
- ✅ **Quality Loss = Startup rejects + Production rejects**
- ✅ **TEEP = OEE × Loading Factor** *(captures 24-hour effectiveness)*
- ✅ **Planned Production Time = Shift Time − Planned Stops** (e.g., breaks, meetings)
- ✅ **Operating Time = Planned Production Time − Unplanned Downtime**
- ✅ **Good Pieces = Total Pieces − Defective Pieces**
- ✅ **Ideal Cycle Time = 1 / Design Output Rate**
- ✅ **Typical OEE in industry = ~60%** → huge scope for TPM improvement
- ✅ **OEE drives TPM decisions** → measure the dominant loss pillar, then act via **PM, Autonomous Maintenance, SMED, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke**
- ✅ **OEE is calculated per shift / per day**, not per week, to enable rapid feedback
- ✅ **OEE ≥ 85%** = world-class; **OEE 40–60%** = typical (improvement opportunity zone)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance Planning** is the systematic pre-determination of *what* maintenance work is to be performed, *when* it should be executed, *how* it will be carried out, *who* will perform it, and *what resources* (spares, tools, budget, time) are required, in order to ensure equipment reliability, safety, and cost-effectiveness while minimizing downtime.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Maintenance Planning is the **"brain function"** of the Maintenance Management System (MMS). It bridges the gap between maintenance *policy* (formulated at the strategic level) and maintenance *execution* (carried out at the shop-floor level). The planning function converts a maintenance demand into a detailed, resourced, time-bound **Work Order** that can be executed efficiently.

The standard ISO 13373-1 and BS 3811 definitions describe planning as the determination of the **maintenance policy**, the **level of maintenance**, and the **resources required**, prior to the actual execution of maintenance work.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Appointment" Model

Think of Maintenance Planning as **preparing for a major surgery in a hospital**:

| Hospital Analogy | Maintenance Planning Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient's diagnosis | Equipment condition monitoring data |
| Pre-surgery tests | Pre-maintenance inspections & tests |
| Booking the operation theatre | Scheduling the maintenance shutdown |
| Surgeon + nurses | Skilled maintenance crew |
| Surgical instruments & implants | Spare parts, tools, consumables |
| OT booking date & duration | Work order with start/end dates |
| Pre-op patient preparation | Isolation, tagging, de-energization |
| Post-op recovery plan | Re-commissioning, handback, follow-up checks |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a hospital cannot afford to *improvise* during a critical surgery, a plant cannot afford unplanned maintenance. **Planning is what converts a chaotic emergency into a controlled, predictable process.**

### 1.3 Why Maintenance Planning is Critical

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO4 mapped):** Maintenance Planning directly contributes to **Equipment Availability**, **Plant Reliability**, **Safety**, and **Cost Optimization** — all of which are primary Course Outcomes for OEIET613 Module 4.

The standard benchmark figures used in industry are:
- **Planned maintenance is 3 to 4 times cheaper** than unplanned (breakdown) maintenance.
- **Planned downtime is typically 60–80% shorter** than unplanned downtime.
- Industry standards target **>85% Planned Maintenance** as a KPI (Key Performance Indicator).

### 1.4 Scope of Maintenance Planning

Maintenance Planning covers the following **five planning dimensions** (often called the **5 W's and 1 H** of planning):

1. **WHAT** — What task/job is to be done? (Task list, job scope)
2. **WHEN** — When will it be done? (Schedule, frequency, timing)
3. **WHO** — Who will do it? (Manpower, skills, contractor)
4. **HOW** — How will it be done? (Procedure, method, sequence)
5. **WITH WHAT** — With what resources? (Spares, tools, budget, materials)
6. **WHY** (optional 6th) — Why is this needed? (Justification, cost-benefit)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Maintenance Planning Hierarchy

Maintenance planning operates at **three hierarchical levels**, each with different time horizons and levels of detail:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   LEVEL 1: STRATEGIC (Long-term)            │
│   Horizon: 1–5 years                        │
│   Decides: Asset replacement, capital       │
│   budget, maintenance philosophy            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LEVEL 2: TACTICAL (Medium-term)           │
│   Horizon: 1–12 months                      │
│   Decides: Annual maintenance plan, major   │
│   overhauls, shutdown windows               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LEVEL 3: OPERATIONAL (Short-term)         │
│   Horizon: Days to weeks                    │
│   Decides: Daily/weekly job lists, work     │
│   orders, specific resource allocation      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** When examiners ask about *planning*, they usually refer to **Level 3 (Operational Planning)** — i.e., the preparation of work orders and weekly schedules.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Maintenance Planning

The maintenance planning cycle consists of the following **sequential logical steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Maintenance Demand**
   Triggered by:
   - Predictive maintenance (PdM) data (vibration, thermography, oil analysis)
   - Preventive maintenance (PM) schedule (calendar or usage-based)
   - Corrective maintenance request (breakdown or operator complaint)
   - Statutory / regulatory inspection requirement
   - Capital project handover

2. **Step 2 — Define the Job Scope & Task List**
   - Break the work into elementary tasks
   - Identify hidden work (scaffolding, insulation removal, safety preparation)
   - Estimate labour-hours per task
   - Specify quality acceptance criteria

3. **Step 3 — Determine the Required Resources**
   - **Manpower** — number, trade, skill level, contractor requirements
   - **Spare parts** — part numbers, quantities, lead times
   - **Consumables** — lubricants, gaskets, welding rods
   - **Tools & equipment** — special tools, lifting gear, test instruments
   - **Documentation** — drawings, manuals, permits-to-work

4. **Step 4 — Estimate Job Duration & Sequencing**
   - Critical path analysis for complex jobs
   - Identify parallel and sequential activities
   - Set start and finish dates

5. **Step 5 — Schedule the Job (Master Planning)**
   - Match jobs to available windows (shifts, planned shutdowns)
   - Resolve resource conflicts (same crew, same crane)
   - Coordinate with production / operations

6. **Step 6 — Issue the Work Order**
   - Formal authorization to proceed
   - Carry out **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**
   - Apply permit-to-work system

7. **Step 7 — Monitor, Control & Feedback**
   - Track actual vs. planned (labour, time, cost)
   - Capture lessons learned
   - Update planning data for future jobs

### 2.3 Types of Maintenance Plans

| Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Calendar-Based Plan** | Tasks triggered on fixed dates (e.g., every 6 months) | Routine servicing, statutory checks |
| **Usage-Based Plan** | Tasks triggered by running hours / cycles / km | Vehicle fleets, aircraft, pumps |
| **Condition-Based Plan** | Tasks triggered by monitored condition thresholds | Vibration, temperature, wear |
| **Predictive Plan** | Tasks triggered by trend analysis and forecasting | Critical rotating equipment |
| **Opportunity-Based Plan** | Tasks done when equipment is already down for other reasons | Combined maintenance windows |
| **Shutdown / Turnaround Plan** | Major consolidated maintenance during a planned plant stoppage | Refineries, large process plants |

### 2.4 Lead Time — The Critical Planning Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead Time** is the time between *initiating* a maintenance requirement and *executing* it. It consists of:
> 1. **Identification time** — Time to detect the need
> 2. **Decision time** — Time to approve the job
> 3. **Procurement time** — Time to obtain spares, materials, contractor
> 4. **Preparation time** — Time to gather tools, isolate, set up
> 5. **Execution time** — Time to actually do the work

> Effective maintenance planning aims to **reduce the aggregate lead time** by parallelizing the above sub-activities and pre-staging resources.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** that KTU 2024 examiners expect students to be able to write and apply for the topic of Maintenance Planning.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | % | Measures how often equipment is *ready* to operate |
| 2 | **Inherent Availability (Ai)** | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | decimal | Availability under ideal support conditions |
| 3 | **Operational Availability (Ao)** | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MLDT}$ | decimal | Includes Mean Logistics Downtime |
| 4 | **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric |
| 5 | **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Maintainability metric |
| 6 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | failures/hour | Frequency of failure |
| 7 | **Repair Rate (μ)** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{MTTR}$ | repairs/hour | Speed of restoration |
| 8 | **Reliability R(t)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ |
| 9 | **Manpower Loading (ML)** | $ML = \dfrac{\text{Total Man-Hours per Period}}{\text{Available Man-Hours per Period}}$ | % | Plans crew capacity utilization |
| 10 | **Spares Coverage (SC)** | $SC = \dfrac{\text{Number of Spare Lines in Stock}}{\text{Total Number of Critical Spare Lines}} \times 100\%$ | % | Measures readiness |
| 11 | **Maintenance Cost Ratio (MCR)** | $MCR = \dfrac{\text{Planned Maintenance Cost}}{\text{Total Maintenance Cost}} \times 100\%$ | % | Quality of planning |
| 12 | **Schedule Compliance (SCo)** | $SCo = \dfrac{\text{Work Orders Completed on Planned Date}}{\text{Total Work Orders Planned}} \times 100\%$ | % | Planning effectiveness |
| 13 | **Backlog (in hours)** | $BL = \text{Man-hours of approved but unexecuted work}$ | hours | Planning load indicator |
| 14 | **Planning Lead Time** | $LT_{plan} = T_{identify} + T_{decide} + T_{procure} + T_{prep}$ | hours/days | Time to convert demand into work order |
| 15 | **PM Compliance** | $PMC = \dfrac{\text{PM Jobs Done on Time}}{\text{Total PM Jobs Scheduled}} \times 100\%$ | % | Adherence to preventive schedule |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics are not just academic — they drive the day-to-day decisions of a maintenance engineer:

- **Availability targets** determine whether a plant needs a redundant pump or can run with a single unit.
- **MTBF** feeds into the **preventive maintenance interval calculation** using the **P-F interval** concept (the gap between the Potential failure point P and the Functional failure point F).
- **MLDT** is what differentiates a well-stocked, well-planned plant from one that suffers chronic downtime due to spares shortages.
- **Schedule Compliance** is the single most quoted KPI by senior management to assess the *maturity* of the maintenance function.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Failure Rate to PM Interval (Reliability Block)

The basis for preventive maintenance planning comes from the **bathtub curve** and the principle that wear-out failures increase with time. We want to overhaul **before** the wear-out region.

Let us derive the **optimal PM interval** using reliability mathematics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation below shows the formal link between **reliability engineering** and **maintenance planning** — a question KTU examiners frequently test under "explain the scientific basis of PM scheduling."

#### Step 1 — Define the time-to-failure probability density

Assume component failure follows an **exponential distribution** (constant failure rate in the useful life region):

$$f(t) = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

#### Step 2 — Define the reliability function

Reliability $R(t)$ is the probability of surviving up to time $t$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

#### Step 3 — Express failure rate in terms of MTBF

Since $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ in the constant-failure-rate region:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$$

#### Step 4 — Define the planned PM interval as $T_P$

If we want the system to survive until the next scheduled PM with a minimum acceptable reliability $R_{min}$:

$$R(T_P) = e^{-\lambda T_P} \geq R_{min}$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for the maximum allowable PM interval

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\lambda \, T_P \geq \ln(R_{min})$$

$$T_P \leq -\frac{\ln(R_{min})}{\lambda}$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda = 1/MTBF$

$$\boxed{\,T_P \leq -\,MTBF \times \ln(R_{min})\,}$$

#### Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a centrifugal pump has an $MTBF = 2000$ hours. The plant policy requires that the system should not fail between PMs with a reliability of at least $R_{min} = 0.95$. Find the maximum PM interval.

Substituting into the formula:

$$T_P \leq -2000 \times \ln(0.95)$$

Compute $\ln(0.95)$:

$$\ln(0.95) = -0.05129$$

Therefore:

$$T_P \leq -2000 \times (-0.05129)$$

$$T_P \leq 102.59 \text{ hours}$$

So the pump must be overhauled **at least every ~103 hours** to maintain 95% inter-PM reliability.

### 3.2 Derivation: Availability Trade-off for PM Interval Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation answers the classical planning question: *"How frequently should I do preventive maintenance?"* — too often wastes money, too rarely causes breakdown losses.

#### Step 1 — Define downtime cost

The **expected downtime per unit time** has two components:

1. **PM downtime** — scheduled, lower production loss
2. **CM (corrective maintenance) downtime** — unscheduled, higher loss

#### Step 2 — Express PM downtime rate

If PM is done every $T_P$ hours, the fraction of time spent in PM is:

$$D_{PM} = \frac{T_{PM}}{T_P}$$

where $T_{PM}$ = average duration of a single PM job.

#### Step 3 — Express CM downtime rate (using exponential reliability)

Number of failures per $T_P$ hours = $\lambda T_P$ (Poisson assumption)

Average CM downtime per failure = $MTTR$

Therefore:

$$D_{CM} = \lambda T_P \times MTTR$$

#### Step 4 — Total downtime fraction

$$D_{total} = D_{PM} + D_{CM} = \frac{T_{PM}}{T_P} + \lambda T_P \times MTTR$$

#### Step 5 — Minimize $D_{total}$ with respect to $T_P$

Differentiate with respect to $T_P$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(D_{total})}{dT_P} = -\frac{T_{PM}}{T_P^2} + \lambda \times MTTR = 0$$

#### Step 6 — Solve for the optimum PM interval

$$\frac{T_{PM}}{T_P^2} = \lambda \times MTTR$$

$$T_P^2 = \frac{T_{PM}}{\lambda \times MTTR}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{PM}}{\lambda \times MTTR}}\,}$$

This is the classical **Barlow-Hunter** formula for optimum preventive maintenance interval.

#### Step 7 — Numerical Example

Given: $T_{PM} = 4$ hours per PM, $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hr, $MTTR = 8$ hours.

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{0.0005 \times 8}}$$

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{0.004}}$$

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{1000}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{P,opt} \approx 31.6 \text{ hours}\,}$$

So the planner should schedule PM at approximately **every 32 running hours** to minimize total downtime.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Planning Decision Grid

The following matrix helps a maintenance planner decide the *type* and *frequency* of intervention based on equipment criticality and failure characteristics:

| Equipment Criticality \ Failure Mode | Sudden Failure | Slow / Wear-out Failure | Random / No Warning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Criticality** (safety, large production loss) | Frequent PM with high redundancy (Red + PM + CBM) | CBM / PdM with trend tracking | Redundancy + PdM |
| **Medium Criticality** | Periodic PM | Calendar / usage-based PM | Calendar-based PM |
| **Low Criticality** (no safety risk, cheap) | Run-to-failure (no PM) | Calendar PM | Run-to-failure |

> [!NOTE]
> This grid is essentially a **Risk-Based Maintenance Planning Matrix** and is widely used in ISO 55000-aligned asset management.

### 3.4 Manpower Planning Calculation (Workforce Loading)

A typical KTU-type short numerical on workforce planning:

> **Problem:** A maintenance department has 12 technicians, working one 8-hour shift, 26 days a month. The current backlog of approved work is 1500 man-hours. What is the manpower loading percentage and the backlog clearance time?

#### Step 1 — Available man-hours per month

$$MH_{avail} = 12 \times 8 \times 26 = 2496 \text{ man-hours/month}$$

#### Step 2 — Manpower Loading

$$ML = \frac{\text{Backlog}}{\text{Available Capacity}} = \frac{1500}{2496}$$

$$\boxed{\,ML \approx 60.1\%\,}$$

#### Step 3 — Backlog clearance time

If no new work arrives and 80% of capacity is available for backlog clearance:

$$T_{clear} = \frac{1500}{0.80 \times 2496}$$

$$T_{clear} = \frac{1500}{1996.8} \approx 0.751 \text{ months}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{clear} \approx 22.5 \text{ days}\,}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Maintenance Planning Process Flow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the complete sequential process of maintenance planning from demand identification to work order closure:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demand Identification] --> B[PM Schedule Trigger]
    A --> C[Breakdown Report]
    A --> D[Condition Monitoring Alert]
    A --> E[Statutory Inspection Due]
    A --> F[Project / Modification Need]

    B --> G[Job Scoping & Task Breakdown]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H[Resource Estimation]
    H --> H1[Manpower & Skills]
    H --> H2[Spare Parts]
    H --> H3[Tools & Equipment]
    H --> H4[Materials & Consumables]
    H --> H5[Safety & Permits]

    H1 --> I[Cost & Time Estimate]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I

    I --> J[Scheduling & Resource Leveling]
    J --> J1[Match to Available Window]
    J --> J2[Resolve Crew Conflicts]
    J --> J3[Coordinate with Production]

    J1 --> K[Work Order Generation]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K

    K --> L[Job Safety Analysis JSA]
    L --> M[Permit to Work Issuance]
    M --> N[Job Execution]
    N --> O[Quality Check & Re-commissioning]
    O --> P[Work Order Closure & Feedback]
    P --> Q[Update Maintenance Database]
    Q --> R[Lessons Learned Archive]
    R --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow `R → A` represents the **closed-loop feedback** — the hallmark of a mature maintenance planning system.

### 4.2 Maintenance Planning Hierarchy (Strategic → Tactical → Operational)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[LEVEL 1: STRATEGIC PLANNING 1 to 5 YEARS] --> L2[LEVEL 2: TACTICAL PLANNING 1 to 12 MONTHS]
    L2 --> L3[LEVEL 3: OPERATIONAL PLANNING DAYS to WEEKS]

    L1 --> S1[Asset Replacement Policy]
    L1 --> S2[Capital Maintenance Budget]
    L1 --> S3[Maintenance Philosophy Selection]

    L2 --> T1[Annual Maintenance Plan]
    L2 --> T2[Shutdown / Turnaround Schedule]
    L2 --> T3[Long Lead Spare Procurement]

    L3 --> O1[Weekly Job Schedule]

    L3 --> O3[Resource Allocation Sheets]

    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T2
    S3 --> T3
    T1 --> O1
    T2 --> O2
    T3 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CMMS-based Planning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        I1[PM Schedule Master]

    subgraph FEEDBACK_LAYER

    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1

    P1 --> P2

```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **digital backbone** of modern planning — a **Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)** or **Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)** platform such as SAP PM, IBM Maximo, or Infor EAM.

### 4.4 Risk-Based Maintenance Planning Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HIGH_CRITICALITY
        HC1[SUDDEN: Redundancy plus PM plus CBM]
        HC2[WEAR: CBM and PdM]
        HC3[RANDOM: Redundancy plus PdM]
    end
    subgraph MEDIUM_CRITICALITY
        MC1[SUDDEN: Calendar PM]
        MC2[WEAR: Calendar or Usage PM]
        MC3[RANDOM: Calendar PM]
```
