```mermaid
        LC1[SUDDEN: Run to Failure]
        LC2[WEAR: Calendar PM]
        LC3[RANDOM: Run to Failure]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEIET613]** Define Maintenance Planning. List the five planning elements (the 5 W's).

**Model Answer:**

Maintenance Planning is the systematic pre-determination of all resources and activities required to execute a maintenance job efficiently — encompassing *what, when, who, how,* and *with what* the work will be done.

**The five planning elements:**

1. **WHAT** — Scope of work / task list
2. **WHEN** — Schedule and timing
3. **WHO** — Manpower allocation
4. **HOW** — Procedure / method statement
5. **WITH WHAT** — Spares, tools, materials, budget

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEIET613]** What is meant by *Lead Time* in maintenance planning? Name its components.

**Model Answer:**

Lead time is the total elapsed time between the moment a maintenance need is identified and the moment the equipment is restored back to service.

**Components of lead time:**

1. Identification time
2. Decision / approval time
3. Procurement time (spares, materials)
4. Preparation time (tools, isolation, permits)
5. Execution time (actual repair/replacement)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEIET613 Module 4]** *(a)* Explain in detail the step-by-step process of maintenance planning with a suitable flow diagram. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A centrifugal pump has an MTBF of 3000 hours. The plant requires a minimum inter-PM reliability of 92%. Assuming constant failure rate, calculate the maximum PM interval. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Step-by-step process of maintenance planning (7 marks):**

1. **Demand Identification** — Triggered by PM schedule, breakdown, condition monitoring, statutory requirement, or project need.
2. **Job Scoping** — Break down the work into elementary tasks; identify hidden work and special precautions.
3. **Resource Estimation** — Determine manpower, spares, tools, consumables, and documentation.
4. **Time & Cost Estimation** — Estimate labour-hours, duration, and cost.
5. **Scheduling** — Match to available maintenance window, resolve resource conflicts.
6. **Work Order Issuance** — Formal authorization with permits and JSA.
7. **Monitoring & Feedback** — Track actual vs. planned, update database, capture lessons learned.

A suitable flowchart should accompany the answer (see Section 4.1).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $MTBF = 3000$ hours, $R_{min} = 0.92$.

The reliability-based PM interval formula is:

$$T_P \leq -MTBF \times \ln(R_{min})$$

Substituting:

$$T_P \leq -3000 \times \ln(0.92)$$

Compute $\ln(0.92) = -0.08338$:

$$T_P \leq -3000 \times (-0.08338)$$

$$T_P \leq 250.15 \text{ hours}$$

**Result:** The pump must be overhauled **at least every ~250 hours** to maintain 92% inter-PM reliability.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEIET613 Module 4]** *(a)* Discuss the various types of maintenance plans. Explain how a risk-based matrix is used to decide the type of plan for a given equipment. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Derive the Barlow–Hunter formula for the optimum preventive maintenance interval and apply it to the following data: $T_{PM} = 5$ hours, $\lambda = 0.0004$ failures/hr, $MTTR = 10$ hours. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Types of maintenance plans & risk-based matrix (7 marks):**

**Types:**
- Calendar-based PM
- Usage-based PM
- Condition-based / Predictive PM
- Opportunity-based PM
- Shutdown / Turnaround PM

**Risk-based decision matrix:** Planning is based on two axes — **Equipment Criticality** (High/Medium/Low) and **Failure Mode** (Sudden/Wear-out/Random). High-criticality equipment with sudden failure mode demands redundancy plus frequent PM plus CBM. Low-criticality equipment with sudden failure can be allowed to run-to-failure. (See Section 4.4 for visual matrix.)

**(b) Derivation of Barlow-Hunter Formula (7 marks):**

Total downtime per cycle:

$$D_{total} = \frac{T_{PM}}{T_P} + \lambda T_P \cdot MTTR$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $T_P$ and equate to zero:

$$-\frac{T_{PM}}{T_P^2} + \lambda \cdot MTTR = 0$$

$$T_P^2 = \frac{T_{PM}}{\lambda \cdot MTTR}$$

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_{PM}}{\lambda \cdot MTTR}}$$

**Numerical application:**

Given: $T_{PM} = 5$ hours, $\lambda = 0.0004$ failures/hr, $MTTR = 10$ hours.

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{5}{0.0004 \times 10}}$$

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{5}{0.004}}$$

$$T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{1250}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{P,opt} \approx 35.36 \text{ hours}\,}$$

So the planner should schedule PM at approximately **every 35 hours** of running time to minimize total downtime.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "Maintenance Planning" with "Maintenance Scheduling"** — Planning is the *preparation* (resource estimation, scoping); Scheduling is the *time-allocation* (placing it on the calendar). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation steps** — In numericals, students often jump directly to the final formula. The KTU key requires **explicit algebraic steps**; missing intermediate steps costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the units in the answer** — Always state the unit (hours, days, %) explicitly. A correct number without a unit is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **Misapplying Barlow-Hunter** — The formula assumes a constant failure rate. If the question mentions wear-out, the assumption breaks down and a modified formula or a P-F interval approach is needed.
> 5. **Ignoring the closed-loop feedback** — When asked about the planning process, students often stop at "work order issued." Always include the **feedback / lessons-learned** loop for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Maintenance Planning = pre-determination of WHAT, WHEN, WHO, HOW, WITH WHAT for a maintenance job.
- **Three Levels:** Strategic (years) → Tactical (months) → Operational (weeks/days).
- **Seven-Step Process:** Identify demand → Scope → Estimate resources → Estimate time/cost → Schedule → Issue work order → Monitor & feedback.
- **Lead Time** = Identification + Decision + Procurement + Preparation + Execution.
- **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Reliability-based PM interval:** $T_P \leq -MTBF \times \ln(R_{min})$.
- **Barlow-Hunter optimum PM interval:** $T_{P,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{PM}}{\lambda \times MTTR}}$.
- **Risk-based planning** uses a 2-axis matrix: Criticality × Failure Mode.
- **Key KPIs:** Schedule Compliance, PM Compliance, Maintenance Cost Ratio, Manpower Loading, Backlog in hours.
- **Planned vs Unplanned** — Industry target: **>85% planned maintenance** for mature plants.
- **CMMS / EAM** is the digital tool used to execute maintenance planning in modern plants.
- **Closed-loop feedback** is the hallmark of a mature planning system.
- **Always** include units, derivations, and a diagram in long-answer questions to score full marks.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic maps primarily to **CO4** — *Plan and schedule maintenance activities using appropriate tools and techniques.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure/Steps in Maintenance Planning, Maintenance Scheduling - Basic Techniques, Maintenance Organization - Factors Affecting Size and Type of Maintenance Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure/Steps in Maintenance Planning, Maintenance Scheduling - Basic Techniques, Maintenance Organization - Factors Affecting Size and Type of Maintenance Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Maintenance Planning — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Planning** is the *preparatory phase* of maintenance management that involves the systematic identification of maintenance tasks, estimation of resources (manpower, materials, tools, spares, time, and budget), determination of the sequence of operations, and documentation of procedures required to execute maintenance work efficiently, safely, and economically.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, maintenance planning is the **upstream intellectual work** that converts maintenance *policy* and *strategy* into actionable *work orders*. It is a **desk-based, paper-driven activity** done before any wrenches are turned on the shop floor.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of maintenance planning like planning a family wedding. You don't just wake up one morning and start cooking. You:
1. List all tasks (catering, decoration, invitations)
2. Decide *who* does *what* and *when*
3. Arrange resources (budget, vendors, time)
4. Document everything (itinerary, guest list)
5. Have a contingency plan (rain backup)

Maintenance planning is the industrial equivalent — a factory cannot "just do maintenance." It needs the same organized forethought.

---

## 1.2 Maintenance Scheduling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance Scheduling** is the *time-binding activity* that assigns specific dates, times, durations, and sequences to the planned maintenance jobs, synchronizing them with production schedules, resource availability, and operational priorities so as to minimize disruption and downtime.

While planning answers **"WHAT"** and **"HOW"**, scheduling answers **"WHEN"** and **"IN WHAT ORDER"**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Planning tells you "we must service 5 machines." Scheduling is the actual calendar entries — Machine A on Monday 9 AM, Machine B on Tuesday 2 PM, etc., making sure they don't clash with production deadlines.

---

## 1.3 Maintenance Organization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Organization** is the *human and structural framework* — the hierarchy of roles, responsibilities, reporting lines, departmental divisions, and coordination mechanisms — established to execute the maintenance function within an enterprise.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A maintenance organization is like the crew of a ship. The Captain (Maintenance Manager), Officers (Engineers), and Deck Crew (Technicians) all have defined roles. A small fishing boat (small factory) needs only 2–3 people; an aircraft carrier (large plant) needs hundreds organized in divisions like Engineering, Hull, Weapons, Supply.

---

## 1.4 Key Distinguishing Metrics in Maintenance

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — the average uptime between two consecutive breakdowns.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — the average downtime per failure.
- **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- **PM Compliance** — the percentage of planned preventive jobs completed on time.
- **Backlog** — total hours of approved but unfinished maintenance work.
- These metrics are typically tracked in **bold** as standard KTU board exam metrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Steps/Procedure in Maintenance Planning (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Maintenance planning is executed in a logical, sequential flow. The standard KTU-recognized 10-step procedure is as follows:

| Step # | Activity | Purpose / Output |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Identification of Equipment & Systems** | Prepare an asset register with criticality classification (A/B/C analysis) |
| 2 | **Collection of Technical Data** | Gather manuals, drawings, BOMs, OEM specs, historical failure records |
| 3 | **Listing of Maintenance Tasks** | Identify routine, preventive, predictive, and corrective tasks per asset |
| 4 | **Frequency Determination** | Decide task intervals (daily/weekly/monthly/yearly) based on OEM, usage, and failure history |
| 5 | **Resource Estimation** | Estimate manpower (man-hours), materials, spares, tools, and cost for each task |
| 6 | **Procedure / Job Plan Preparation** | Write standardized job cards, step-by-step work instructions, safety permits |
| 7 | **Spare Parts Planning** | Identify critical spares, set reorder levels (ROL), safety stock (SS), EOQ |
| 8 | **Workload Smoothing / Leveling** | Balance the maintenance workload across the planning horizon to avoid peaks |
| 9 | **Coordination with Production** | Align maintenance windows with production schedules (joint planning) |
| 10 | **Documentation & Review** | Maintain master plan, KPI tracking, post-job feedback for continuous improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Steps 5, 6, 7, and 9 are the most frequently asked as 14-mark questions. Always draw the **flow of information** from equipment identification → task list → job plan → work order → feedback loop.

---

## 2.2 Maintenance Scheduling — Basic Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Gantt Chart (Bar Chart) Scheduling

The simplest and oldest scheduling tool. Each maintenance job is represented as a **horizontal bar** plotted against a **time axis**. Length of bar = duration.

**Advantages:** Visual, simple, easy to communicate.
**Limitations:** Does not show interdependencies clearly; not suitable for complex networks.

### B. Line-of-Balance (LOB) Technique

Used when **repetitive identical jobs** must be performed on a large number of similar units (e.g., overhauling 50 identical pumps). The method balances the rate of resource consumption against the rate of completion.

Let:
- $N$ = number of identical units to overhaul
- $T_a$ = total time available (days/hours)
- $t_j$ = time to complete one unit
- $m$ = crew size

The required production rate is:

$$
R_p = \frac{N}{T_a}
$$

The crew output rate is:

$$
R_c = \frac{m \times 8}{t_j} \quad \text{(assuming 8-hr shift)}
$$

If $R_c > R_p$, schedule is feasible; otherwise, increase crew size $m$ or shift hours.

### C. Network Techniques — CPM (Critical Path Method)

CPM determines the **longest path** through a network of maintenance activities. Activities on the critical path have **zero float/slack** and any delay delays the whole shutdown.

**Key Network Parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_i &= \text{Earliest Start of activity } i \\
EF_i &= ES_i + D_i \quad (D_i = \text{Duration}) \\
LF_i &= LS_i + D_i \\
LS_i &= LF_i - D_i \\
TF_i &= LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i \quad (\text{Total Float}) \\
FF_i &= ES_{j} - EF_i \quad (\text{Free Float}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Critical Path** is the chain of activities where $TF = 0$.

### D. Network Techniques — PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique)

Used when activity durations are **uncertain (probabilistic)**. Three time estimates are used:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} \quad \text{(Expected time, beta distribution)}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \quad \text{(Standard deviation)}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Variance)}
$$

Where:
- $t_o$ = Optimistic time
- $t_m$ = Most likely time
- $t_p$ = Pessimistic time

### E. Priority Sequencing Rules

When multiple jobs compete for limited resources, common KTU-recognized rules include:
- **FCFS** (First Come First Served)
- **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time)
- **EDD** (Earliest Due Date)
- **SLACK** (Minimum Slack First)
- **CR** (Critical Ratio) = (Due Date – Today) / Remaining Processing Time

---

## 2.3 Maintenance Organization — Types & Structures

### 2.3.1 Centralized Maintenance Organization

All maintenance personnel, workshops, stores, and tools report to a **single Maintenance Manager** at the top. All decisions flow from one point.

**Best for:** Small to medium plants with one location.
**Advantage:** Economy of scale; uniform standards.
**Disadvantage:** Slow response to distant units.

### 2.3.2 Decentralized Maintenance Organization

Each production unit/division has its **own maintenance team** reporting to a divisional production head, with only policy guidance from a corporate maintenance chief.

**Best for:** Large plants, multi-location companies.
**Advantage:** Faster local response; better ownership.
**Disadvantage:** Duplication; inconsistent standards.

### 2.3.3 Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organization

A **matrix structure** combining centralized policy/standards with decentralized execution. Common in modern KTU-context industries.

### 2.3.4 Functional vs. Divisional vs. Matrix Structures

| Structure | Description | When Used |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Functional** | Grouped by skill (mechanical, electrical, instrumentation) | Specialized plants |
| **Divisional** | Grouped by product/area | Multi-product companies |
| **Matrix** | Dual reporting (functional + project) | Shutdowns, projects |

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Size and Type of Maintenance Organization

The **size** (number of people) and **type** (structure) of a maintenance organization depend on multiple inter-related factors:

| Factor | Effect on Size | Effect on Type |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Plant Size & Capacity** | Larger plant → larger crew | Large plants prefer decentralized |
| **Plant Age & Condition** | Older plant → larger crew (more failures) | May need strong corrective team |
| **Type of Industry** | Process industries need more staff | Continuous-process favors centralized on-site |
| **Degree of Automation** | High automation → fewer but more skilled | Centralized specialized teams |
| **Number of Equipment** | More equipment → more manpower | Centralized stores/workshops |
| **Criticality of Equipment** | High-criticality → standby crews | Centralized emergency response |
| **Geographic Dispersion** | Multi-location → duplicated teams | Forces decentralized structure |
| **Availability of Spare Parts** | Poor spares → more repair time → more people | May require on-site workshops |
| **Skill Level of Workforce** | Higher skill → smaller crew possible | Allows functional specialization |
| **Maintenance Strategy** | Predictive/PM heavy → planned crew | Centralized planning cell needed |
| **Production Schedule (24×7 vs 8-hr)** | Continuous ops → shift crews | Strong shift organization |
| **Budget Constraints** | Limited budget → lean organization | May outsource (contract maintenance) |
| **Safety/Statutory Requirements** | Hazardous plants → more safety staff | Need dedicated safety cell |
| **Management Philosophy** | TPM culture → operator involvement | Flat, participative structure |
| **Outsourcing Policy** | High outsourcing → small core team | Contract management cell needed |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) or % |
| 2 | Line-of-Balance Required Rate | $R_p = \dfrac{N}{T_a}$ | Units per day/hour |
| 3 | Line-of-Balance Crew Rate | $R_c = \dfrac{m \times H}{t_j}$ | Units per day/hour |
| 4 | CPM Earliest Finish | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Days/Hours |
| 5 | CPM Latest Start | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Days/Hours |
| 6 | CPM Total Float | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Days/Hours |
| 7 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Days/Hours |
| 8 | PERT Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | Days/Hours |
| 9 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | (Days/Hours)² |
| 10 | Critical Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{DD - Today}{RP}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Reorder Level (ROL) | $ROL = \text{Max daily usage} \times \text{Lead time}$ | Units |
| 12 | Safety Stock | $SS = z \times \sigma_{LT}$ | Units |
| 13 | EOQ (Economic Order Qty) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Units/Order |
| 14 | Maintenance Crew Size (basic) | $m = \dfrac{\sum \text{Man-hours per period}}{\text{Available hours per worker}}$ | Whole number |
| 15 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Formulas 1, 4–7, 11, 13 are the most frequently tested. Always state the **units** explicitly — a common reason for losing marks is forgetting units in numerical problems.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPM/PERT** is used for plant shutdowns and overhauls where hundreds of activities must be coordinated.
- **Gantt charts** dominate day-to-day weekly maintenance planning in manufacturing.
- **LOB** is critical in airline base maintenance where 30+ identical aircraft must be C-checked per year.
- The **maintenance organization** choice directly affects KPIs like MTTR, downtime cost, and PM compliance.
- In **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** environments, a hybrid structure with operator-driven daily maintenance + specialized centralized teams for complex work is the gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example — CPM Calculation on a Plant Shutdown

A fertilizer plant is shut down for 14 days. The following maintenance activities are identified:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (Days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 3 |
| B | — | 4 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 6 |
| E | B | 4 |
| F | B | 7 |
| G | C, D | 5 |
| H | E | 6 |
| I | F, G, H | 4 |

**Step 1: Forward Pass (Earliest Times)**

For each activity, $ES_i$ = maximum $EF$ of all predecessors. $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A: &\quad ES_A = 0,\ EF_A = 0 + 3 = 3 \\
B: &\quad ES_B = 0,\ EF_B = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
C: &\quad ES_C = EF_A = 3,\ EF_C = 3 + 5 = 8 \\
D: &\quad ES_D = EF_A = 3,\ EF_D = 3 + 6 = 9 \\
E: &\quad ES_E = EF_B = 4,\ EF_E = 4 + 4 = 8 \\
F: &\quad ES_F = EF_B = 4,\ EF_F = 4 + 7 = 11 \\
G: &\quad ES_G = \max(EF_C, EF_D) = \max(8, 9) = 9,\ EF_G = 9 + 5 = 14 \\
H: &\quad ES_H = EF_E = 8,\ EF_H = 8 + 6 = 14 \\
I: &\quad ES_I = \max(EF_F, EF_G, EF_H) = \max(11, 14, 14) = 14,\ EF_I = 14 + 4 = 18 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project completion time** = $EF_I = 18$ days.

**Step 2: Backward Pass (Latest Times)**

For the final activity I, $LF_I = EF_I = 18$, then $LS_I = 18 - 4 = 14$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I: &\quad LF_I = 18,\ LS_I = 14 \\
H: &\quad LF_H = LS_I = 14,\ LS_H = 14 - 6 = 8 \\
G: &\quad LF_G = LS_I = 14,\ LS_G = 14 - 5 = 9 \\
F: &\quad LF_F = LS_I = 14,\ LS_F = 14 - 7 = 7 \\
E: &\quad LF_E = LS_H = 8,\ LS_E = 8 - 4 = 4 \\
D: &\quad LF_D = LS_G = 9,\ LS_D = 9 - 6 = 3 \\
C: &\quad LF_C = LS_G = 9,\ LS_C = 9 - 5 = 4 \\
B: &\quad LF_B = \min(LS_E, LS_F) = \min(4, 7) = 4,\ LS_B = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
A: &\quad LF_A = \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(4, 3) = 3,\ LS_A = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Float Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_A &= LS_A - ES_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
TF_B &= LS_B - ES_B = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
TF_C &= LS_C - ES_C = 4 - 3 = 1 \\
TF_D &= LS_D - ES_D = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
TF_E &= LS_E - ES_E = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
TF_F &= LS_F - ES_F = 7 - 4 = 3 \\
TF_G &= LS_G - ES_G = 9 - 9 = 0 \\
TF_H &= LS_H - ES_H = 8 - 8 = 0 \\
TF_I &= LS_I - ES_I = 14 - 14 = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Identify Critical Path**

Activities with $TF = 0$: **A, B, D, G, H, I**

Following predecessor links: B → E → H → I and A → D → G → I.

The longer critical path is **A → D → G → I** with duration = 3 + 6 + 5 + 4 = **18 days**.

Alternative critical path B → E → H → I = 4 + 4 + 6 + 4 = **18 days** (also critical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The shutdown *must* be planned for at least 18 days. Activities C (float = 1 day) and F (float = 3 days) can be delayed up to their floats without delaying the whole project.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — PERT Expected Time & Project Variance

For a maintenance project of three activities in sequence: I → J → K

| Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| I | 3 | 5 | 11 |
| J | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| K | 5 | 8 | 17 |

**Step 1: Expected time for each activity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{e,I} &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 11}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 11}{6} = \frac{34}{6} \approx 5.67 \text{ days} \\
t_{e,J} &= \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00 \text{ days} \\
t_{e,K} &= \frac{5 + 4(8) + 17}{6} = \frac{5 + 32 + 17}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9.00 \text{ days} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Expected project duration**

$$
T_E = t_{e,I} + t_{e,J} + t_{e,K} = 5.67 + 4.00 + 9.00 = 18.67 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3: Variance of each activity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_I^2 &= \left(\frac{11 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.78 \\
\sigma_J^2 &= \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.44 \\
\sigma_K^2 &= \left(\frac{17 - 5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = (2)^2 = 4.00 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Project variance and std deviation (sequential critical path)**

$$
\sigma^2_{project} = \sigma_I^2 + \sigma_J^2 + \sigma_K^2 = 1.78 + 0.44 + 4.00 = 6.22
$$

$$
\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{6.22} \approx 2.49 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5: Probability of completion within 22 days**

$$
Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma_{project}} = \frac{22 - 18.67}{2.49} = \frac{3.33}{2.49} \approx 1.34
$$

From standard normal tables, $P(Z \le 1.34) \approx 0.9099$ ≈ **91% probability** of finishing within 22 days.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Maintenance Crew Sizing

A plant has **120 preventive maintenance jobs** scheduled in the next quarter (90 days). Average job duration = **4 hours**. Standard shift = **8 hours/day**, 22 working days/month → **66 days** in the quarter. Apply a **20% contingency factor** for breakdowns, emergencies, and absenteeism.

**Step 1: Total man-hours required**

$$
MH_{required} = 120 \times 4 = 480 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 2: Add contingency**

$$
MH_{actual} = 480 \times 1.20 = 576 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 3: Available hours per technician per quarter**

$$
H_{available} = 66 \times 8 = 528 \text{ hours/technician}
$$

**Step 4: Crew size**

$$
m = \frac{MH_{actual}}{H_{available}} = \frac{576}{528} \approx 1.09
$$

Round up → **2 technicians** for PM execution (excluding supervisory and breakdown crew).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Scheduling Techniques

| Criterion | Gantt Chart | Line-of-Balance | CPM | PERT |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|-----|------|
| Data type | Deterministic | Repetitive units | Deterministic | Probabilistic |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Best for | Daily/weekly plans | Repetitive jobs (aircraft) | One-time shutdowns | R&D, first-time jobs |
| Shows interdependencies | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Crashing/cost analysis | No | No | Yes | Limited |
| Probability of completion | No | No | No | Yes |
| KTU exam weightage | Low | Medium | **High** | **High** |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Maintenance Organization Type Selection

| Industry Type | Recommended Structure | Reason |
|---------------|----------------------|--------|
| Small single-product factory | Centralized, functional | Economy, simplicity |
| Multi-location utility | Decentralized, divisional | Local responsiveness |
| Continuous process (refinery) | Centralized on-site + matrix for shutdowns | Reliability-critical |
| Discrete manufacturing (auto) | Hybrid with TPM cells | Operator involvement |
| R&D / pilot plants | Project-based matrix | Flexibility |
| Construction equipment maintenance | Decentralized mobile crews | Geographic spread |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **six steps** involved in maintenance planning.

**Model Answer (6 points, expanded):**
1. Identification of equipment and systems to be maintained.
2. Collection of technical data (manuals, drawings, OEM specs).
3. Listing of maintenance tasks (routine, preventive, predictive, corrective).
4. Determination of task frequencies.
5. Estimation of resources (manpower, materials, spares, time, cost).
6. Preparation of job plans and work instructions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** What is a **Gantt chart**? Mention two advantages and two limitations.

**Model Answer:**
A Gantt chart is a horizontal bar chart used for maintenance scheduling in which each maintenance activity is plotted against time on the X-axis. The length of each bar represents the duration of the activity.

*Advantages:*
1. Simple and easy to construct.
2. Visually shows the schedule and overlapping tasks clearly.

*Limitations:*
1. Does not clearly show interdependencies between activities.
2. Becomes cumbersome and cluttered for large projects.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* Explain in detail the **various steps involved in maintenance planning**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **basic techniques of maintenance scheduling** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) Steps in Maintenance Planning (7 Marks)

Maintenance planning is a systematic preparatory activity that converts maintenance policy into actionable work orders. The steps are:

1. **Equipment Identification and Criticality Analysis:** Prepare an asset register. Classify equipment as A (critical), B (important), C (general) using criteria like downtime cost, safety impact, and production loss.

2. **Data Collection:** Gather technical data — operating manuals, P&IDs, electrical drawings, BOMs, OEM maintenance recommendations, and historical failure records.

3. **Task Listing:** Identify all maintenance activities — routine cleaning, lubrication, inspection, calibration, part replacement, overhauls.

4. **Frequency Setting:** Decide intervals — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annually — based on OEM guidelines, MTBF, operating hours, and failure history.

5. **Resource Estimation:** Estimate man-hours, skills required, spare parts, tools, and budget for each task.

6. **Job Plan Preparation:** Prepare standard job cards containing job description, tools, spares, safety precautions, estimated time, and quality checks.

7. **Spare Parts Planning:** Determine reorder levels, safety stock, and EOQ for critical spares. Coordinate with stores.

8. **Workload Leveling:** Distribute workload evenly across the planning horizon to avoid resource bottlenecks.

9. **Coordination with Production:** Joint planning with production so that maintenance windows align with low-load periods or planned shutdowns.

10. **Documentation and Feedback:** Maintain master plan, work orders, completion records, and use KPIs (PM compliance, backlog) for continuous improvement.

*(Examiner will award 7 marks typically as: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per key step × 6–7 steps)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) Basic Techniques of Maintenance Scheduling (7 Marks)

The common techniques used for scheduling maintenance work are:

1. **Gantt Chart (Bar Chart):** A simple visual tool where each maintenance activity is shown as a horizontal bar plotted against a time axis. Suitable for routine weekly planning. Example: scheduling 10 PM jobs over a week.

2. **Line-of-Balance (LOB) Technique:** Used when many identical units (e.g., 50 motors) require the same maintenance. It balances the rate of work output against resource availability. Required production rate $R_p = N/T_a$, crew output rate $R_c = mH/t_j$. Schedule is feasible if $R_c \ge R_p$.

3. **Critical Path Method (CPM):** Used for complex shutdowns and overhauls. Activities are represented as a network with arrows/nodes. The longest path (critical path) determines the minimum project duration. Activities on it have zero float and any slippage delays the whole project.

4. **Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT):** Used when activity durations are uncertain. Three-time estimates ($t_o$, $t_m$, $t_p$) are used to compute expected time $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ and variance $\sigma^2 = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^2$. Gives probability of meeting deadline.

5. **Priority Sequencing Rules:** When multiple jobs compete for limited resources, rules like FCFS, SPT, EDD, SLACK, and Critical Ratio (CR = (Due Date – Today)/Remaining Processing Time) are used to decide which job goes first.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(a)* Explain the **various types of maintenance organization**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **factors affecting the size and type of maintenance organization**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) Types of Maintenance Organization (7 Marks)

1. **Centralized Maintenance Organization:** All maintenance personnel, workshops, stores, and tools are under one Maintenance Manager. Decisions, standards, and resources are controlled centrally. Best suited for small to medium single-location plants. Advantage: uniform standards, economy of scale. Disadvantage: slow response time.

2. **Decentralized Maintenance Organization:** Each plant division or location has its own maintenance team reporting locally, with policy guidance from a corporate maintenance chief. Suitable for multi-location, large, or geographically dispersed companies. Advantage: faster response, better local ownership. Disadvantage: duplication of resources, inconsistent standards.

3. **Combined / Hybrid Organization:** A matrix structure combining centralized planning, standards, and specialized support with decentralized execution teams. Most modern process plants use this. Allows best of both worlds.

4. **Functional vs. Divisional vs. Matrix Structures:**
   - *Functional:* Crews grouped by trade (mechanical, electrical, instrumentation).
   - *Divisional:* Crews grouped by plant area or product.
   - *Matrix:* Dual reporting — to functional head (e.g., Electrical Engineer) and to project/shutdown manager.

5. **TPM-based Organization:** Operators perform routine maintenance; specialized teams handle complex work. Maintenance organization is flat and integrated with production.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) Factors Affecting Size and Type (7 Marks)

The size (number of personnel) and type (structure) of a maintenance organization are influenced by:

1. **Plant Size and Capacity:** Larger plants need larger maintenance teams.
2. **Age and Condition of Plant:** Older plants need more corrective crews.
3. **Type of Industry:** Process industries (refineries, power) require larger dedicated teams.
4. **Degree of Automation:** Highly automated plants need fewer but more skilled staff.
5. **Criticality of Equipment:** Plants with critical equipment need standby crews.
6. **Geographic Dispersion:** Multi-location plants need decentralized teams.
7. **Operating Hours (Shift Pattern):** 24×7 plants need shift-based staffing.
8. **Maintenance Strategy:** Predictive/PM-based plants need strong planning cells.
9. **Skill Availability:** Higher skill level → smaller crew.
10. **Outsourcing Policy:** High outsourcing → small core team + contract cell.
11. **Budget:** Limited budget forces lean organizations.
12. **Safety and Statutory Requirements:** Hazardous industries need dedicated safety staff.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing Planning with Scheduling:** Planning answers WHAT and HOW; Scheduling answers WHEN and IN WHAT ORDER. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems:** Always write units (days, hours, man-hours) — KTU board examiners award zero marks even for a correct number without units.
> 3. **Drawing CPM Networks Without Arrows:** Use proper arrow/activity notation. Don't show mere boxes — indicate precedence clearly.
> 4. **Listing Factors Without Grouping:** Don't write 15 factors in a single paragraph. Group them into *Internal (Plant-related)* and *External (Policy/Environment-related)* for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Feedback Loops in Planning:** Always mention that planning is iterative and includes a feedback/review step (Step 10). Examiners specifically look for this.
> 6. **Forgetting Float Calculation in CPM:** Students often find the critical path but forget to compute Total Float for non-critical activities — losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

**Maintenance Planning:**
- Planning is the *preparatory* phase; scheduling is the *time-binding* phase.
- The **10-step procedure** is the KTU-standard answer template.
- Key inputs: equipment list, OEM data, failure history.
- Key outputs: job plans, work orders, resource estimates.
- Always includes a **feedback loop** for continuous improvement.

**Maintenance Scheduling — Basic Techniques:**
- **Gantt Chart** → simple, visual, for daily/weekly plans.
- **Line-of-Balance (LOB)** → repetitive identical jobs (aircraft, fleets).
- **CPM** → deterministic durations, single-time estimates; finds critical path (TF = 0).
- **PERT** → probabilistic; uses $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ and $\sigma^2 = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^2$.
- **Priority Rules** → FCFS, SPT, EDD, SLACK, CR for resource-constrained jobs.

**CPM Key Formulas:**
- $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$
- $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$
- $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$
- Critical Path = chain with TF = 0.

**PERT Key Formulas:**
- $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$
- $\sigma = (t_p - t_o)/6$
- $Z = (T_S - T_E)/\sigma_{project}$

**Maintenance Organization Types:**
- Centralized, Decentralized, Hybrid (Combined), Functional, Divisional, Matrix.

**Key Factors Affecting Size & Type:**
- Plant size, age, automation, criticality, geography, shifts, strategy, skill, budget, safety, outsourcing.

**Golden Numbers to Memorize:**
- **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- **EOQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$.
- **Critical Ratio** = (Due Date – Today) / Remaining Processing Time.
- **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality.

**Exam Pattern Reminder (KTU 2024):**
- Part A: 2 × 3 = 6 marks (definitions, short lists).
- Part B: 1 × 14 marks with internal choice (deep answer, often with a small numerical in PERT/CPM).
- **Total Module Weight:** ~20% of course grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Objectives and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Maintenance Planning and Scheduling** is the systematic, scientific, and proactive engineering discipline of determining *what* maintenance work must be done, *when* it must be done, *how* it will be accomplished, *who* will perform it, and *what resources* (spares, tools, budget) are required — all in advance of execution, to maximize equipment availability, reliability, and safety while minimizing lifecycle cost.

**Objectives** are the *quantifiable end-goals* the maintenance function aims to achieve through its plans (e.g., 95% availability, MTTR < 2 hours).

**Characteristics** are the *intrinsic qualities or attributes* that distinguish a well-engineered maintenance plan/schedule from a poorly designed one (e.g., flexibility, foresight, resource adequacy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Box**
> **Planning** = deciding *what* and *how* (intellectual, desk-based activity)
> **Scheduling** = deciding *when* and *who* (administrative, calendar-based activity)
> *Planning precedes Scheduling. Scheduling is the time-bound execution arm of a Plan.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🏠 Real-World Analogy: Planning a Family Wedding**
>
> Imagine you are organizing a wedding. You would never just show up at the venue and decide things on the spot. You would:
> 1. **Plan** (Months Before): Decide the menu, guest list, venue, budget, photographers.
> 2. **Schedule** (Days Before): Make a *timetable* — 6 AM pandit arrives, 7 AM breakfast, 8 AM mandap setup, etc., assigned to specific people.
>
> A sudden illness of the cook, a missing photographer, or a forgotten ritual = **Maintenance Emergency** (Breakdown).
>
> A good plan + schedule = *the wedding runs smoothly despite surprises*. This is exactly what **Maintenance Planning & Scheduling** does for industrial plant equipment.

**Maintenance Planning** in a factory = the *strategic wedding plan*.
**Maintenance Scheduling** in a factory = the *wedding-day timetable*.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Industry Standards)

The following metrics define the *Objectives* numerically:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Industrial Target |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment Availability | **A** | **≥ 95%** |
| Mean Time To Repair | **MTTR** | **< 2 hours** |
| Mean Time Between Failures | **MTBF** | **Maximize** |
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | **OEE** | **≥ 85% (World Class)** |
| Planned Maintenance % | **% PM** | **≥ 90%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, when asked to "list the objectives," always link the objective to its **metric** (e.g., *Objective: Maximize availability → Metric: ≥95%*).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Maintenance Planning

Maintenance Planning is the *preparatory backbone* of the entire maintenance function. Without a sound plan, scheduling becomes guesswork.

### The 7 Core Objectives (KTU-Standard)

1. **To Ensure Equipment Availability & Reliability**
   - Minimize unscheduled downtime.
   - Make the plant available *when production needs it*.

2. **To Optimize Maintenance Cost (Lifecycle Cost Minimization)**
   - Avoid premature replacement (wasted CAPEX) and late replacement (catastrophic failure loss).
   - Achieve the **lowest Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**.

3. **To Ensure Safety of Personnel, Equipment, and Environment**
   - Comply with statutory regulations (Factories Act, OSHA).
   - Prevent accidents caused by equipment failure.

4. **To Optimize Use of Resources (Manpower, Spares, Tools)**
   - Pre-arrange spares so that technicians are not idle waiting for parts.
   - **The 6 Rs of Spares**: Right spares, Right quantity, Right quality, Right place, Right time, Right cost.

5. **To Extend Useful Life of Equipment (Asset Life Extension)**
   - Proactive care slows the wear-out curve.

6. **To Improve Product Quality**
   - A well-maintained machine produces within tighter tolerances.

7. **To Provide Documentation for Legal, ISO 9001/14001/45001 Audit Compliance**
   - Auditable records of *what was planned, executed, and verified*.

## 2.2 Objectives of Maintenance Scheduling

Scheduling *operationalizes* the plan on a calendar. Its objectives are more tactical:

1. **To Allocate Specific Time Windows for Maintenance** without disturbing production.
2. **To Assign Specific Skilled Manpower** to specific jobs.
3. **To Coordinate Multiple Disciplines** (mechanical, electrical, instrumentation) on the same machine during the same shutdown window.
4. **To Provide Advance Notice** to production, stores, and contractors.
5. **To Enable Performance Measurement** (backlog hours, schedule compliance %).

## 2.3 Characteristics of a Good Maintenance Plan

A *good plan* has the following inherent attributes:

| # | Characteristic | Meaning in Engineering Terms |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Foresight** | Predicts failures and prepares in advance |
| 2 | **Flexibility** | Can accommodate emergency insertions |
| 3 | **Feasibility** | Realistic with available resources |
| 4 | **Economy** | Cost-effective in long term |
| 5 | **Safety** | Incorporates LOTO, PPE, risk assessment |
| 6 | **Comprehensiveness** | Covers all equipment hierarchies |
| 7 | **Documentation** | Every step is recorded |

## 2.4 Characteristics of a Good Maintenance Schedule

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Realistic Time Estimates** | Based on standard job times, not guesses |
| 2 | **Resource-Loaded** | Spares, tools, manpower pre-arranged |
| 3 | **Coordination-Ready** | Synced with production schedule |
| 4 | **Priority-Based** | Criticality ranking applied (A-B-C analysis) |
| 5 | **Contingency-Bearing** | Includes buffer time for unforeseen issues |
| 6 | **Communicable** | Clear job cards to technicians |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** and frequently tested in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Equipment Availability (A)} \quad A &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Operating Time + Downtime}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\textbf{2. MTBF} \quad \text{MTBF} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{3. MTTR} \quad \text{MTTR} &= \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{4. OEE} \quad \text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
\quad \text{where } P &= \text{Performance}, \; Q = \text{Quality} \\[4pt]
\textbf{5. Schedule Compliance (SC)} \quad SC &= \frac{\text{Jobs Completed on Time}}{\text{Total Scheduled Jobs}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\textbf{6. PM Ratio} \quad \text{PM\%} &= \frac{\text{Planned Maintenance Hours}}{\text{Total Maintenance Hours}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\textbf{7. Backlog (in hours)} \quad BL &= \sum \text{Estimated Hours of Pending Jobs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Conditions & Units:**

| Formula | Valid Range | Units | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $0 \le A \le 100$ | % | Use **Operating Time**, not Calendar Time |
| MTBF | $\text{MTBF} > 0$ | hours | Exclude downtime from numerator |
| MTTR | $\text{MTTR} > 0$ | hours | Includes *all* downtime (logistic + repair) |
| OEE | $0 \le \text{OEE} \le 100$ | % | World-class benchmark is **85%** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Refineries** use these metrics to plan turnaround shutdowns every 4-6 years.
- **Aircraft Maintenance** (MRO hangars) schedules by *flying-hour-based* planning — a textbook example of planning tied to MTBF.
- **Power Plants** achieve >95% availability through disciplined planning, directly impacting per-unit cost of electricity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Availability, MTBF, and MTTR

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 4.

### Given
Let:
- $T_o$ = Total Operating Time (hours)
- $T_d$ = Total Downtime (hours)
- $N$ = Number of Failures

### Step 1 — Express Availability Numerically

By definition:
$$
A = \frac{T_o}{T_o + T_d}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute MTBF and MTTR Definitions

By definition:
$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_o}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_o = N \cdot \text{MTBF}
$$
$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_d}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_d = N \cdot \text{MTTR}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Availability Equation

$$
A = \frac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}
$$

### Step 4 — Cancel Common Factor N

Since $N > 0$ (the machine has experienced at least one failure cycle in observation window):

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Engineering Insight

$$
\boxed{\,A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}\,}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation**: To improve availability $A$, the manager must either **increase MTBF** (better reliability through preventive maintenance) **OR decrease MTTR** (better planning, kitting, training).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Objectives vs. Characteristics vs. KPI Mapping

This matrix is the **"one-page answer template"** for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

| S.No. | Objective (Goal) | Characteristic (Attribute) | Enabling KPI / Formula |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximize Equipment Availability | Foresight + Reliability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}} \ge 95\%$ |
| 2 | Minimize Lifecycle Cost | Economy + Feasibility | $\text{LCC} = \text{CAPEX} + \sum \text{OPEX}_\text{disc}$ |
| 3 | Ensure Safety | Safety-first + Documentation | LOTO Audit Score; Zero Accidents |
| 4 | Optimize Spares | The 6-R Principle | Stock-out % $\le 2\%$ |
| 5 | Reduce Breakdown | Proactive PM | PM% $\ge 90\%$ |
| 6 | Improve Quality | Comprehensiveness | Defect Rate (PPM) |
| 7 | Extend Asset Life | Predictive techniques | MTBF trend (year-over-year ↑) |
| 8 | Schedule Compliance | Coordination-Ready | $SC = \frac{\text{Jobs On-Time}}{\text{Total Scheduled}} \times 100$ |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (For Numerical-Type 7-Mark Sub-Parts)

**Problem:** A CNC machine was operated for 7200 hours in a year. During this period, it failed 6 times. Total downtime (including repair & logistics) was 60 hours. Calculate Availability, MTBF, MTTR, and comment on the maintenance plan quality.

### Step 1 — MTBF

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_o}{N} = \frac{7200}{6} = 1200 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 2 — MTTR

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_d}{N} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 3 — Availability

$$
A = \frac{1200}{1200 + 10} = \frac{1200}{1210} = 0.9917
$$

Converting to percent:
$$
A = 99.17\%
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Comment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** Availability of **99.17%** is **well above the 95% benchmark** — indicative of an excellent maintenance plan. However, **MTTR of 10 hours** is high. The maintenance planning team should focus on **kitting and spares availability** to reduce MTTR (logistics component), as this would further improve availability and OEE.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Maintenance Planning & Scheduling Hierarchy

This block diagram illustrates the *flow* of maintenance planning through a typical industrial plant, mapping the relationship between objectives and characteristics at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Corporate Maintenance Policy] --> B1[Define Objectives]
    B1 --> B2[Set KPIs e.g. A MTBF MTTR]
    B2 --> C1[Collect Equipment Data]
    C1 --> C2[Criticality Analysis ABC]
    C2 --> D1[Failure Mode Analysis FMEA]
    D1 --> D2[Select Maintenance Strategy]
    D2 --> E1[Plan the Job]
    E1 --> E2[Determine Resources Spares Tools Manpower]
    E2 --> F1[Schedule on Calendar]
    F1 --> F2[Issue Job Cards]
    F2 --> G1[Execute Maintenance]
    G1 --> G2[Record Feedback]
    G2 --> H1[Analyze KPI Performance]
    H1 --> B1
    H1 --> A0

    classDef policy fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef obj fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef plan fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef sched fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef exec fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    class A0 policy
    class B1,B2 obj
    class C1,C2,D1,D2,E1,E2 plan
    class F1,F2 sched
    class G1,G2,H1 exec
```

**How to read this diagram for exam writing:**
- The **blue band** is *Policy*.
- The **orange band** is *Objectives* — this is where you list the 7 objectives.
- The **green band** is *Planning* — this is where characteristics of a *good plan* apply (foresight, feasibility).
- The **red band** is *Scheduling* — this is where characteristics of a *good schedule* apply (realistic time, resource-loaded).
- The **purple band** is *Execution & Feedback* — closed-loop KPI monitoring.

## 4.2 Mapping of Objectives to Characteristics — Cross-Functional View

```mermaid
graph LR
    O1[Obj: Maximize Availability] --- C1[Char: Foresight]
    O2[Obj: Minimize Cost] --- C2[Char: Economy]
    O3[Obj: Safety] --- C3[Char: Safety Compliance]
    O4[Obj: Resource Optimization] --- C4[Char: Resource-Loaded]
    O5[Obj: Reduce Breakdown] --- C5[Char: Proactiveness]
    O6[Obj: Extend Life] --- C6[Char: Documentation]

    C1 -.enables.-> K1[High MTBF]
    C2 -.enables.-> K2[Low LCC]
    C3 -.enables.-> K3[Zero LTI]
    C4 -.enables.-> K4[Low MTTR]
    C5 -.enables.-> K5[High PM%]
    C6 -.enables.-> K6[Audit Compliance]

    classDef obj fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    classDef char fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    classDef kpi fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724

    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5,O6 obj
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 char
    class K1,K2,K3,K4,K5,K6 kpi
```

## 4.3 Planning vs. Scheduling — Visual Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLAN[PLANNING PHASE - DESK BASED]
        P1[What to do] --> P2[How to do] --> P3[Resources needed]
    end

    subgraph SCHED[SCHEDULING PHASE - CALENDAR BASED]
        S1[When to do] --> S2[Who will do] --> S3[Job Card Issuance]
    end

    PLAN --> SCHED

    classDef plan fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    classDef sched fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    class P1,P2,P3 plan
    class S1,S2,S3 sched
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation**: The diagram visually confirms the KTU definition — Planning (blue) is the *thinking* phase; Scheduling (red) is the *time-binding* phase. The arrow indicates that **a schedule is meaningless without a preceding plan**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Maintenance Planning. List any four objectives of maintenance planning.**

**Model Answer:**
Maintenance Planning is the pre-determining activity that decides **what** maintenance work is to be done, **how** it is to be done, and **what resources** are required — before actual execution.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To maximize equipment availability and reliability.
2. To minimize total lifecycle cost of equipment.
3. To ensure safety of personnel, equipment, and environment.
4. To optimize usage of manpower, spares, and tools.

*(For full 3 marks, students must also mention the metric or end-state — e.g., "Achieve availability ≥95%.")*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Maintenance Planning and Maintenance Scheduling. State two characteristics of a good maintenance schedule.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Planning | Scheduling |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | *What* and *How* | *When* and *Who* |
| Nature | Strategic / Intellectual | Tactical / Calendar-based |
| Time Horizon | Long-term (weeks to years) | Short-term (days to weeks) |
| Output | Job Plan, BOM, SOP | Calendar, Job Card |

**Two Characteristics of a Good Schedule:**
1. **Realistic time estimates** based on standard job durations.
2. **Resource-loaded** — spares, tools, and skilled manpower are pre-arranged.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven major objectives of maintenance planning**. Relate each objective to the relevant KPI. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **Availability, MTBF, and MTTR**. A pump in a process plant operates for 8760 hours/year. It fails 4 times per year with a total downtime of 32 hours. Calculate availability and comment on the maintenance plan quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

The seven objectives of maintenance planning are:

1. **Maximize Equipment Availability** — Measured by Availability $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}} \ge 95\%$.

2. **Minimize Lifecycle Cost (LCC)** — Total cost = CAPEX + Discounted OPEX over asset life. Measured via **LCC analysis**.

3. **Ensure Safety & Environmental Compliance** — Measured via **Lost Time Injury (LTI)** frequency, statutory audit pass-rate, ISO 45001 compliance.

4. **Optimize Resource Utilization** — Manpower, spares, tools. Measured via **spares stock-out %** (target $\le 2\%$) and **technician utilization %**.

5. **Minimize Unplanned Breakdowns** — Measured via **PM%** = $\frac{\text{Planned Hours}}{\text{Total Maintenance Hours}} \times 100 \ge 90\%$.

6. **Improve Product Quality** — A maintained machine produces tighter tolerances. Measured via **defect rate (PPM)** of dependent product.

7. **Ensure Documentation for Audit** — ISO 9001/14001/45001 require traceable maintenance records.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Derivation:** *(already shown in Section 3.1)*

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Given:
$$
T_o = 8760 \text{ hours}, \quad N = 4, \quad T_d = 32 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 2 — Compute MTBF:
$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{8760}{4} = 2190 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 3 — Compute MTTR:
$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 4 — Compute Availability:
$$
A = \frac{2190}{2190 + 8} = \frac{2190}{2198} = 0.9964
$$

Converting to percentage:
$$
A = 99.64\%
$$

Step 5 — **Comment:**
Availability of **99.64%** is **excellent** and far exceeds the 95% benchmark, indicating a high-quality maintenance plan. However, **MTTR of 8 hours** suggests scope for improvement — possibly through better spares kitting, faster fault diagnosis training, or contractor coordination.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **characteristics of a good maintenance plan** and a **good maintenance schedule**. Illustrate with a comparative table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop maintenance planning process** from policy formulation to KPI feedback. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Characteristics of a Good Maintenance Plan:**

| # | Characteristic | Explanation |
|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | Foresight | Anticipates failures using FMEA / RCM |
| 2 | Flexibility | Accommodates emergency work orders |
| 3 | Feasibility | Realistic within budget, manpower constraints |
| 4 | Economy | Cost-optimized, no over/under-maintenance |
| 5 | Safety | Includes LOTO, risk assessment, PPE |
| 6 | Comprehensiveness | Covers all assets hierarchically |
| 7 | Documentation | Paperless, audit-ready, ISO-aligned |

**Characteristics of a Good Maintenance Schedule:**

| # | Characteristic | Explanation |
|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | Realistic Time Estimates | Uses standard job time data |
| 2 | Resource-Loaded | Spares, tools, manpower pre-arranged |
| 3 | Coordination-Ready | Aligned with production schedule |
| 4 | Priority-Based | A-B-C criticality ranking applied |
| 5 | Contingency-Bearing | Buffer time for unexpected delays |
| 6 | Communicable | Clear, simple job cards to technicians |

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Closed-Loop Maintenance Planning Process:**

The closed-loop process consists of **8 stages** (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Corporate Policy Formulation** — Top management defines maintenance vision, budget, KPIs.
2. **Define Objectives** — Set targets: Availability ≥95%, PM% ≥90%, MTTR <2 hrs.
3. **Equipment Data Collection** — Gather nameplate, drawings, history, MTBF data.
4. **Criticality Analysis (ABC)** — Rank equipment by consequence of failure.
5. **Failure Mode Analysis (FMEA/RCM)** — Identify failure modes, root causes, effects.
6. **Strategy Selection** — Choose preventive, predictive, or corrective strategy.
7. **Planning & Scheduling** — Develop job plan (steps, spares, tools) and schedule (calendar).
8. **Execution & KPI Feedback** — Execute, record data, compute A, MTBF, MTTR, PM%. Feedback refines Stage 2 (Objectives).

The *closed loop* ensures continuous improvement (Kaizen philosophy).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Planning with Scheduling** — Most common reason for losing 2-3 marks. Planning = *what/how*, Scheduling = *when/who*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting Units** — Writing MTBF = 1200 instead of **1200 hours**. Marks deducted for missing units.
> 3. **Using Calendar Time instead of Operating Time** in Availability — Major conceptual error.
> 4. **Not commenting** on the numerical answer — 1-2 marks are reserved for engineering interpretation. Always end numerical problems with a "Comment" line.
> 5. **Writing objectives without metrics** — "To maximize availability" alone is incomplete. Add "≥95%" or "MTBF > 2000 hrs".
> 6. **Skipping the derivation** in 7-mark sub-parts — Direct formula substitution without showing $A = \frac{T_o}{T_o + T_d}$ → $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ costs full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Bullets — Print This Before Exam**

- **Planning** = "**What & How**" (Intellectual).
- **Scheduling** = "**When & Who**" (Calendar).
- **Primary Goal of Maintenance** = Maximize *Availability* at minimum *Lifecycle Cost* with *Zero Accidents*.
- **Master Formula** — $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ (memorize with derivation).
- **7 Objectives of Planning** = Availability, Cost, Safety, Resource Optimization, Breakdown Reduction, Quality, Documentation.
- **7 Characteristics of a Good Plan** = Foresight, Flexibility, Feasibility, Economy, Safety, Comprehensiveness, Documentation.
- **6 Characteristics of a Good Schedule** = Realistic, Resource-loaded, Coordinated, Priority-based, Contingent, Communicable.
- **6 Rs of Spares** = Right Item, Quantity, Quality, Place, Time, Cost.
- **KPI Benchmarks**:
   - Availability $\ge 95\%$
   - OEE $\ge 85\%$ (world class)
   - PM% $\ge 90\%$
   - Spares stock-out $\le 2\%$
- **MTTR Includes**: Repair time + Logistic time + Diagnostic time + Wait time.
- **MTBF Includes**: Operating time only — *exclude* downtime.
- **Closed Loop** ends at KPI feedback → revises objectives → **continuous improvement**.
- **FMEA & ABC Analysis** are *enabling techniques* of planning, not objectives themselves.
- **ISO 9001/14001/45001** documentation requirement is a legitimate KTU-listed objective.
- **Always include units** (hours, %) in numerical answers.
- **Always end numerics with a "Comment"** on engineering interpretation.
- **LCC** = CAPEX + discounted OPEX over asset life.
- **LOTO** = Lock Out Tag Out — non-negotiable safety characteristic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Maintenance Organisation - Formal and Informal Organisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Maintenance Organisation - Formal and Informal Organisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Maintenance Organisation

A **Maintenance Organisation** is the structured framework of personnel, departments, policies, procedures, and authority relationships established within an industrial enterprise to plan, execute, control, and continuously improve all maintenance activities (preventive, predictive, corrective, breakdown) on physical assets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEIET613, Module 4):**
> "Maintenance organisation refers to the systematic arrangement of human resources, functional responsibilities, and reporting lines designed to ensure that equipment, machinery, and plant facilities achieve their intended useful life with optimal reliability, availability, maintainability, and safety (RAMS) parameters."

## 1.2 The Two Broad Archetypes: Formal vs. Informal Organisation

In every industrial setup, two parallel organisational realities coexist:

### A. Formal Organisation (The "Blueprint")

A **Formal Maintenance Organisation** is the *officially sanctioned, deliberately planned, written, and documented* structure. It appears on organisation charts, is bound by Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), and has clearly defined authority, responsibility, accountability, and communication channels.

### B. Informal Organisation (The "Hidden Network")

An **Informal Maintenance Organisation** is the *unofficial, spontaneous, socially evolved* network of personal relationships, friendships, trust-bonds, and unwritten norms that emerges naturally among maintenance workers, supervisors, technicians, and engineers outside the formal hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Operating Theatre**
>
> - The **Formal Organisation** is like the *official surgical protocol*: who is the surgeon, who is the anaesthetist, who scrubs in, who hands instruments, what is the documented checklist before incision. It is rigid, written, and auditable.
> - The **Informal Organisation** is the *unspoken coordination* between the senior surgeon and the scrub nurse who have worked together for 12 years — a glance, a nod, an intuitive handover of the right scalpel at the right moment without a single word. It is fluid, unwritten, but indispensable when the lights flicker.
>
> In a plant, the **Maintenance Manager** relies on the *formal chart* for budgets, KPIs, and audits, but depends on the *informal network* to actually get a critical compressor back online at 2:00 AM.

## 1.3 Why Both Exist Simultaneously — The KTU "Two-Organisation" Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Barnard–Roethlisberger Principle (Chester Barnard, 1938):**
> *"Every formal organisation gives rise to an informal organisation — the formal provides the skeleton, the informal provides the soul."*
>
> **KTU Board Marker Statement:** A maintenance department that ignores its informal organisation loses shop-floor trust; one that ignores its formal organisation loses accountability. Effective maintenance leadership balances BOTH.

## 1.4 Core Parameters That Distinguish the Two

| Parameter | Formal Organisation | Informal Organisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deliberately designed by management | Spontaneously emerges from social interaction |
| **Authority Basis** | Legitimate (positional) authority | Personal (expert/referent) authority |
| **Communication** | Official memos, work orders, CMMS tickets | Grapevine, verbal, peer-to-peer |
| **Structure Visibility** | Visible on organisation chart | Invisible — revealed by sociometric analysis |
| **Stability** | Stable, slow to change | Dynamic, changes with personnel |
| **Purpose** | Task accomplishment (maintenance goals) | Social satisfaction, belonging, mutual aid |
| **Compliance Driver** | Rules, SOPs, ISO 55000 audits | Norms, peer pressure, trust |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Formal Maintenance Organisation

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Formal Maintenance Organisation

A formal maintenance organisation is typically built around **four canonical pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Centralised Maintenance Organisation
- All maintenance authority, planning, scheduling, and execution resides under a single **Maintenance Head / Chief Maintenance Engineer** at the plant or corporate level.
- **Reporting line:** Maintenance Crew → Supervisor → Maintenance Engineer → Maintenance Manager → Plant Head.
- **KTU Industrial Use:** Adopted in process industries (refineries, petrochemicals, power plants) where critical assets are highly interdependent.

### Pillar 2 — Decentralised Maintenance Organisation
- Maintenance authority is **delegated to individual production units, zones, or shop floors**.
- Each unit has its own mini-maintenance crew reporting to the Unit Production Manager.
- **KTU Industrial Use:** Adopted in multi-product discrete manufacturing (automotive, FMCG) where product lines are geographically or process-wise separated.

### Pillar 3 — Hybrid (Combined Centralised + Decentralised) Organisation
- **Strategic functions** (planning, budgeting, CMMS ownership, reliability engineering) are centralised.
- **Operational functions** (day-to-day lubrication, cleaning, minor adjustments) are decentralised to operators via Autonomous Maintenance (TPM pillar 1).

### Pillar 4 — Third-Party / Contracted Maintenance Organisation
- Maintenance is outsourced to Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) or specialist contractors under Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

## 2.2 Formal Organisation — Characteristic Features (KTU High-Yield Points)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Defining Attributes of Formal Maintenance Organisation (Examinable):**

1. **Division of Work** — Specialisation: Mechanical, Electrical, Instrumentation, Civil maintenance are separate wings.
2. **Hierarchy of Authority** — Clear scalar chain from Maintenance Helper → Technician → Supervisor → Engineer → Manager.
3. **Span of Control** — Typically **6 to 8 subordinates per supervisor** (Graicunas' theorem) in maintenance due to technical complexity.
4. **Unity of Command** — Each maintenance worker reports to ONE supervisor (Fayol's principle).
5. **Formal Communication Channels** — Work orders, maintenance job cards, CMMS notifications, shift logs.
6. **Documentation & Standardisation** — SOPs, ISO 55001 Asset Management System, maintenance manuals, lubrication charts.

## 2.3 Formal Organisation — Merits & Demerits (KTU Board Favourite Table)

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| Clear authority & responsibility — eliminates ambiguity | Slow decision-making due to red-tape and rigid hierarchy |
| Easy performance audit and KPI tracking | Poor adaptability to sudden breakdowns |
| Specialisation → higher technical competence | "Us vs. Them" silo mentality between Maintenance & Production |
| Conducive to long-term planning & budgeting | Demotivates creative technicians bound by SOPs |
| Compliance with ISO 9001 / ISO 55000 | Communication filtered through layers — message distortion |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Organisational Design Metrics for Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulas for Maintenance Organisation Sizing & Structuring:**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard / Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Maintenance Crew Size** | $N_m = \dfrac{A \times L}{P \times E}$ | $N_m$ = number of maintainers, $A$ = number of assets, $L$ = labour hours per asset per year, $P$ = productive hours per maintainer per year, $E$ = efficiency factor ($\approx 0.85$) |
| **Maintenance Cost Ratio** | $R_{mc} = \dfrac{C_{maint}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | World-class: **3–6 %** of plant replacement cost |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours between failures |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours to restore |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | World-class target $\geq 0.95$ |
| **Span of Control (Graicunas)** | $n \left( \dfrac{2^{n}}{2} + n - 1 \right)$ interactions for $n$ subordinates | Optimal: 6–8 in maintenance |
| **Maintenance Labour Productivity** | $\text{MLP} = \dfrac{\text{Output (tonnes)}}{\text{Maintenance Man-hours}}$ | Industry-specific |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design Phase:** Sets reporting lines, recruitment pattern, training roadmap.
- **Operations Phase:** Drives CMMS structure, RACI matrix, maintenance budget cycle.
- **Compliance Phase:** Satisfies ISO 55001 clause 7 (Support) and clause 8 (Operation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Informal Organisation

## 3.1 The Genesis of Informal Organisation

The informal organisation is **not designed** — it **emerges** the moment two or more people interact repeatedly. In a maintenance context, it arises from:

- Repeated co-working in breakdown situations.
- Shared technical language and jargon.
- Mutual dependence during night shifts or shutdowns.
- Cultural and linguistic homogeneity of technicians.
- The need to bypass slow formal channels.

## 3.2 Structural Characteristics of Informal Maintenance Organisation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examinable Features of Informal Maintenance Organisation:**

1. **No Formal Chart** — Structure must be discovered via **Sociometric Testing** (Moreno's Sociogram).
2. **Leader Emerges Naturally** — Often the *most experienced turner* or *senior fitter*, NOT the appointed supervisor.
3. **Based on Common Interest** — Solving the recurring pump failure, sharing tools, gossip about the new manager.
4. **Flexible & Fluid** — Groups form and dissolve around specific breakdowns.
5. **Power Centre Shifts** — Authority moves to whoever has *critical tacit knowledge* of the asset.
6. **Communication = Grapevine** — Single-strand, cluster, gossip, probability chains (Davis' research).

## 3.3 Functions Performed by Informal Organisation (Why KTU Asks This)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Positive Functions:**

1. **Fills Gaps in Formal Structure** — Quick coordination during emergencies.
2. **Social Satisfaction** — Sense of belonging reduces absenteeism in maintenance crews.
3. **Communication Efficiency** — Critical information travels faster than through official channels.
4. **Cultural Transmission** — Tacit knowledge (e.g., "that bearing always fails in monsoon") passes from senior to junior.
5. **Constraint on Management** — Informal groups can resist irrational orders.
6. **Innovation Incubator** — Shop-floor improvisations are often born here.

## 3.4 Dysfunctions of Informal Organisation

| Dysfunction | Maintenance Context Example |
|---|---|
| **Resistance to Change** | Crew sabotages introduction of new CMMS software |
| **Rumour Mongering** | "Plant is shutting down" causes attrition |
| **Groupthink** | Senior fitter conceals a recurring fault to protect reputation |
| **Favouritism & Clique Formation** | Tools/access shared only within caste/linguistic group |
| **Conflict with Formal Authority** | "We don't take orders from that young engineer" |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Formal vs. Informal Organisation (Comprehensive Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Master Table — Memorise This:**

| Dimension | Formal Organisation | Informal Organisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deliberately designed | Spontaneously emerges |
| **Purpose** | Maintenance objectives & KPIs | Social needs & belonging |
| **Structure** | Hierarchical, written | Network, unwritten |
| **Authority Source** | Position / Role | Knowledge / Personality / Trust |
| **Communication** | Official (work orders, CMMS) | Grapevine (verbal, WhatsApp) |
| **Leader** | Appointed supervisor | Emergent informal leader |
| **Behaviour Control** | Rules, SOPs, audits | Norms, peer pressure, ostracism |
| **Stability** | High (changes slowly) | Low (changes with personnel) |
| **Emotional Tone** | Impersonal, contractual | Personal, emotional |
| **Documentation** | Heavily documented | Undocumented |
| **Performance Metric** | MTBF, MTTR, OEE | Trust, reciprocity, loyalty |
| **Lifespan** | Exists as long as enterprise exists | Exists as long as group members stay together |
| **KTU Example** | Maintenance organogram on the wall | "Tea-shop committee" that decides shift swaps |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — How to Identify Informal Networks in Maintenance (Sociometric Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Moreno's Sociogram Procedure (Examinable Procedure):**

**Step 1 — Question Formulation**
Ask each technician: *"If you had a tough breakdown on a CNC machine at 11 PM, which 3 colleagues would you call first?"*

**Step 2 — Construct the Sociomatrix**
Let workers be $W_1, W_2, \dots, W_n$. Define choice indicator:
$$ c_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } W_i \text{ chooses } W_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Indegree (Popularity Score)**
$$ \deg^{-}(W_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{ij} $$

**Step 4 — Compute Outdegree (Sociability Score)**
$$ \deg^{+}(W_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} $$

**Step 5 — Identify the Informal Star**
The worker with the **highest indegree** $\max_j \deg^{-}(W_j)$ is the *de facto* informal leader — usually the maintenance guru.

**Step 6 — Plot the Sociogram**
A directed graph where arrows go from chooser to chosen. Clusters reveal cliques; isolates reveal alienation risks.

> **KTU Interpretation:** If the informal star is *also* the formal supervisor → high alignment. If the informal star is a *technician* while the formal leader is an *engineer* → potential conflict zone to be managed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
