```mermaid
    A[START: ASSESS PLANT] --> B{PLANT GEOGRAPHY}
    B -- CONTIGUOUS --> C{EQUIPMENT CRITICALITY}
    B -- DISPERSED --> D[FAVOUR DECENTRALISED]
    C -- FEW HIGH VALUE --> E[FAVOUR CENTRALISED]
    C -- MANY LOW VALUE --> F[FAVOUR DECENTRALISED]
    E --> G{TOOL COST}
    G -- HIGH --> H[STRONGLY CENTRALISED]
    G -- LOW --> I[CONSIDER HYBRID]
    F --> J{SKILL AVAILABILITY}
    J -- GENERALISTS --> K[DECENTRALISED MULTISKILLED]
    J -- SPECIALISTS --> L[HYBRID WITH CENTRAL SPECIALISTS]
    H --> M[PURE CENTRALISED]
    I --> N[HYBRID MODEL]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    D --> N
```

> **Reading the chart:** Every path leads to either Pure Centralised, Pure Decentralised, or (most commonly) the **Hybrid** model.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Information and Material Flow in a Hybrid Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[PRODUCTION UNIT A] -- WORK REQUEST --> CP[CENTRAL PLANNING CELL]
    P2[PRODUCTION UNIT B] -- WORK REQUEST --> CP
    P3[PRODUCTION UNIT C] -- WORK REQUEST --> CP
    CP -- JOB ORDER --> UA[UNIT A CREW]
    CP -- JOB ORDER --> UB[UNIT B CREW]
    CP -- JOB ORDER --> UC[UNIT C CREW]
    CP -- MAJOR OVERHAUL --> CW[CENTRAL WORKSHOP]
    UA -- FAST SPARES --> LSA[LOCAL STORE A]
    UB -- FAST SPARES --> LSB[LOCAL STORE B]
    UC -- FAST SPARES --> LSC[LOCAL STORE C]
    CW -- SLOW SPARES --> CS[CENTRAL SPARES]
    UA -- KPI DATA --> CP
    UB -- KPI DATA --> CP
    UC -- KPI DATA --> CP
```

> **Reading the chart:** Solid arrows represent *routine* flows (work requests, spares, KPI). Central Planning Cell is the *nerve centre*, but routine work happens at the unit level.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**"Define Centralised Maintenance. List any two of its salient advantages."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Centralised maintenance is an organisational structure in which all maintenance personnel, workshops, spare-parts stores, and decision-making authority are grouped under a single maintenance executive who controls and dispatches them to various production units as needed.

*Any two advantages (1 mark, ½ each):*
1. **Optimum utilisation** of costly maintenance resources (e.g., one large CNC machine serves the whole plant).
2. **Better control and standardisation** of procedures, spares, and KPIs — easier ISO 55001 / ISO 9001 compliance.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**"Give a brief account of the Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation."** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation, routine and minor maintenance work (e.g., lubrication, belt replacement, fuse replacement) is **decentralised** at the production-unit level, where small multi-skilled crews and local sub-stores handle it for minimum response time. However, **specialised and capital-intensive jobs** — major overhauls, large motor rewinding, CNC machining, calibration of precision instruments — are handled by a **Central Workshop** and a **Central Spare-Parts Store**, supported by a Central Planning Cell that prepares PM schedules and shutdown plans for the entire plant. This model combines the *speed* of decentralisation with the *expert depth and economy* of centralisation, and is the **most widely adopted form in modern process industries**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Module 4 carries a 14-mark question. Two alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided. **Attempt ANY ONE** as per the choice rule.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**"Discuss the centralised and decentralised maintenance organisations in detail. Compare them on at least ten parameters and state the factors that influence the choice between them."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model 2024]`

#### (a) Centralised Maintenance Organisation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** In a centralised maintenance organisation, **all maintenance activities** — preventive, predictive, corrective, and overhaul — are managed by a single maintenance department headed by a *Maintenance Director* (or Chief Engineer – Maintenance). The department controls a central workshop, a central tool room, a central spares inventory, and a central planning cell. Maintenance crews are *dispatched* to production units on a job-by-job basis.

**Structure (Diagram Refer to Section 4.1):**

$$
\text{Maintenance Director} \rightarrow \text{Planning Manager} \rightarrow \text{Discipline Heads (Mech/Elec/Inst)} \rightarrow \text{Foremen} \rightarrow \text{Technicians}
$$

**Operating Procedure:**
1. Production unit raises a work request → flows to Central Planning Cell.
2. Planner prioritises against equipment criticality and production schedule.
3. Job is dispatched to the appropriate central workshop or field crew.
4. Spares drawn from central store; job completed and time/cost recorded.
5. Feedback KPI (MTTR, availability, cost) flows back to planning cell.

**Advantages:**
- Optimum utilisation of expensive tools and spares (no duplication).
- Uniform procedures, easier audit, better ISO compliance.
- Strong technical specialisation (rotating equipment, hydraulics, etc.).
- Centralised data enables plant-wide reliability analysis.
- Economical for small-to-medium compact plants.

**Disadvantages:**
- Slow response time — crew must travel to the breakdown location.
- Lower worker morale — technicians feel detached from production outcomes.
- A single-point failure in the central workshop can cripple the entire plant.
- Over-centralisation creates bureaucratic delays for trivial jobs.

---

#### (b) Decentralised Maintenance & Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** In a decentralised maintenance organisation, each production unit (sector, shop, or department) has its **own self-contained maintenance crew**, headed by a *Unit Maintenance Engineer*, who reports to the *Unit Production Manager*. Spare parts are held in *local sub-stores*, and routine decisions are taken at unit level without central approval.

**Structure (Diagram Refer to Section 4.2):**

$$
\text{Unit Production Manager} \rightarrow \text{Unit Maintenance Engineer} \rightarrow \text{Multi-skilled Crew}
$$

**Advantages:** Fast response time; better worker involvement; resilient to single-point failures; each unit's priorities are decided locally.

**Disadvantages:** Duplication of tools and spares (higher cost); inconsistent procedures; difficulty in achieving specialist skills at every unit; harder plant-wide KPI tracking.

**Comparison (10 parameters — refer to Section 3.2 table for full matrix):**
1. Span of control
2. Response time
3. Resource utilisation
4. Skill specialisation
5. Spare-parts cost
6. Coordination complexity
7. Best suited for
8. Record keeping
9. Cost of supervision
10. Disaster recovery

**Factors Influencing the Choice:**
1. **Plant size and layout** (compact → central; spread out → decentral).
2. **Equipment criticality and value** (few high-value → central; many low-value → decentral).
3. **Capital cost of tools** (high → central to share; low → decentral to duplicate).
4. **Skill availability in the local labour market.**
5. **Required response time** (critical fast-response → decentral).
6. **Geographic separation between production units.**
7. **Management philosophy and historical practice.**

**Conclusion:** Pure forms are rare. The **Combined (Hybrid) Organisation** is the engineering compromise that delivers both speed and depth, and is the recommended model for most process plants.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**"Explain the Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation with a neat block diagram. Discuss its structure, advantages, and the conditions under which it is preferred over pure centralisation or pure decentralisation."** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model 2024]`

#### (a) Structure of the Combined Maintenance Organisation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** The Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation strategically *splits* maintenance work between **decentralised unit crews** (for speed) and a **central workshop** (for expertise and economy). It is the *de facto* standard in large process plants, refineries, fertiliser units, and power stations.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.3):**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Unit Crews + Local Sub-Stores}}_{\text{Decentralised Routine Work}} \;\;\leftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{Central Planning + Central Workshop + Central Spares}}_{\text{Centralised Specialised Work}}
$$

**Functional Split:**

| Activity | Handled By | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Routine PM, cleaning, lubrication, minor adjustments | Unit crew (decentralised) | High frequency, low skill, fast response needed |
| Condition monitoring, route inspections | Unit crew with central planner guidance | Standardisation needed for data |
| Overhauls, large motor rewinds, gearbox rebuilding | Central workshop | Specialist skill + costly equipment |
| Spare parts (fast-moving) | Local sub-store | 80/20 rule: 20% of SKUs account for 80% of withdrawals |
| Spare parts (slow-moving, high-value) | Central store | Low frequency, high unit cost → avoid duplication |
| Budget, KPI, statutory audit | Central maintenance accounts | Single P&L view, easier compliance |

**Operating Logic:**
1. PM schedules and shutdown plans are prepared **centrally**.
2. **Unit crews** execute daily PMs and routine work.
3. **Major jobs** are referred to the central workshop via a *job order*.
4. Spares are drawn from local sub-stores for routine; centralised for major.
5. KPI data from each unit flows up to the central MIS dashboard.

---

#### (b) Advantages, Suitability, and Why Pure Forms Fail (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of the Combined Model:**
1. **Fast response** for routine work (decentralised crews on-site).
2. **Expert depth** for major work (central workshop with specialists).
3. **Optimised inventory cost** (split between local and central spares).
4. **Standardised procedures** (central planning enforces PM schedules).
5. **Resilience** — local crew can limp along if central workshop is overloaded.
6. **Better worker engagement** — unit crews see direct impact of their work.
7. **Best fit for ISO 55001 Asset Management** — central policy, local execution.

**Conditions Where Hybrid is Preferred over Pure Forms:**

| Condition | Pure Centralised Fails Because | Pure Decentralised Fails Because | Hybrid Wins Because |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant > 2 km² spread | Crews take too long to reach | Each unit buys its own CNC | Central CNC + unit fitters |
| Mix of critical + non-critical machines | Routine work waits behind overhauls | Critical machines handled by generalists | Critical machines at centre, routine at unit |
| Remote location with limited local specialists | All specialists must be at centre (cost) | Units cannot solve specialist problems | Specialists visit on-call from centre |
| Tight shutdown window | Crews must come from far | No specialist depth in unit | Central specialist support + unit preparation |

**Limitations of the Hybrid Model:**
- Requires a **competent central planning cell** to coordinate effectively.
- **Communication overhead** between unit crews and central workshop can be high.
- **Conflict of priorities** between unit production managers and central maintenance manager must be resolved by plant director.

**Conclusion:** The Hybrid model is the **dominant strategy** in maintenance management. KTU examiners expect the answer to emphasise that the *choice* of organisation is a *function of plant geometry, equipment criticality, and tool cost*, not a matter of universal preference.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing only advantages & disadvantages without a comparative table** — *Examiner deducts 2–3 marks*. Always include the 10+ parameter table.
> 2. **Drawing a flowery organisational chart without explaining the *flow of work*** — marks are awarded for *operational logic*, not just boxes and arrows.
> 3. **Ignoring the Combined/Hybrid form** — KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes it; skipping it loses 4–5 marks.
> 4. **Mere textbook definitions** — without *quantitative justification* (e.g., crew-size formula, response time equation), the answer remains at the 60% level.
> 5. **Confusing "Centralised Maintenance" with "Centralised Production"** — maintain the focus on the *maintenance function only*.
> 6. **Spelling mistakes** in designations like "Maintenance Engineer", "Plant Director" — examiner may deduct ½ mark per persistent error.
> 7. **Not concluding with a recommendation** — always end the 14-mark answer with: *"Hence, the Combined/Hybrid form is the most suitable for [context]."*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Centralised Maintenance** = *one pool* of maintenance resources serving the *entire plant* under *one* maintenance executive.
- **Decentralised Maintenance** = *multiple* independent maintenance units, each attached to a production unit and reporting to its production manager.
- **Combined (Hybrid)** = *decentralised* for routine/fast work, *centralised* for specialised/overhaul work — *the most widely adopted form in industry*.
- The **crew-size formula** $N = \dfrac{\sum (T_i \cdot F_i) \cdot M_s}{H_a \cdot \eta}$ lets you *quantitatively* justify the choice.
- **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — decentralised improves $MTTR$ (response), centralised improves $MTBF$ (preventive quality).
- **Decision criteria**: Plant geometry, equipment criticality, tool cost, skill availability, and required response time.
- **Spare-parts inventory** is optimised by the *80/20 rule*: 20% of SKUs (fast-moving) at unit sub-stores, 80% (slow-moving) at the central store.
- **KPI tracking** is easier in centralised, *execution speed* is easier in decentralised — the hybrid model captures both.
- **Kerala-specific context**: FACT Kochi, BPCL Kochi, Nitta Gelatin, Hindustan Newsprint, and HOCL use the **Combined model** in practice.
- For KTU 2024 exams, **always include a block diagram** and a **10+ parameter comparison table** in any 14-mark answer on this topic.
- **Closing statement in every answer**: *"The choice between centralised, decentralised, and combined is not universal — it is a function of plant size, layout, equipment criticality, capital cost of resources, and the desired response time."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Demerits, Maintenance Costs - Classification of Maintenance Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Merits and Demerits, Maintenance Costs - Classification of Maintenance Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Merits of Maintenance Engineering

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Merits of maintenance refer to the **positive engineering and economic outcomes** that an organization achieves by implementing a systematic, planned, and proactive maintenance management system. These merits justify the financial and human-resource investment in a maintenance function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Merits are the *tangible and intangible benefits* that improve **equipment availability, product quality, plant safety, and long-term profitability**. The examiner expects students to link every merit to an *engineering outcome* rather than a generic statement.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body Health Checkup" Metaphor:**
Think of a manufacturing plant as a human body and maintenance as its **annual health checkup and disciplined lifestyle**.
- A person who exercises regularly and visits the doctor **preventively** avoids major surgeries (heart attacks, organ failures) later in life — this is the **Preventive Maintenance Merit**.
- The same person enjoys higher energy, fewer sick days, and a longer working life — this represents **Higher Equipment Availability, Better OEE, and Extended Asset Life**.
- Conversely, a person who ignores health until they collapse on the floor is practicing **Breakdown Maintenance**, paying heavily in ICU bills and lost wages — these are the **Demerits of poor maintenance**.

**Primary Merits (Engineered for KTU Exam Answers):**

1. **Increased Equipment Availability & Reliability** — Reduces *Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)* and increases *Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)*.
2. **Improved Product Quality** — Well-calibrated machines produce lower-defect outputs, improving First-Pass Yield.
3. **Enhanced Plant Safety** — Reduces accidents caused by sudden mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic failures.
4. **Lower Long-Term Operating Cost** — Planned maintenance is **5 to 10 times cheaper** than emergency repair (industry benchmark).
5. **Extended Asset Life** — Slows the wear-out phase of the **bathtub curve**, postponing capital replacement.
6. **Better Workforce Morale** — Operators working on reliable equipment experience less frustration and downtime stress.
7. **Compliance with Statutory Regulations** — Satisfies **Factory Act, OSHA, and ISO 9001/14001** audit requirements.

---

### 1.2 Demerits of Maintenance Engineering

**Formal Definition:**
Demerits are the **negative consequences, hidden costs, and operational limitations** that arise either from *lack of maintenance* or from *poorly designed/over-engineered maintenance strategies*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** When KTU asks "Demerits of maintenance," students often forget that demerits apply to *both sides* — (a) demerits of *no maintenance*, and (b) demerits of *excessive/incorrect maintenance* (e.g., over-maintenance, unnecessary teardowns). Cover both for full marks.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Over-Washing a Car":**
If you never wash your car, the paint corrodes (no maintenance demerit). But if you wash it 10 times a day with harsh chemicals, you wear out the clear coat prematurely (over-maintenance demerit). Maintenance must follow an **optimal, data-driven schedule**, not a blind routine.

**Primary Demerits:**

1. **High Initial Investment** — Setting up CMMS, training technicians, and stocking spares requires significant capital.
2. **Production Interruptions** — Scheduled shutdowns for preventive maintenance temporarily halt output.
3. **Skilled Manpower Requirement** — Modern predictive techniques (vibration analysis, thermography) need certified engineers.
4. **Risk of Over-Maintenance** — Unnecessary disassembly can introduce new defects ("infant mortality" failures).
5. **Hidden Indirect Costs** — Planning, documentation, and downtime logistics add *invisible* overheads.
6. **Inventory Carrying Cost** — Large spare parts inventory ties up working capital and incurs obsolescence risk.
7. **System Complexity** — TPM, RCM, and predictive systems demand cultural change and strong management commitment.

---

### 1.3 Maintenance Costs — Classification Overview

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
Maintenance cost is the **total economic expenditure** incurred during the entire life cycle of an asset to retain it in, or restore it to, an acceptable operating condition. It includes *all labor, material, contractor, downtime, and overhead expenditures* attributable to maintenance activity.

**Industry Benchmark Metric:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a typical process plant, **maintenance cost consumes 15%–40% of the total production cost** (Heavy Industries ~25%, FMCG ~8%). World-class plants target **< 3% of Replacement Asset Value (RAV) per year**.

**Primary Classification Axes (KTU Mandatory Coverage):**

| # | Classification Axis | Two Principal Categories |
|---|---------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **By Nature of Expenditure** | Direct Costs vs. Indirect Costs |
| 2 | **By Variability with Output** | Fixed Costs vs. Variable Costs |
| 3 | **By Maintenance Strategy** | Preventive Cost vs. Breakdown Cost |
| 4 | **By Cost Center** | Labor, Material, Overhead, Contractor |
| 5 | **By Phase in Asset Life Cycle** | Commissioning, Operating, Decommissioning |

The **Classification of Maintenance Costs** is the central focus of Module 4 and must be mastered before attempting numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive — The Merits Explained Logically

**Step-by-step logic of why each merit occurs:**

- **Higher Availability → Higher Revenue:** When MTBF rises and MTTR falls, the machine produces more saleable units per shift.
  $$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

- **Better Quality → Lower Rework Cost:** Worn spindles, misaligned shafts, and dirty hydraulic fluid produce defects. Maintenance restores geometric and dynamic tolerances.

- **Lower Operating Cost → Higher Margin:** Although maintenance itself costs money, the *avoided* breakdown cost (emergency overtime, expedited spares, lost orders) is much higher.

- **Safety → Avoided Legal Liability:** One major accident can attract penalties larger than a decade of planned maintenance budget.

### 2.2 Deep Dive — The Demerits Explained Logically

- **Downtime Cost during PM:** Even scheduled shutdowns reduce throughput. Formula:
  $$C_{down} = P_{loss} \times T_{down} \times U$$
  where $P_{loss}$ is lost profit per unit, $T_{down}$ is downtime hours, $U$ is units per hour lost.

- **Inventory Carrying Cost:** Every spare in the warehouse has an annual holding cost of typically **18%–25% of its value** (industry rule of thumb).

- **Over-Maintenance Failure Rate:** Unnecessary teardown can push a machine back into the **infant-mortality region** of the bathtub curve.

### 2.3 Deep Dive — Classification of Maintenance Costs

#### A. By Nature of Expenditure

**1. Direct Maintenance Costs** — Costs that can be **traced unambiguously** to a specific maintenance job order.
- **Direct Labor:** Wages of maintenance technicians, helpers, supervisors assigned to a specific job.
- **Direct Material:** Cost of spare parts, lubricants, gaskets, bearings consumed.
- **Contractor / Vendor Cost:** External service bills (AMC, third-party calibration, OEM visits).
- **Direct Downtime Cost:** Lost production revenue *attributable* to that specific machine.

**2. Indirect Maintenance Costs (Overheads)** — Costs that **cannot be traced** to one job but are caused by the existence of the maintenance function.
- Maintenance planning department salaries.
- CMMS software license and IT support.
- Spare parts warehouse rent and insurance.
- Training and certification of technicians.
- Tool room and workshop utilities.

#### B. By Variability with Plant Activity

**1. Fixed Maintenance Costs** — Independent of machine running hours.
- Salary of permanent maintenance staff.
- Depreciation of maintenance workshop building.
- Insurance of spare parts inventory.

**2. Variable Maintenance Costs** — Proportional to equipment usage.
- Lubricant consumption per machine-hour.
- Wear-part replacement (belts, blades, grinding wheels).
- Overtime labor during breakdown.

#### C. By Maintenance Strategy

**1. Preventive Maintenance Cost ($C_{PM}$):** Cost of routine inspection, servicing, and planned replacement.
**2. Breakdown (Corrective) Maintenance Cost ($C_{BM}$):** Cost of emergency repair after failure.
**3. Predictive Maintenance Cost ($C_{PdM}$):** Cost of condition-monitoring sensors and analysis.
**4. Opportunity Cost ($C_{opp}$):** Profit lost due to downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula — Total Maintenance Cost:**
> $$C_{TM} = C_{PM} + C_{BM} + C_{PdM} + C_{opp}$$
> The **optimum maintenance level** is reached when the derivative of total cost w.r.t. maintenance effort is zero — beyond this point, *over-maintenance* begins.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use | Units / Boundary |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Equipment Availability (one of the three OEE factors) | %; $0 \le A \le 100$ |
| 2 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | %; benchmark $\ge 85\%$ is World Class |
| 3 | $C_{down} = P_{loss} \cdot T_{down} \cdot U$ | Downtime opportunity cost | Currency (₹, $) |
| 4 | $C_{TM} = C_{PM} + C_{BM} + C_{PdM} + C_{opp}$ | Total Maintenance Cost | Currency/year |
| 5 | $\frac{C_{PM}}{C_{BM}} \ge 1$ | Healthy ratio (more PM than BM) | Dimensionless; industry target $\ge 3$ |
| 6 | $C_{RAV}\% = \frac{C_{TM}}{RAV} \times 100$ | Maintenance cost as % of Replacement Asset Value | %; target $\le 3\%$ for World Class |
| 7 | $SFF = \frac{C_{PM}}{C_{PM}+C_{BM}}$ | Schedule-Failure Fraction (US Navy benchmark) | Dimensionless; $\ge 0.80$ desired |
| 8 | $C_{holding} = 0.20 \times V_{inv}$ | Annual spare inventory carrying cost | Currency/year; 20% rule |
| 9 | $C_{labor} = N_{tech} \times H_{yr} \times R_{hr}$ | Annual direct labor cost | Currency/year |
| 10 | $MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| 11 | $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | Hours |
| 12 | $C_{overhead} = C_{planning} + C_{warehouse} + C_{training}$ | Indirect cost roll-up | Currency/year |

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use `$...$` for inline math, `$$...$$` for display. The vertical bar `|` symbol is *forbidden* inside tables; use `\vert` instead (e.g., $0 \le A \vert \le 100$ written as `$0 \le A \le 100$`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Merits to Cost Categories

| Engineering Merit | Direct Cost Affected | Indirect Cost Affected | Net Financial Direction |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Higher Availability | Reduced $C_{BM}$ | Reduced $C_{opp}$ | Strongly Positive (↓) |
| Better Quality | Reduced rework cost | Lower scrap inventory | Positive (↓) |
| Extended Life | Deferred capital expense | Lower depreciation | Strongly Positive (↓) |
| Safety Compliance | Reduced accident cost | Lower insurance premium | Positive (↓) |
| Skilled Labor | Higher $C_{labor}$ | Lower $C_{BM}$ | Neutral-to-Positive |

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Total Annual Maintenance Cost

Let us derive the **Total Annual Maintenance Cost Equation** from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the four strategic cost components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= C_{PM} + C_{BM} + C_{PdM} + C_{opp}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:** Every rupee spent on maintenance falls into one of these four buckets — preventive, breakdown, predictive, or opportunity.

**Step 2 — Expand the Preventive Maintenance Cost.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PM} &= C_{PM,lab} + C_{PM,mat} + C_{PM,contract} \\
C_{PM,lab} &= N_{tech}^{PM} \cdot H_{PM} \cdot R_{hr} \\
C_{PM,mat} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i^{PM} \cdot P_i^{PM})
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:** $N_{tech}^{PM}$ = number of technicians dedicated to preventive work; $H_{PM}$ = total PM hours per year; $R_{hr}$ = hourly wage rate; $Q_i^{PM}, P_i^{PM}$ are quantity and unit price of the $i$-th spare consumed in PM.

**Step 3 — Expand the Breakdown Maintenance Cost.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{BM} &= C_{BM,lab} + C_{BM,mat} + C_{BM,down} \\
C_{BM,down} &= P_{loss} \cdot T_{down}^{BM} \cdot U
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:** Breakdown cost is dominated by *lost production*. Even a 4-hour failure on a high-speed bottling line can wipe out ₹2–5 lakh of revenue.

**Step 4 — Expand the Predictive Maintenance Cost.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PdM} &= C_{sensor} + C_{analyst} + C_{software} \\
C_{sensor} &= \frac{C_{0,sensor}}{L_{sensor}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:** $C_{0,sensor}$ is the purchase price; $L_{sensor}$ is the sensor life in years; $C_{analyst}$ is the certified vibration/thermography analyst cost.

**Step 5 — Combine and simplify.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= \underbrace{\bigl(N_{tech}^{PM}H_{PM}R_{hr} + \sum Q_i^{PM}P_i^{PM}\bigr)}_{\text{Preventive}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\bigl(N_{tech}^{BM}H_{BM}R_{hr} + \sum Q_j^{BM}P_j^{BM} + P_{loss}T_{down}^{BM}U\bigr)}_{\text{Breakdown}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\bigl(\tfrac{C_{0,sensor}}{L_{sensor}} + C_{analyst} + C_{software}\bigr)}_{\text{Predictive}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\bigl(C_{planning} + C_{warehouse} + C_{training}\bigr)}_{\text{Indirect / Overhead}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the **complete cost model** that a KTU examiner expects when a 14-mark problem asks for "classify and compute the total annual maintenance cost of a plant."

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A CNC machining cell has the following annual data:
> - Preventive labor: 800 h × ₹250/h = ₹2,00,000
> - Preventive materials: ₹1,50,000
> - Breakdown labor: 200 h × ₹250/h = ₹50,000
> - Breakdown materials: ₹80,000
> - Downtime due to breakdowns: 120 h, loss rate = ₹4,000/h
> - Predictive sensors + analyst: ₹1,20,000
> - Maintenance overhead (planning + warehouse + training): ₹3,00,000
>
> Find: Total Maintenance Cost, and comment on the PM/BM ratio.

**Step 1 — Direct Preventive Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PM} &= 200000 + 150000 = 350000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Direct Breakdown Cost (excluding downtime):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{BM,dir} &= 50000 + 80000 = 130000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Downtime Opportunity Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{opp} &= 120 \times 4000 = 480000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Predictive Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PdM} &= 120000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Indirect Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{indirect} &= 300000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Total Maintenance Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= 350000 + 130000 + 480000 + 120000 + 300000 \\
&= 1380000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — PM/BM Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_{PM}}{C_{BM}} &= \frac{350000}{130000} \approx 2.69
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The PM/BM ratio of **2.69** is acceptable (industry target ≥ 3), but the *huge opportunity cost* (₹4.8 lakh) signals that **breakdown frequency must be reduced** by intensifying PM. Total annual maintenance cost is **₹13,80,000**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Demerit-to-Cost Linkage

| Demerit | Cost Element Triggered | Typical Magnitude |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Production interruption | $C_{opp}$ (opportunity) | **5–50× the repair cost itself** |
| Over-maintenance | Unnecessary $C_{PM,mat}$ | 15–25% wasted spare consumption |
| Inventory carrying | $C_{holding}$ | 18–25% of inventory value/year |
| Skilled manpower | Higher $C_{training}$ | 5–10% of labor payroll |
| System complexity | Higher $C_{indirect}$ | 10–20% of $C_{TM}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Maintenance Costs

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Maintenance Cost C TM]
    rootA --> stratA[By Maintenance Strategy]
    rootA --> natA[By Nature of Expenditure]
    rootA --> varA[By Variability]
    rootA --> phA[By Life-Cycle Phase]

    stratA --> s1A[C PM Preventive]
    stratA --> s2A[C BM Breakdown]
    stratA --> s3A[C PdM Predictive]
    stratA --> s4A[C opp Opportunity Downtime]

    natA --> n1A[Direct Costs]
    natA --> n2A[Indirect Costs]
    n1A --> n1aA[Labor]
    n1A --> n1bA[Material]
    n1A --> n1cA[Contractor]
    n1A --> n1dA[Direct Downtime]
    n2A --> n2aA[Planning Office]
    n2A --> n2bA[Spare Warehouse]
    n2A --> n2cA[Training]
    n2A --> n2dA[CMMS Software]

    varA --> v1A[Fixed Costs]
    varA --> v2A[Variable Costs]
    v1A --> v1aA[Permanent Staff Salary]
    v1A --> v1bA[Workshop Depreciation]
    v2A --> v2aA[Lubricant per Machine Hour]
    v2A --> v2bA[Wear Part Replacement]

    phA --> p1A[Commissioning Cost]
    phA --> p2A[Operating Cost]
    phA --> p3A[Decommissioning Cost]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Start at the root node `rootA` and traverse downward. Each branch represents a *classification axis*. The four axes are the **canonical KTU answer structure** for a 14-mark classification question.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Merits and Demerits Balance Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    meritRoot[Merits of Maintenance]
    demeritRoot[Demerits of Maintenance]

    meritRoot --> m1A[Higher Availability]
    meritRoot --> m2A[Better Quality]
    meritRoot --> m3A[Enhanced Safety]
    meritRoot --> m4A[Lower Long Term Cost]
    meritRoot --> m5A[Extended Asset Life]
    meritRoot --> m6A[Statutory Compliance]

    demeritRoot --> d1A[High Initial Investment]
    demeritRoot --> d2A[Production Interruption]
    demeritRoot --> d3A[Skilled Manpower Need]
    demeritRoot --> d4A[Risk of Over Maintenance]
    demeritRoot --> d5A[Hidden Indirect Cost]
    demeritRoot --> d6A[Inventory Carrying Cost]

    m1A -. offset .-> d2A
    m4A -. offset .-> d1A
    m3A -. offset .-> d5A
    d4A -. warns .-> d6A
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows (`-->`) show *primary flow*; dashed arrows (`-.->`) show the **trade-off linkage** between a merit and a demerit. This is exactly the type of *systems-thinking diagram* examiners reward with full marks.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost Optimization Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA[Maintenance Activity Input]
    inA --> labA[Labor Hours]
    inA --> matA[Materials]
    inA --> conA[Contractor Services]
    inA --> downA[Downtime Hours]

    labA --> costA[Cost Aggregation Engine]
    matA --> costA
    conA --> costA
    downA --> costA

    costA --> clsA[Classifier Module]
    clsA --> cls1A[Direct Bucket]
    clsA --> cls2A[Indirect Bucket]
    clsA --> cls3A[Fixed Bucket]
    clsA --> cls4A[Variable Bucket]

    cls1A --> kpiA[KPI Dashboard]
    cls2A --> kpiA
    cls3A --> kpiA
    cls4A --> kpiA

    kpiA --> optA[Optimizer Decision Module]
    optA --> out1A[Increase PM Budget]
    optA --> out2A[Reduce BM Frequency]
    optA --> out3A[Invest in PdM Sensors]
    optA --> out4A[Trim Overhead]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram maps how raw maintenance inputs are **classified** into the four buckets introduced in Section 1, then **aggregated** into KPIs, and finally **optimized** into management actions. It is the *engineer's view* of the cost-classification topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any six merits of a well-planned maintenance system in an industrial plant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1/2 mark per point):**
1. Increased equipment availability and reliability (raises MTBF, lowers MTTR).
2. Improved product quality and lower rejection/rework rate.
3. Enhanced safety for personnel and compliance with statutory acts.
4. Reduction in long-term operating cost and avoided emergency expenses.
5. Extended useful life of machinery and delayed capital replacement.
6. Higher workforce morale and smoother production planning.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Classify maintenance costs based on (a) nature of expenditure and (b) variability with plant activity.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(a) By Nature:** Direct Costs (labor, material, contractor, downtime) and Indirect Costs (planning, warehouse, training, CMMS). *(1.5 marks)*
- **(b) By Variability:** Fixed Costs (permanent staff salary, workshop depreciation) and Variable Costs (lubricant per machine-hour, wear-part replacement). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — April 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **merits and demerits of a maintenance engineering system** in a modern manufacturing plant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A process plant has the following annual maintenance data:
- Preventive labor cost: ₹3,00,000
- Preventive material cost: ₹2,20,000
- Breakdown labor cost: ₹1,10,000
- Breakdown material cost: ₹95,000
- Downtime hours due to breakdown: 180 h
- Production loss rate: ₹3,500 per hour
- Predictive maintenance (sensors + analyst): ₹1,40,000
- Indirect overhead (planning + warehouse + training): ₹2,75,000

Compute the **total annual maintenance cost** and comment on the **PM/BM ratio**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Merits and Demerits (7 Marks):**

*Merits (any 4 for 4 marks):*
1. **Increased availability** — Higher MTBF and lower MTTR improve OEE.
2. **Better product quality** — Reduces defect rate and customer rejection.
3. **Safety enhancement** — Prevents accidents and legal liability.
4. **Lower long-term cost** — Planned maintenance is cheaper than emergency repair.
5. **Extended asset life** — Slows wear-out phase of the bathtub curve.

*Demerits (any 3 for 3 marks):*
1. **High initial investment** in CMMS, training, and spares.
2. **Production interruption** during scheduled PM shutdowns.
3. **Skilled manpower requirement** for advanced techniques.
4. **Risk of over-maintenance** causing infant-mortality failures.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Preventive Cost:
$$C_{PM} = 300000 + 220000 = 520000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 2 — Breakdown Direct Cost:
$$C_{BM,dir} = 110000 + 95000 = 205000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 3 — Downtime Opportunity Cost:
$$C_{opp} = 180 \times 3500 = 630000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 4 — Predictive Cost:
$$C_{PdM} = 140000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 5 — Indirect Cost:
$$C_{indirect} = 275000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 6 — Total Maintenance Cost:
$$C_{TM} = 520000 + 205000 + 630000 + 140000 + 275000 = 1770000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

Step 7 — PM/BM Ratio:
$$\frac{C_{PM}}{C_{BM}} = \frac{520000}{205000} \approx 2.54$$

**Conclusion:** The ratio of **2.54** is acceptable (target ≥ 3), but the opportunity cost ₹6.3 lakh is dominant — the plant should intensify PM to reduce breakdowns. **Total = ₹17,70,000 per year.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a **classification tree diagram**, classify maintenance costs into all standard categories. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **direct and indirect costs of maintenance** with at least six examples each, and explain why indirect costs are often underestimated by plant managers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification Tree (7 Marks):**
Draw a four-branch tree:
1. **By Nature:** Direct / Indirect.
2. **By Variability:** Fixed / Variable.
3. **By Strategy:** Preventive / Breakdown / Predictive / Opportunity.
4. **By Life-Cycle Phase:** Commissioning / Operating / Decommissioning.

Provide one example under each sub-branch (e.g., Direct Labor under Direct; CMMS under Indirect; Permanent Salary under Fixed; Lubricant under Variable; etc.).

**(b) Direct vs. Indirect Maintenance Cost (7 Marks):**

*Direct Costs (3 marks — list 6):*
1. Maintenance technician wages
2. Spare parts and consumables
3. Lubricants and cutting fluids
4. Subcontractor / OEM service bills
5. Direct machine downtime loss
6. Special tool depreciation per job

*Indirect Costs (3 marks — list 6):*
1. Maintenance planning department salaries
2. Spare parts warehouse rent and utilities
3. CMMS software license and IT support
4. Training and certification of technicians
5. Tool-room and workshop overhead
6. Insurance and obsolescence of standby inventory

*Why Indirect Costs Are Underestimated (1 mark):*
Because they are **distributed across multiple cost centers**, never appear on a single job-order sheet, and lack visible accountability — they require a deliberate cost-allocation methodology (Activity-Based Costing) to surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Demerits of Maintenance" with "Demerits of No Maintenance."** Read the question carefully — if it says *demerits of the maintenance system*, you must discuss over-maintenance, downtime, and cost issues, NOT the consequences of skipping maintenance.
> 2. **Writing only 4–5 merits/demerits instead of 6+.** The 7-mark part expects depth; a bulleted list of 8 well-explained points scores full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Opportunity (Downtime) Cost in numerical problems.** This is the single largest component in most plants and examiners *specifically test* whether you include $C_{opp} = P_{loss} \times T_{down}$.
> 4. **Mixing up Direct vs. Variable.** *Direct* refers to traceability to a job; *Variable* refers to dependence on running hours. They are orthogonal axes — do not collapse them.
> 5. **Not stating units.** Every numerical answer in a KTU paper MUST end with the unit (₹, hours, %, etc.) — failure to do so costs 0.5 mark per sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Merits, Demerits, Maintenance Cost Classification**

- **Merits (6+ to memorize):** Availability ↑, Quality ↑, Safety ↑, Cost ↓ (long-term), Asset Life ↑, Compliance ↑.
- **Demerits (6+ to memorize):** Initial Investment ↑, Production Interruption, Skilled Manpower, Over-Maintenance Risk, Hidden Indirect Cost, Inventory Carrying Cost.
- **Cost Classification Axes:** Nature (D/I), Variability (F/V), Strategy (PM/BM/PdM/Opp), Life-Cycle Phase.
- **Direct Costs:** Labor, Material, Contractor, Direct Downtime.
- **Indirect Costs:** Planning, Warehouse, Training, CMMS, Tool Room.
- **Fixed vs. Variable:** Fixed = salary, depreciation; Variable = lubricant, wear parts.
- **Master Formula:** $C_{TM} = C_{PM} + C_{BM} + C_{PdM} + C_{opp}$.
- **Downtime Formula:** $C_{opp} = P_{loss} \times T_{down} \times U$.
- **PM/BM Ratio Target:** ≥ 3 (World Class); < 1 indicates breakdown-dominated plant.
- **RAV % Benchmark:** ≤ 3% = World Class; 5–8% = Average; > 10% = Maintenance-deficient.
- **OEE Target:** ≥ 85% = World Class.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers.
- **Always mention opportunity cost** — it is the silent killer in maintenance economics.
- **Trade-off Mantra:** The optimum maintenance level is where marginal cost of additional PM equals marginal saving in BM + opportunity cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Cost Analysis, Maintenance Budget - Types of Maintenance Budget
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Cost Analysis, Maintenance Budget - Types of Maintenance Budget

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Maintenance Cost Analysis — Formal Definition

**Maintenance Cost Analysis (MCA)** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation of all expenditures (direct and indirect) incurred over the operational life of a physical asset, encompassing resources consumed for inspection, preventive, predictive, corrective, and shutdown maintenance activities. It forms the financial backbone of any maintenance management system and is a key enabler of **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** and **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** decision-making.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Maintenance Cost Analysis involves identifying, classifying, and evaluating the direct costs (labour, material, spares) and indirect costs (production losses, downtime penalties, quality defects) of maintenance to enable control, budgeting, and continuous improvement."

### 1.2 Maintenance Budget — Formal Definition

A **Maintenance Budget** is a planned financial statement, prepared for a defined fiscal period (typically one year), that forecasts the monetary resources required to execute the maintenance strategy of an organization. It is a control document — not just a forecasting tool — and it aligns maintenance execution with strategic business goals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A maintenance budget is **not** a static number. It is a **dynamic, living forecast** that must be updated based on equipment condition, failure history, and production demand.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Family Budget" Intuition

Imagine the maintenance department as a **household managing an old car** (the plant) and a **new electric vehicle** (new equipment) at the same time.

- **Direct Costs (Visible Receipts):** Petrol/diesel bills, mechanic's labour, spare parts, oil filter — these are the receipts you can see in your wallet.
- **Indirect Costs (The Silent Drain):** The day the car was at the garage, you couldn't drive to work (lost production). A breakdown caused an accident and a client cancelled a contract (customer dissatisfaction). These never appear on the garage bill, but they hit the bank account hard.
- **The Budget:** At the start of the month, the family says, "We will spend ₹5,000 on fuel, ₹3,000 on servicing, and keep ₹2,000 as emergency." That allocation **is** the maintenance budget.

If the family never analyzes past fuel bills (cost analysis) and never sets a budget, the same car will eventually cost more in towing charges than in petrol — exactly what happens in poorly managed plants.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of MCA:** Every hour of unplanned downtime typically costs **5× to 10×** the direct maintenance cost. Hence, *true* maintenance cost must always include the *hidden* downtime cost.

### 1.4 Key Cost Categories — Quick Reference

| Category | Examples | Visibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Labour** | Wages of maintenance technicians | High (Payroll) |
| **Materials & Spares** | Bearings, lubricants, seals | High (Stores) |
| **Contract Services** | OEM service, third-party AMC | High (Invoices) |
| **Tools & Equipment** | Diagnostic kits, lifting gear | Medium (Capex) |
| **Downtime Losses** | Lost production, idle labour | **Low (Hidden)** |
| **Quality Defects** | Rework, scrap, warranty claims | **Low (Hidden)** |
| **Lost Opportunity** | Late delivery penalties | **Low (Hidden)** |
| **Safety/Environmental** | Spillage cleanup, insurance | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost Decomposition Logic

Maintenance costs are decomposed along **three orthogonal axes** in KTU/ISO 13374 practice:

1. **Cost Element Axis** — What was bought? (Labour, Material, Contract, Overhead)
2. **Asset/Equipment Axis** — On which equipment was it spent? (Pump-101, Boiler-3)
3. **Activity Axis** — Why was it spent? (Inspection, PM, CM, PdM, Shutdown)

> [!NOTE]
> The **3-Dimensional Maintenance Cost Cube** (Element × Asset × Activity) is the foundation of any Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS) reporting.

### 2.2 Lifecycle Cost (LCC) — The True Cost of an Asset

The **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** captures the *cradle-to-grave* cost. It is the most rigorous KTU-evaluated cost metric.

$$\text{LCC} = C_{acq} + C_{inst} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{down} + C_{env} + C_{dispose}$$

Where:
- $C_{acq}$ = Acquisition cost
- $C_{inst}$ = Installation & commissioning
- $C_{op}$ = Operating cost (energy, consumables)
- $C_{maint}$ = Lifetime maintenance cost (PM, CM, spares, labour)
- $C_{down}$ = Downtime / lost production cost
- $C_{env}$ = Environmental, safety, regulatory penalties
- $C_{dispose}$ = Decommissioning / disposal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In mature plants, **$C_{maint} + C_{down}$ alone can exceed $C_{acq}$ by a factor of 3 to 10** over a 20-year life. This is why LCC is the heart of make-or-buy / replace-or-repair decisions.

### 2.3 Key Maintenance Cost Ratios

### 2.4 The Cost of Downtime Formula

$$C_{down} = (P_{loss} \times t_{down} \times V_u) + (L_{idle} \times t_{down})$$

Where:
- $P_{loss}$ = Lost production rate (units/hr)
- $t_{down}$ = Downtime duration (hours)
- $V_u$ = Value per unit produced (₹/unit)
- $L_{idle}$ = Idle labour cost per hour (₹/hr)

### 2.5 Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) — For Capital Decisions

$$EAC = \frac{LCC \times i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$$

Where $i$ = discount rate, $n$ = life in years. This converts the entire LCC into a fair **yearly** figure for comparison of mutually exclusive options.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Typical Range | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maintenance Cost Ratio (MCR) | $\dfrac{C_m}{C_p}$ | 5%–18% | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Maintenance Cost per Unit | $\dfrac{C_m}{Q}$ | Plant-specific | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Maintenance Cost per Equipment-Hour | $\dfrac{C_m}{H \times N}$ | ₹/hr | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Downtime Cost | $P_{loss} \cdot t_{down} \cdot V_u$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Life Cycle Cost (LCC) | $\sum C_i$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Equivalent Annual Cost | $\dfrac{LCC \cdot i}{1-(1+i)^{-n}}$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Breakdown Ratio | $\dfrac{C_{CM}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | < 30% (world class) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | PM Compliance | $\dfrac{N_{PM, done}}{N_{PM, planned}} \times 100$ | > 90% | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | MTBF | $\dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}$ | hrs | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | MTTR | $\dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}$ | hrs | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | Availability | $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | > 95% (world class) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat on Table 2.6:** For absolute value bars inside any formula, KTU examiners accept the **typeset form** $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, not the keyboard pipe `|`, to avoid markdown ambiguity.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Pharma / Food plants:** MCR is a regulatory disclosure parameter.
- **Power plants:** LCC decides boiler vs. turbine refurbishment timing.
- **Aircraft MRO:** MTTR × fleet size = annual airtime availability.
- **Manufacturing (TPM):** OEE is built from cost data.
- **Insurance:** Downtime cost ratios drive business-interruption premiums.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Annual Cost from LCC

> **Starting Premise:** A capital sum $C$ invested today costs more than the same sum spread over $n$ years, because of the time value of money at interest rate $i$ per year.

**Step 1 — Present Value of a Uniform Annual Series.**
Let $A$ be an equivalent yearly payment for $n$ years. Its present value is:

$$PV = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to LCC.**

$$LCC = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$ (the EAC).**

$$A = LCC \cdot \frac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$$

This is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** multiplied by $LCC$. The quantity $A$ is what should be charged to P&L *every year* of the asset's life to recover the LCC.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Downtime Cost (14-Mark Style)

> **Question (KTU pattern):** A CNC machining centre produces components worth ₹800 per unit at a rate of 12 units/hour. The plant operates 2 shifts × 8 hours × 300 days/year. During the year, the machine suffered 6 breakdowns of 5 hours, 3 hours, 8 hours, 4 hours, 6 hours and 2 hours respectively. Idle labour during downtime costs ₹250/hr. Calculate the **annual downtime cost**.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Downtime Hours.**

$$t_{down,total} = 5 + 3 + 8 + 4 + 6 + 2 = 28 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 2 — Lost Production Volume.**

$$P_{loss,total} = 12 \text{ units/hr} \times 28 \text{ hr} = 336 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 3 — Lost Production Revenue.**

$$R_{loss} = 336 \times 800 = ₹2,68,800/\text{year}$$

**Step 4 — Idle Labour Cost.**

$$L_{idle} = 250 \times 28 = ₹7,000/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Total Downtime Cost.**

$$C_{down} = 2,68,800 + 7,000 = ₹2,75,800/\text{year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cost of *just one spare part* during the same period was ₹40,000. Yet the **hidden downtime cost (₹2,75,800) is 6.9× the spare cost** — a classic KTU teaching point: *direct costs fool managers.*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Maintenance Budget Types Comparison

| Budget Type | Basis | Strength | Weakness | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Budget** | Historical + % increment | Simple, stable | Ignores activity changes | Mature, steady plants |
| **Flexible (Variable) Budget** | Activity level (units, hours) | Adapts to volume | Complex accounting | Seasonal demand |
| **Zero-Based Budget (ZBB)** | Each activity justified from zero | Eliminates waste | Time-consuming | Cost-cutting drives |
| **Rolling (Continuous) Budget** | Always 12 months ahead | Always current | High admin effort | Volatile operations |
| **Activity-Based Budget (ABB)** | Cost per maintenance activity | Highly accurate | Needs CMMS data | TPM/World-class plants |
| **Top-Down Budget** | Senior management allocation | Quick, strategic | Ignores ground reality | Strategic CAPEX years |
| **Bottom-Up Budget** | Aggregated from work orders | Accurate, accepted | Slow, optimistic bias | Operational years |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks students to **match** the budget type to the situation. Memorize: **"ZBB = zero waste; Rolling = always 12; ABB = CMMS-fed; Top-Down = CEO decision; Bottom-Up = work order roll-up."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define Maintenance Cost Analysis. List its four main cost heads.**

**Model Answer:**

*Maintenance Cost Analysis (MCA) is the systematic identification, measurement, classification, and evaluation of all expenditures incurred to keep equipment in its required operating condition over its service life.*

Four main cost heads are:
1. **Direct Labour Cost** — wages, overtime, benefits of maintenance crew.
2. **Materials and Spares Cost** — bearings, seals, lubricants, replacement parts.
3. **Contract and Outsourced Services Cost** — OEM AMCs, third-party calibration.
4. **Indirect (Downtime) Cost** — lost production, idle labour, quality penalties, lost opportunity.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Top-Down and Bottom-Up maintenance budgets.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Top-Down Budget | Bottom-Up Budget |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Senior management / finance | Work orders / maintenance floor |
| **Data basis** | Strategic allocation | Granular activity cost |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow |
| **Accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Acceptance by team** | Poor | High |
| **Best for** | Strategic / capex years | Operational / routine years |
| **Risk** | Ignores ground reality | Optimism bias at base level |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

> **Internal Choice Question (Module 4):**
> **Q3 (a) Discuss in detail the various cost heads involved in Maintenance Cost Analysis, with suitable industrial examples. (7 marks)**
> **(b) A chemical reactor has an acquisition cost of ₹25,00,000, installation cost of ₹3,00,000, annual maintenance cost ₹4,50,000 (growing at 6% per year for 5 years), annual downtime cost ₹6,00,000, and disposal cost ₹50,000. Compute the LCC and the EAC at i = 10%. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q3(a) — 7 Marks

Maintenance cost heads are classified along three axes:

**(i) Direct Costs (Visible on invoices):**
- **Labour:** Wages of fitters, electricians, instrument technicians. Example: ₹80,000/month for a 10-member crew.
- **Materials and Spares:** Bearings, gaskets, filters, lubricants, belts. Example: ₹3 lakh/month for a cement plant.
- **Contract Services:** OEM annual maintenance contracts, third-party vibration analysis, NDT services.
- **Tools and Diagnostics:** Vibration analysers, thermal cameras, laser alignment kits (CapEx, depreciated).

**(ii) Indirect Costs (Hidden, not invoiced):**
- **Downtime Losses:** Lost production revenue during breakdowns.
- **Idle Labour:** Operators paid during equipment stoppage.
- **Quality Defects:** Rework, scrap, warranty claims caused by degraded equipment.
- **Lost Opportunity:** Late-delivery penalties, cancelled orders, customer churn.
- **Environmental / Safety:** Spillage cleanup, statutory fines, insurance hikes.

**(iii) Lifecycle Costs (Cradle-to-grave):**
- Acquisition, installation, operation, maintenance, downtime, environment, disposal — combined as LCC.

> **[Valuation tip — 1 mark is reserved for "industrial example per head." Without examples, full marks are not awarded.]**

---

#### Solution to Q3(b) — 7 Marks (Numerical)

**Step 1 — Total Maintenance Cost over 5 years (geometric series with growth g = 6%).**
Annual maintenance in year $k$ is $A_k = 4,50,000 \times (1.06)^{k-1}$ for $k = 1, 2, ..., 5$.

**Step 2 — Sum of geometric series.**

$$S_5 = A_1 \cdot \frac{(1+g)^5 - 1}{g} = 4{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1.06^5 - 1}{0.06}$$

$$1.06^5 = 1.33823 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_5 = 4{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.33823}{0.06} = 4{,}50{,}000 \cdot 5.6371 \approx 25{,}36{,}695$$

**Step 3 — Sum of annual downtime cost (constant ₹6,00,000).**

$$D_5 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Total LCC.**

$$LCC = 25{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}36{,}695 + 30{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 83{,}86{,}695$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Annual Cost using CRF at i = 10%, n = 5.**

$$CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.6105}{1.6105 - 1} = \frac{0.16105}{0.6105} \approx 0.2638$$

$$EAC = 83{,}86{,}695 \times 0.2638 \approx 22{,}12{,}250 \text{ per year}$$

> **[Answer: LCC ≈ ₹83.87 lakh; EAC ≈ ₹22.12 lakh/year]**

---

### 5.3 Alternative 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> **Q4 (a) Explain the different types of maintenance budgets. State two advantages and two limitations of each. (7 marks)**
> **(b) The annual production of a plant is 1,20,000 units. Annual maintenance cost is ₹18,00,000, of which ₹4,00,000 is corrective maintenance. Compute (i) Maintenance Cost Ratio, (ii) Cost per unit, and (iii) Breakdown Cost Percentage. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q4(a) — 7 Marks

The seven major types are:
1. **Fixed Budget** — based on last year + inflation. *Pros:* simple. *Cons:* ignores activity.
2. **Flexible / Variable Budget** — varies with production volume. *Pros:* adapts. *Cons:* needs good cost-volume model.
3. **Zero-Based Budget (ZBB)** — every rupee re-justified yearly. *Pros:* kills waste. *Cons:* laborious.
4. **Rolling (Continuous) Budget** — 12-month window always maintained. *Pros:* always current. *Cons:* high admin load.
5. **Activity-Based Budget (ABB)** — cost per maintenance activity, fed by CMMS. *Pros:* accurate. *Cons:* needs CMMS.
6. **Top-Down Budget** — dictated by top management. *Pros:* strategic alignment. *Cons:* field rejection.
7. **Bottom-Up Budget** — rolled up from work orders. *Pros:* realistic, owned. *Cons:* slow, base-level optimism.

#### Solution to Q4(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Q = 1,20,000 units; $C_{total}$ = ₹18,00,000; $C_{CM}$ = ₹4,00,000.

Assume $C_p$ (production cost) is given as **₹1,00,00,000** (typical KTU standard; if the problem does not state it, students must declare the assumption).

**(i) Maintenance Cost Ratio:**

$$MCR = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 1.8\%$$

**(ii) Cost per Unit:**

$$CPU = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = ₹15/\text{unit}$$

**(iii) Breakdown Cost Percentage:**

$$BCP = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 22.22\%$$

> **[If world-class BCP < 30% is the benchmark, this plant is borderline; recommended action: shift to predictive maintenance.]**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Maintenance Cost Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add idle labour cost** to downtime cost — losing 2 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Confusing CRF and its inverse** — the annuity factor and capital recovery factor are reciprocals.
> 3. **Omitting the disposal cost** in LCC problems (1 mark gone silently).
> 4. **Mixing up MCR with MTTR** — MCR is a *ratio*, MTTR is *hours*.
> 5. **In ABB / ZBB questions, not stating the assumption** that the CMMS or justification system exists — examiners deduct 1 mark for unstated assumption.
> 6. **Failing to use $\vert x \vert$ in tables** — write as `\vert x \vert` to avoid LaTeX rendering issues.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintenance Cost Analysis (MCA)** is the *quantitative* side of maintenance management; it converts shop-floor activities into rupees.
- Always separate **Direct (visible)** costs from **Indirect (hidden)** costs — the latter are typically **5–10×** the former.
- **LCC = Acquisition + Installation + Operation + Maintenance + Downtime + Environmental + Disposal.**
- **EAC = LCC × CRF** is the standardized yearly cost for fair comparison of options.
- **Key ratios to memorize for KTU:** MCR, Cost/Unit, Breakdown %, PM Compliance, MTBF, MTTR, Availability.
- **Seven Budget Types** (must know all): Fixed, Flexible, ZBB, Rolling, ABB, Top-Down, Bottom-Up.
- **ZBB** = re-justify every year from zero — best for cost-cutting drives.
- **Rolling** = always 12 months ahead — best for volatile operations.
- **ABB** = activity-driven, CMMS-fed — best for TPM / world-class plants.
- **Top-Down** = management decides; **Bottom-Up** = work-order rolled up.
- A maintenance budget is a **control document**, not just a forecast — variances must be analyzed monthly.
- For 14-mark numericals, **state assumptions, show geometric-series expansion for growing costs, and use CRF carefully.**
- Internal choice questions in Module 4 typically pair a *theory* (a) with a *numerical* (b) — practice both formats.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "DEF-FAST-TRAB":** **D**irect, **E**quipment, **F**unctional axis; **F**ixed, **A**ctivity-based, **S**pecial (ZBB), **T**op-down, **R**olling, **A**BB, **B**ottom-up — for budget-type classification under exam pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Maintenance Budget (Basics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Maintenance Budget (Basics)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Budget (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Maintenance Budget* is a comprehensive **financial planning document** that estimates and allocates monetary resources required to sustain the operational efficiency, reliability, and availability of physical assets, machinery, and infrastructure over a defined fiscal period. It serves as the economic backbone of the *Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)* and *Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)* frameworks adopted in modern industrial enterprises.

The maintenance budget translates engineering decisions, asset condition data, and strategic reliability goals into quantifiable **monetary allocations** across labor, materials, spares, contracts, and overheads. Under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the preparation of a maintenance budget maps directly to the following Course Outcomes:

| CO Mapping | Course Outcome Description | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **CO4** | Develop maintenance planning and resource budgeting strategies | Applying (Level 3) |
| **CO5** | Evaluate the economic impact of maintenance decisions on plant profitability | Evaluating (Level 5) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Maintenance Budget like a Household Monthly Budget — But for a Factory!**

Imagine you own a car. Every year, you know you must spend money on:
- **Petrol** (consumables for daily running)
- **Engine oil, filters, tyres** (preventive maintenance)
- **Major repairs** when the gearbox fails (breakdown maintenance)
- **Mechanic's salary** (in-house labor)
- **Garage visit fees** (outsourced contracts)

If you don't plan this expenditure in advance, you will be caught off-guard when the car breaks down on a highway. A *Maintenance Budget* for a factory is the same idea — but at **industrial scale** involving **hundreds of machines, thousands of components, and crores of rupees**.

### Industrial Analogy: The Hospital Budget

A hospital is an excellent analogy:
- **Doctors and nurses** = Maintenance technicians and engineers
- **Medicines and equipment** = Spare parts and consumables
- **Operating theatre maintenance** = Critical machine upkeep
- **Emergency room** = Breakdown maintenance (unplanned)
- **Annual health check-ups** = Preventive maintenance (planned)

The hospital manager prepares an *annual maintenance budget* to ensure that **patient care never stops** — just as a plant manager prepares a maintenance budget to ensure **production never stops**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Maintenance Budgeting

> [!NOTE]
> The following are universally accepted industrial metrics used in maintenance budgeting:
> - **Maintenance Cost as % of Replacement Asset Value (RAV):** Typically **2% – 6%** for process industries, **3% – 8%** for manufacturing.
> - **Maintenance Cost per Unit of Output:** Measured in currency per kg, liter, or piece produced.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Measured in **hours**.
> - **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Measured in **hours**.
> - **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**, with world-class benchmark of **≥ 85%**.

## 1.4 Why Maintenance Budgeting Matters in Industry

A well-prepared maintenance budget delivers five strategic advantages:

1. **Financial Discipline** — Prevents cost overruns and unplanned capital drain.
2. **Equipment Reliability** — Ensures adequate spares and labor are available *before* failure.
3. **Production Continuity** — Reduces unplanned downtime, protecting revenue.
4. **Strategic Planning** — Aligns maintenance spending with corporate financial cycles.
5. **Audit & Compliance** — Provides documented evidence of prudent asset management for ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and statutory auditors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Components of a Maintenance Budget

A maintenance budget, irrespective of industry sector, comprises **five primary cost heads** as shown below:

### 2.1.1 Direct Maintenance Costs

- **Labor Costs (L):** Wages, salaries, overtime, training, and benefits of in-house maintenance crew.
- **Material Costs (M):** Cost of spares, replacement parts, lubricants, and consumables.
- **Contract Costs (C):** Payments to external vendors for outsourced services (e.g., AMC contracts).

### 2.1.2 Indirect Maintenance Costs

- **Downtime Losses (D):** Lost production revenue during equipment failure.
- **Overhead Allocation (O):** Utilities, supervision, insurance, and administrative support for the maintenance department.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation:** Total Maintenance Budget (TMB) is the sum of all four cost components.

$$TMB = L + M + C + D$$

Where downtime losses D are often **converted to monetary equivalent** using:

$$D = U \times (R_p - V_c) \times T_d$$

Where:
- $U$ = number of units not produced due to downtime
- $R_p$ = selling price per unit (currency/unit)
- $V_c$ = variable cost per unit (currency/unit)
- $T_d$ = downtime duration factor (hours/event)

## 2.2 Types of Maintenance Budgets

| Budget Type | Description | When Used | KTU Relevance |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Fixed (Static) Budget** | Allocated amount remains constant regardless of actual activity | Small enterprises with predictable loads | Low — historical method |
| **Flexible (Variable) Budget** | Varies with production volume or equipment usage | Industries with seasonal demand | **High — Industry Standard** |
| **Zero-Based Budget (ZBB)** | Every expense must be justified from "zero" each year | Cost-control focused organizations | **Very High — KTU 2024 Emphasis** |
| **Rolling (Continuous) Budget** | Updated monthly/quarterly by adding a new period | Dynamic manufacturing environments | High |
| **Planned vs. Unplanned Budget** | Separates preventive (planned) and breakdown (unplanned) costs | TPM-driven plants | **Very High** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorize These for KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary / Typical Range |
|---|---------|-------------|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $TMB = L + M + C + D$ | Total Maintenance Budget | Currency (₹) | $L, M, C, D \geq 0$ |
| 2 | $D = U \times (R_p - V_c) \times T_d$ | Downtime Loss Cost | Currency (₹) | $R_p > V_c$ for profit |
| 3 | $MC_{RAV} = \dfrac{TMB}{RAV} \times 100$ | Maintenance Cost % of Replacement Asset Value | % | **2% – 6%** (Process); **3% – 8%** (Mfg.) |
| 4 | $MC_{unit} = \dfrac{TMB}{Q_{annual}}$ | Maintenance Cost per Unit Output | ₹/unit | Industry-specific |
| 5 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % (decimal) | $\geq 0.85$ world-class |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}}$ | Availability | ratio | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{run}}$ | Performance | ratio | $0 \leq P \leq 1$ |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | Quality | ratio | $0 \leq Q \leq 1$ |
| 9 | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N_f}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | $MTBF \to \infty$ ideal |
| 10 | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_f}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours | $MTTR \to 0$ ideal |
| 11 | $U_{cost} = \dfrac{TMB - P_{budget}}{P_{budget}} \times 100$ | Budget Utilization Variance | % | $\vert U_{cost} \vert \leq 10\%$ healthy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** When asked to calculate maintenance budget, always show **all five cost components** separately before summation. KTU examiners award **method marks** for showing structured breakdown.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Budget Preparation

The preparation of a maintenance budget follows a **six-stage sequential logic**:

1. **Asset Inventory & Classification** — List all maintainable equipment, classify as critical/non-critical using FMEA or criticality analysis.
2. **Historical Data Analysis** — Review last 3–5 years of maintenance spending to identify trends, seasonality, and major overhauls.
3. **Workload Forecasting** — Estimate upcoming preventive, predictive, and corrective maintenance tasks.
4. **Cost Estimation** — Apply standard rates (labor hours × wage rate; material quantities × unit cost) to forecasted workload.
5. **Budget Compilation & Approval** — Aggregate departmental estimates, review with finance, and obtain management sign-off.
6. **Monitoring & Control** — Track actual vs. budgeted expenditure using variance analysis; revise forecast quarterly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Maintenance budgets are used in *power plants* (to plan turbine overhauls), *shipping* (dry-docking budgets), *aviation* (A-check, C-check, D-check budgets), and *IT infrastructure* (server hardware refresh budgets).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A medium-scale manufacturing plant has the following data for the upcoming fiscal year. Prepare a *Total Maintenance Budget (TMB)* and analyze cost metrics.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Number of maintenance technicians | 25 | persons |
| Average annual wage per technician (including benefits) | 4,80,000 | ₹/year |
| Cost of spares and consumables | 35,00,000 | ₹/year |
| Outsourced AMC contracts (5 vendors) | 18,00,000 | ₹/year |
| Expected unplanned downtime events | 40 | events/year |
| Average downtime per event | 6 | hours/event |
| Plant production rate | 500 | units/hour |
| Selling price per unit | 800 | ₹/unit |
| Variable cost per unit | 450 | ₹/unit |
| Replacement Asset Value (RAV) of plant | 50,00,00,000 | ₹ (50 Crore) |
| Annual production (planned) | 12,00,000 | units |

### Step 1: Calculate Labor Cost (L)

$$L = N_{tech} \times W_{avg}$$

$$L = 25 \times 4{,}80{,}000$$

$$\boxed{L = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (1.2 Crore)}}$$

> *Interpretation:* 25 technicians × ₹4.8 Lakh annual wage = ₹1.2 Crore direct labor burden.

### Step 2: Material Cost (M)

Given directly:

$$M = 35{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{M = 0.35 \text{ Crore}}$$

### Step 3: Contract Cost (C)

$$C = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{C = 0.18 \text{ Crore}}$$

### Step 4: Downtime Loss Cost (D)

First, calculate the lost units:

$$U = N_{events} \times T_{d} \times R_{prod}$$

$$U = 40 \times 6 \times 500$$

$$U = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ units lost per year}$$

Now, apply the lost margin formula:

$$D = U \times (R_p - V_c)$$

$$D = 1{,}20{,}000 \times (800 - 450)$$

$$D = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 350$$

$$\boxed{D = 4{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (4.2 Crore)}}$$

> *Critical Insight:* Downtime losses constitute the **largest cost component** at ₹4.2 Crore — far exceeding direct maintenance costs combined (₹1.73 Crore). This justifies **higher investment in preventive maintenance** to reduce future downtime.

### Step 5: Total Maintenance Budget (TMB)

$$TMB = L + M + C + D$$

$$TMB = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 35{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}20{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TMB = 5{,}93{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (5.93 Crore)}}$$

### Step 6: Key Cost Metrics (Analytical Matrix)

**Metric 1: Maintenance Cost as % of RAV**

$$MC_{RAV} = \frac{TMB}{RAV} \times 100$$

$$MC_{RAV} = \frac{5{,}93{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$MC_{RAV} = 0.1186 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC_{RAV} = 11.86\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** 11.86% is **significantly above** the healthy range of 2%–6%. KTU examiners expect a recommendation to reduce this through better preventive maintenance. Marks awarded for: (a) correct formula, (b) correct substitution, (c) interpretation, (d) recommendation.

**Metric 2: Maintenance Cost per Unit**

$$MC_{unit} = \frac{TMB}{Q_{annual}}$$

$$MC_{unit} = \frac{5{,}93{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{MC_{unit} = 49.42 \text{ ₹/unit}}$$

**Metric 3: OEE Estimate (Assuming current performance)**

Given: $A = 0.90$, $P = 0.95$, $Q = 0.98$

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

$$OEE = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.98$$

$$\boxed{OEE = 0.8379 \approx 83.79\%}$$

> *Interpretation:* OEE of 83.79% is **below world-class** (≥85%) but **above typical** (60%). There is a **1.21 percentage-point gap** representing ₹72 lakh hidden loss potential.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Zero-Based vs. Incremental Budgeting

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a frequently tested KTU 14-mark question:** Compare and justify the choice between Zero-Based and Incremental budgeting approaches for maintenance.

| Decision Criterion | Zero-Based Budget (ZBB) | Incremental Budget | KTU Exam Weightage |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Starting Point** | ₹0 (every rupee justified) | Previous year + % increment | Core concept |
| **Time Required** | High (4–8 weeks) | Low (1–2 weeks) | Practical application |
| **Cost Reduction Potential** | **High** (5%–15% savings typical) | Low (1%–3%) | Critical analysis |
| **Best Suited For** | Cost-control initiatives, mature plants | Stable operations, small firms | Scenario analysis |
| **Risk of Inefficiency** | Low (forces scrutiny) | High (legacy waste persists) | Evaluation level |
| **Employee Resistance** | High (requires justification) | Low (status-quo) | Behavioral aspect |

## 3.3 Budget Variance analysis formula derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favorite:** A budget is approved at ₹5 Crore. Actual spend is ₹5.45 Crore. Calculate the variance and classify it as favorable or unfavorable.

**Variance Formula:**

$$V_{budget} = A_{actual} - B_{planned}$$

$$V_{budget} = 5{,}45{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{V_{budget} = +45{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (Unfavorable)}}$$

> *Classification Logic:* Positive variance on a **cost budget** is **unfavorable** (overspending). Positive variance on a **revenue budget** is **favorable** (over-earning). KTU examiners test this sign convention rigorously.

**Percentage Variance:**

$$V_{\%} = \frac{V_{budget}}{B_{planned}} \times 100$$

$$V_{\%} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{V_{\%} = 9\%}$$

> *Industry Benchmark:* A variance of ±10% is considered acceptable; beyond this triggers management review and root-cause analysis.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: Maintenance Budget Preparation Process

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end logic** of preparing a maintenance budget in a TPM-driven plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Annual Planning Cycle] --> B[Asset Inventory and Criticality Classification]
    B --> C[Historical Maintenance Data Review 3 to 5 years]
    C --> D[Identify Planned PM and PDM Tasks]
    D --> E[Forecast Corrective Workload Using MTBF]
    E --> F[Estimate Labor Hours and Material Quantities]
    F --> G[Apply Wage Rates and Unit Costs]
    G --> H[Compute Downtime Loss in Monetary Terms]
    H --> I[Add Contract and Overhead Costs]
    I --> J{Total Budget Within Management Cap?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Submit to Finance for Approval]
    J -->|No| L[Optimize by Reducing Noncritical Spend]
    L --> J
    K --> M[Approved Budget Released]
    M --> N[Monthly Variance Tracking]
    N --> O{Variance within 10 percent}
    O -->|Yes| P[Continue Plan]
    O -->|No| Q[Root Cause Analysis and Reforecast]
    Q --> N
    P --> R[End of Fiscal Year Review]
    R --> S[Feed Data into Next Year Budget]
    S --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Flow:** The diagram shows a **closed-loop feedback system**. The end of one fiscal year's analysis feeds directly into the next year's asset inventory and historical review, enabling continuous improvement — a core principle of TPM and ISO 55000 Asset Management.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC[Direct Maintenance Costs]
        L1[Labor Cost L] --> TMB1[Total Maintenance Budget TMB]

        C1[Contract Cost C] --> TMB1
    end
    subgraph IC[Indirect Maintenance Costs]
        D1[Downtime Loss D] --> TMB1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

> [!NOTE]

KTU 2024 expects students to understand **both the micro (department-level) and macro (corporate-level)** perspectives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
